Square Root Operation] E --> F[Compute Sub-Diagonal Elements l_ij
Division by l_jj] F --> G{j Less Than n?} G -- Yes --> H[Increment j to j+1] H --> E G -- No --> I[Output: Lower Triangular Matrix L] I --> J[Verification Step
Compute L times L transpose] J --> K{Equals A?} K -- Yes --> L[Solution Valid] K -- No --> M[Recheck Arithmetic] M --> C ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Block Architecture: Solving Ax = b via Cholesky This diagram shows how a linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ is solved using the two-step Cholesky pipeline. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1: Cholesky Factorization
A = L L transpose] --> P2[Step 2: Forward Substitution
Solve L y = b] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Backward Substitution
Solve L transpose x = y] P3 --> P4[Output: Solution Vector x] P4 --> P5{Residual Check
Norm of A x minus b} P5 -- Small --> P6[Accept Solution] P5 -- Large --> P7[Numerical Instability
Re-evaluate] ``` ## 4.3 Algorithmic State Diagram: Entry Computation Order This flowchart depicts the **strict order** in which entries of $\mathbf{L}$ must be computed to satisfy the KTU board exam requirements. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Given SPD Matrix A]) --> Step1[Compute l_11 = sqrt of a_11] Step1 --> Step2[Compute l_21, l_31, ..., l_n1
First Column Below Diagonal] Step2 --> Step3[Compute l_22 = sqrt of a_22 minus l_21 squared] Step3 --> Step4[Compute l_32, l_42, ..., l_n2
Second Column Below Diagonal] Step4 --> Step5[Compute l_33 = sqrt of a_33 minus l_31 squared minus l_32 squared] Step5 --> Step6[Continue Column by Column
Until j = n] Step6 --> Done([Done: Matrix L Fully Assembled]) ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: LU vs. Cholesky Decomposition The following diagram contrasts the general LU decomposition with the specialized Cholesky decomposition to highlight the structural and computational advantages. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LU["General LU Decomposition"] LU1[Any Square Matrix A] LU2[A = L U
Requires Row Pivoting] LU3[Cost: n cubed over 3 FLOPs] LU1 --> LU2 --> LU3 end subgraph CH["Cholesky Decomposition"] CH1[Symmetric Positive-Definite A only] CH2[A = L L transpose
No Pivoting Needed] CH3[Cost: n cubed over 6 FLOPs
Roughly Half of LU] CH1 --> CH2 --> CH3 end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Dec 2024 Style]** ### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **Q:** State the Cholesky decomposition theorem and list the conditions that a real matrix $\mathbf{A}$ must satisfy for the decomposition $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$ to exist. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Cholesky Decomposition Theorem:** Every real symmetric positive-definite matrix $\mathbf{A}$ of size $n \times n$ can be uniquely decomposed as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$, where $\mathbf{L}$ is a lower triangular matrix with strictly positive diagonal entries. **Conditions for existence (any two are sufficient; the matrix must satisfy all three for equivalence):** 1. **Symmetry:** $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}$. 2. **Positive-definiteness:** $\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} > 0$ for all non-zero $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. 3. **All leading principal minors strictly positive:** $\det(\mathbf{A}_k) > 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q:** Write the formula for computing the diagonal and off-diagonal entries of the Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ for a symmetric positive-definite matrix $\mathbf{A} = [a_{ij}]$. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Diagonal entries** ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$): > $$\ell_{ii} = \sqrt{\, a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik}^{2} \,}$$ > > **Off-diagonal entries** ($i > j$): > $$\ell_{ij} = \frac{1}{\ell_{jj}} \left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik} \ell_{jk} \right)$$ The order of computation must be **column-wise from top to bottom** within each column. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU End Semester Examination — Module Internal Choice Format]** ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **Q:** Find the Cholesky decomposition $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$ of the matrix: $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 3 & -6 \\ 3 & 5 & 1 \\ -6 & 1 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$$ Hence, solve the linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 14 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$. **(14 Marks)** #### Part (a): Cholesky Factorization (7 Marks) **Step 1: Verify symmetry and positive-definiteness.** Symmetry: $\mathbf{A}^{\top} = \mathbf{A}$ ✓ Leading principal minors: - $\det(\mathbf{A}_1) = 9 > 0$ ✓ - $\det(\mathbf{A}_2) = (9)(5) - (3)(3) = 45 - 9 = 36 > 0$ ✓ - $\det(\mathbf{A}_3) = 9(5 \cdot 14 - 1 \cdot 1) - 3(3 \cdot 14 - 1 \cdot (-6)) + (-6)(3 \cdot 1 - 5 \cdot (-6))$ $= 9(70 - 1) - 3(42 + 6) - 6(3 + 30)$ $= 9(69) - 3(48) - 6(33)$ $= 621 - 144 - 198 = 279 > 0$ ✓ **Step 2: Compute $\ell_{11}$.** $$\ell_{11} = \sqrt{a_{11}} = \sqrt{9} = 3$$ **Step 3: Compute $\ell_{21}$ and $\ell_{31}$.** $$\ell_{21} = \frac{a_{21}}{\ell_{11}} = \frac{3}{3} = 1$$ $$\ell_{31} = \frac{a_{31}}{\ell_{11}} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$ **Step 4: Compute $\ell_{22}$.** $$\ell_{22} = \sqrt{a_{22} - \ell_{21}^{2}} = \sqrt{5 - 1} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$ **Step 5: Compute $\ell_{32}$.** $$\ell_{32} = \frac{a_{32} - \ell_{31}\ell_{21}}{\ell_{22}} = \frac{1 - (-2)(1)}{2} = \frac{1 + 2}{2} = \frac{3}{2}$$ **Step 6: Compute $\ell_{33}$.** $$\ell_{33} = \sqrt{a_{33} - \ell_{31}^{2} - \ell_{32}^{2}} = \sqrt{14 - (-2)^{2} - \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}$$ $$= \sqrt{14 - 4 - \frac{9}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{56 - 16 - 9}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{31}{4}} = \frac{\sqrt{31}}{2}$$ **Final Cholesky Factor:** $$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ -2 & 3/2 & \sqrt{31}/2 \end{bmatrix}$$ #### Part (b): Solve the Linear System (7 Marks) Substitute $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{L}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ and solve $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$ first. **Step 1: Forward substitution $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$.** Row 1: $3y_1 = 6 \Rightarrow y_1 = 2$. Row 2: $1 \cdot y_1 + 2y_2 = 14 \Rightarrow 2 + 2y_2 = 14 \Rightarrow y_2 = 6$. Row 3: $-2y_1 + \dfrac{3}{2}y_2 + \dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{2}y_3 = 5$ $-2(2) + \dfrac{3}{2}(6) + \dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{2}y_3 = 5$ $-4 + 9 + \dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{2}y_3 = 5$ $5 + \dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{2}y_3 = 5$ $\dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{2}y_3 = 0 \Rightarrow y_3 = 0$ **Step 2: Backward substitution $\mathbf{L}^{\top}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$.** Row 3: $\dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{2} x_3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_3 = 0$. Row 2: $2x_2 + \dfrac{3}{2}x_3 = 6 \Rightarrow 2x_2 = 6 \Rightarrow x_2 = 3$. Row 1: $3x_1 + 1 \cdot x_2 + (-2)x_3 = 2 \Rightarrow 3x_1 + 3 = 2 \Rightarrow x_1 = -\dfrac{1}{3}$. **Final Solution:** $$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/3 \\ 3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Verification:** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 9(-1/3) + 3(3) + (-6)(0) \\ 3(-1/3) + 5(3) + 1(0) \\ -6(-1/3) + 1(3) + 14(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 + 9 \\ -1 + 15 \\ 2 + 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 14 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$ ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q:** Given the symmetric positive-definite matrix: $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & 15 & -5 \\ 15 & 13 & 3 \\ -5 & 3 & 17 \end{bmatrix}$$ **(a)** Compute the Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ such that $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain briefly how Cholesky decomposition is used in **Machine Learning** for sampling from a multivariate Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$. **(7 Marks)** #### Part (a): Cholesky Factorization (7 Marks) **Step 1: Compute $\ell_{11}$.** $$\ell_{11} = \sqrt{a_{11}} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$ **Step 2: Compute $\ell_{21}$ and $\ell_{31}$.** $$\ell_{21} = \frac{a_{21}}{\ell_{11}} = \frac{15}{5} = 3$$ $$\ell_{31} = \frac{a_{31}}{\ell_{11}} = \frac{-5}{5} = -1$$ **Step 3: Compute $\ell_{22}$.** $$\ell_{22} = \sqrt{a_{22} - \ell_{21}^{2}} = \sqrt{13 - 9} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$ **Step 4: Compute $\ell_{32}$.** $$\ell_{32} = \frac{a_{32} - \ell_{31}\ell_{21}}{\ell_{22}} = \frac{3 - (-1)(3)}{2} = \frac{3 + 3}{2} = 3$$ **Step 5: Compute $\ell_{33}$.** $$\ell_{33} = \sqrt{a_{33} - \ell_{31}^{2} - \ell_{32}^{2}} = \sqrt{17 - 1 - 9} = \sqrt{7}$$ **Final Cholesky Factor:** $$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 2 & 0 \\ -1 & 3 & \sqrt{7} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Verification (sample entry):** $(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top})_{23} = (3)(-1) + (2)(3) = -3 + 6 = 3 = a_{23}$ ✓ #### Part (b): ML Application — Sampling from Multivariate Gaussian (7 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Full Model Answer (7 Marks):** > > In Machine Learning, we often need to draw random samples from a multivariate normal distribution $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$, where $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is a covariance matrix. A covariance matrix is always **symmetric and positive-semi-definite**, and for non-degenerate distributions it is **strictly positive-definite**. Hence, Cholesky decomposition is the perfect tool. > > **Procedure (5 sub-steps, each worth ~1.4 marks):** > > 1. **Compute the Cholesky factor:** Factorize $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$ once (this is a one-time $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ cost). > > 2. **Sample from the standard normal:** Generate $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ with each $z_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ independently. > > 3. **Apply the linear transformation:** Compute $\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{z}$. This is an $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ matrix-vector multiply. > > 4. **Add the mean:** Compute $\mathbf{x} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{z}$. > > 5. **Verify the covariance:** $\mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top}] = \mathbf{L} \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{z}\mathbf{z}^{\top}] \mathbf{L}^{\top} = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{I} \mathbf{L}^{\top} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ ✓ > > **Why this works (intuition):** The matrix $\mathbf{L}$ acts as a "covariance injector" — multiplying an isotropic Gaussian $\mathbf{z}$ (which has identity covariance) by $\mathbf{L}$ stretches and rotates it so that the resulting distribution has the desired covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. > > **Why Cholesky is preferred over direct inversion of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$:** Inverting an $n \times n$ matrix costs $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ and is numerically unstable for ill-conditioned matrices. Cholesky decomposition is also $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ but is **roughly twice as fast** and is **much more numerically stable** for matrices that are well-conditioned. For each new sample, only an $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ matrix-vector product is needed, making it the gold standard in **Gaussian Process Regression**, **Variational Inference**, and **Reinforcement Learning** (e.g., TRPO algorithms). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the symmetry check (–1 to –2 marks):** The examiner explicitly tests whether you know that the input **must** be symmetric. If you attempt Cholesky on a non-symmetric matrix, you will lose marks for misapplying the theorem. > > 2. **Incorrect computation order (–2 marks):** Computing $\ell_{32}$ before $\ell_{31}$, $\ell_{21}$, and $\ell_{22}$ is a **very common mistake**. The correct order is column-by-column, top-to-bottom within each column. > > 3. **Sign errors in the off-diagonal formula (–1 to –2 marks):** The minus sign inside the parentheses $a_{ij} - \sum \ell_{ik}\ell_{jk}$ is frequently dropped. Always double-check the formula. > > 4. **Ignoring the negative argument under the square root (–2 to –3 marks):** If the term under the radical becomes negative during computation, the matrix is **not positive-definite** and Cholesky **fails**. Stop and state this explicitly rather than producing a complex number. > > 5. **Skipping the verification step (–1 mark):** The KTU board values **verification**. Always end with "Verify: $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top} = \mathbf{A}$" and show at least 2–3 sample entries. > > 6. **Confusing $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$ with $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}^{\top}\mathbf{L}$ (–1 mark):** While both are mathematically valid (one uses lower triangular, the other upper triangular), the KTU convention strictly uses $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$. Be consistent. > > 7. **Not mentioning uniqueness in the answer (–1 mark):** Always state that "the Cholesky decomposition is **unique**" when $\mathbf{L}$ is constrained to have positive diagonal entries. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cholesky Decomposition (KTU 2024 Scheme)** ### Conceptual Recap - **Cholesky Decomposition** factorizes an SPD matrix as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\top}$. - $\mathbf{A}$ must be **symmetric** ($\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}$) and **positive-definite** (all eigenvalues $> 0$). - All **leading principal minors** of $\mathbf{A}$ must be strictly positive. - The Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ is **unique** when constrained to have positive diagonal entries. ### Formula Quick-Reference - **Diagonal:** $\ell_{ii} = \sqrt{a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik}^{2}}$ - **Off-diagonal:** $\ell_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{\ell_{jj}} \left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik} \ell_{jk} \right)$ for $i > j$ - **Verification:** $a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{\min(i,j)} \ell_{ik} \ell_{jk}$ ### Order of Computation (Strict) 1. $\ell_{11}$ (diagonal of column 1) 2. $\ell_{21}, \ell_{31}, \ldots, \ell_{n1}$ (below diagonal of column 1) 3. $\ell_{22}$ (diagonal of column 2) 4. $\ell_{32}, \ell_{42}, \ldots, \ell_{n2}$ (below diagonal of column 2) 5. ... continue column by column ### Computational Advantages - **Cost:** $\approx \frac{n^3}{6}$ FLOPs — roughly **half** of general LU. - **Storage:** $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ entries — **half** of full matrix storage. - **No pivoting required** (unlike LU) for symmetric positive-definite inputs. - **Numerically stable** — exploits the inherent conditioning of SPD matrices. ### ML / Engineering Applications - **Linear regression** normal equations: $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$. - **Multivariate Gaussian sampling** in Gaussian Processes, VAEs, and Bayesian inference. - **Finite Element Analysis:** solving $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{f}$ for stiffness matrices. - **Kalman Filters:** computing innovation covariance in state estimation. - **Second-order optimization:** forming the trust-region subproblem in TRPO and L-BFGS variants. ### Common Exam Pitfalls - ❌ Forgetting symmetry/positive-definiteness checks. - ❌ Computing entries in the wrong order. - ❌ Sign errors in the off-diagonal formula. - ❌ Skipping the verification step. - ❌ Failing to mention uniqueness. ### Key Insight > Cholesky decomposition is essentially **"LU decomposition with the symmetry constraint built-in"** — by recognizing that $\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{L}^{\top}$, you halve the computational cost while inheriting the numerical stability that comes from the positive-definite structure. It is one of the most elegant and widely-used algorithms in numerical linear algebra.

topics.title: Eigen Decomposition and Diagonalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eigen Decomposition and Diagonalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Eigendecomposition** (also called **Spectral Decomposition**) is a matrix factorization technique in linear algebra where a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is decomposed into a canonical form using its **eigenvalues** and **eigenvectors**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Eigenvalue-Eigenvector Pair)**
> A non-zero vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is called an **eigenvector** of a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ if there exists a scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{C}$) such that:
> $$A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$
> The scalar $\lambda$ is called the **eigenvalue** corresponding to eigenvector $\mathbf{v}$.

Equivalently, the action of $A$ on a non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ produces a result that is a **scalar multiple** of the original vector — the vector is merely *scaled*, never rotated or sheared out of its own line of action.

### 1.2 Diagonalization — The Master Concept

A square matrix $A$ is said to be **diagonalizable** if it can be factorized as:

$$A = P D P^{-1}$$

where $P$ is a **non-singular (invertible)** matrix whose columns are the linearly independent eigenvectors of $A$, and $D$ is a **diagonal matrix** whose diagonal entries are the corresponding eigenvalues of $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Machine Learning**
> Diagonal matrices are computationally trivial to work with — raising them to powers, inverting them, or computing their determinants is essentially $O(n)$. Many ML algorithms (PCA, Spectral Clustering, PageRank, Google's search algorithm) reduce complex matrices to diagonal form to extract structure from high-dimensional data.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Sheet Stretching" Intuition

Imagine a perfectly flat, square rubber sheet pinned at its center, lying on a table. Now stretch it in **specific directions** by grabbing its edges and pulling.

- The **rubber sheet** represents the matrix $A$ acting on a vector space.
- The **directions in which the sheet stretches without twisting** are the **eigenvectors** — these are the "natural axes" of the transformation.
- The **amount of stretching along each of these natural axes** is the corresponding **eigenvalue** $\lambda$.

If $\lambda > 1$, the rubber stretches outward in that direction.
If $0 < \lambda < 1$, it shrinks (compression).
If $\lambda < 0$, it flips — a reflection through the origin combined with scaling.
If $\lambda = 0$, the sheet collapses to a line or point (matrix becomes singular).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** Eigenvectors tell you *which directions* survive a transformation unchanged in orientation; eigenvalues tell you *how much* they get stretched or squished along those directions.

### 1.4 Physical & Mathematical Constants in Context

- The **trace** of $A$ equals the sum of its eigenvalues: $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$
- The **determinant** of $A$ equals the product of its eigenvalues: $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$
- The **rank** of $A$ equals the number of *non-zero* eigenvalues (counting multiplicities).
- For any diagonalizable matrix, $A^k = P D^k P^{-1}$, which means $A^k \mathbf{v} = \lambda^k \mathbf{v}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deriving the Eigenvalue Equation — The "Why"

Starting from the defining equation:

$$A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

We rearrange by bringing everything to one side:

$$A \mathbf{v} - \lambda \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

$$(A - \lambda I) \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

For a **non-trivial solution** ($\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$) to exist, the matrix $(A - \lambda I)$ must be **singular** (non-invertible). This requires its determinant to vanish:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This polynomial equation in $\lambda$ is called the **Characteristic Equation** of $A$.

### 2.2 The Characteristic Polynomial

For an $n \times n$ matrix $A$, the characteristic polynomial is:

$$p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I) = (-1)^n \left[ \lambda^n - (\text{tr} A) \lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + (-1)^n \det(A) \right]$$

**Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic to Find Eigen-Everything:**

1. Form the matrix $A - \lambda I$ by subtracting $\lambda$ from each diagonal entry of $A$.
2. Compute the determinant of this $(n \times n)$ matrix to get a polynomial in $\lambda$.
3. Set the polynomial equal to **zero** and solve — the roots are the **eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$.
4. For each $\lambda_i$, solve the homogeneous system $(A - \lambda_i I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$.
5. The non-zero solutions form the **eigenspace** $E_{\lambda_i} = \ker(A - \lambda_i I)$.
6. Choose a basis for each eigenspace — these basis vectors are the **eigenvectors**.

### 2.3 Conditions for Diagonalizability

A matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is diagonalizable if and only if **at least one** of the following equivalent conditions holds:

- $A$ has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.
- The geometric multiplicity equals the algebraic multiplicity for every eigenvalue.
- The minimal polynomial of $A$ has no repeated roots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case — Symmetric Matrices (Spectral Theorem)**
> Every real symmetric matrix ($A = A^T$) is always diagonalizable, and importantly, it can be diagonalized using an **orthogonal** matrix $Q$:
> $$A = Q \Lambda Q^T \quad \text{where} \quad Q^T Q = I$$
> The columns of $Q$ are orthonormal eigenvectors. This is the foundation of **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** in Machine Learning.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all critical formulas** required for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Eigendecomposition. The symbol $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ is rendered as `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** | **Remarks / Conditions** |
|---|---|---|
| Eigenvalue Equation | $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$, $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Foundation definition |
| Characteristic Equation | $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ | $n$-th degree polynomial in $\lambda$ |
| Characteristic Polynomial | $p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A)$ | Monic polynomial of degree $n$ |
| Sum of Eigenvalues | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(A)$ | Always true, no conditions |
| Product of Eigenvalues | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \det(A)$ | Always true, no conditions |
| Eigendecomposition | $A = P D P^{-1}$ | Requires $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors |
| Spectral Decomposition (Symmetric) | $A = Q \Lambda Q^{T}$ | $A = A^{T}$, $Q$ orthogonal |
| Power of Diagonalizable Matrix | $A^{k} = P D^{k} P^{-1}$ | Valid for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ if $D^{-1}$ exists |
| Matrix Exponential | $e^{A} = P \, e^{D} \, P^{-1}$ | $e^{D} = \text{diag}(e^{\lambda_1}, \ldots, e^{\lambda_n})$ |
| Inverse (if $\lambda_i \neq 0$) | $A^{-1} = P D^{-1} P^{-1}$ | $D^{-1} = \text{diag}(1/\lambda_i)$ |
| Geometric Multiplicity | $\dim \ker(A - \lambda_i I)$ | Number of LI eigenvectors for $\lambda_i$ |
| Algebraic Multiplicity | Multiplicity of $\lambda_i$ as root of $p(\lambda)$ | From characteristic polynomial |
| Similarity Invariance | Eigenvalues invariant under similarity $B = P^{-1}AP$ | $A$ and $B$ have same eigenvalues |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Role of Eigendecomposition** |
|---|---|
| **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** | Find directions of maximum variance — eigenvectors of covariance matrix |
| **Google PageRank Algorithm** | Dominant eigenvector of the web-link stochastic matrix |
| **Vibration Analysis (Mechanical Eng.)** | Natural frequencies = eigenvalues of stiffness-mass system matrix |
| **Quantum Mechanics (Physics)** | Observable operators diagonalized; eigenvalues = measurable quantities |
| **Spectral Clustering (ML)** | Use eigenvectors of graph Laplacian for low-dimensional embedding |
| **Google's TensorFlow / NumPy** | `np.linalg.eig(A)` and `np.linalg.eigh(A)` for symmetric matrices |
| **Differential Equations** | System $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ decouples into scalar ODEs using $P$ |
| **Facial Recognition (Eigenfaces)** | Turk & Pentland (1991) — PCA on face image covariance matrix |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Characteristic Polynomial of a $2 \times 2$ Matrix

Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$. We seek $\lambda$ such that $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$.

**Step 1:** Form the matrix $A - \lambda I$:

$$
A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant by expansion along the first row:

$$
\det(A - \lambda I) = (a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - bc
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the product:

$$
\det(A - \lambda I) = ad - a\lambda - d\lambda + \lambda^2 - bc
$$

**Step 4:** Regroup into a standard polynomial in $\lambda$:

$$
\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + (ad - bc)
$$

**Step 5:** Identify the coefficients using the trace and determinant:

- Coefficient of $\lambda$: $-(a+d) = -\text{tr}(A)$
- Constant term: $ad - bc = \det(A)$

**Step 6:** The final characteristic equation is:

$$
\lambda^2 - (\text{tr} A)\lambda + \det(A) = 0
$$

Setting this equal to zero gives the **quadratic formula for eigenvalues**:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\text{tr}(A) \pm \sqrt{[\text{tr}(A)]^2 - 4\det(A)}}{2}
$$

The expression $\Delta = [\text{tr}(A)]^2 - 4\det(A)$ is the **discriminant**. If $\Delta < 0$, the eigenvalues are complex conjugates (rotation, not stretching).

### 3.2 Derivation II: Full Eigendecomposition of a $3 \times 3$ Matrix (Worked Example)

Consider the matrix:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & 4 & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Notice that $A$ is **symmetric** ($A = A^T$). Therefore, by the Spectral Theorem, $A$ is orthogonally diagonalizable. We can apply the formula $A = Q\Lambda Q^T$.

**Step 2:** Compute the trace and determinant for sanity check:
- $\text{tr}(A) = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12$ → Expected sum of eigenvalues = $12$.
- Determinant: expanding along row 1:
$$\det(A) = 4[(4)(4) - (-2)(-2)] - (-2)[(-2)(4) - (-2)(2)] + 2[(-2)(-2) - (4)(2)]$$
$$\det(A) = 4[16 - 4] + 2[-8 + 4] + 2[4 - 8]$$
$$\det(A) = 4(12) + 2(-4) + 2(-4) = 48 - 8 - 8 = 32$$
- Expected product of eigenvalues = $32$.

**Step 3:** Form the characteristic polynomial $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$:

$$
A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & 4 - \lambda & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & 4 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the determinant by cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$
p(\lambda) = (4 - \lambda) \cdot M_{11} - (-2) \cdot M_{12} + 2 \cdot M_{13}
$$

where the minors are:

$$
M_{11} = \det \begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda & -2 \\ -2 & 4 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (4 - \lambda)^2 - 4
$$

$$
M_{12} = \det \begin{pmatrix} -2 & -2 \\ 2 & 4 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = -2(4 - \lambda) - (-2)(2) = -8 + 2\lambda + 4 = 2\lambda - 4
$$

$$
M_{13} = \det \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 4 - \lambda \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} = (-2)(-2) - (4 - \lambda)(2) = 4 - 8 + 2\lambda = 2\lambda - 4
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into $p(\lambda)$:

$$
p(\lambda) = (4 - \lambda)[(4 - \lambda)^2 - 4] + 2(2\lambda - 4) + 2(2\lambda - 4)
$$

**Step 6:** Expand $(4 - \lambda)^2 - 4 = 16 - 8\lambda + \lambda^2 - 4 = \lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 12$. Therefore:

$$
p(\lambda) = (4 - \lambda)(\lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 12) + 4(2\lambda - 4)
$$

**Step 7:** Expand the first term:
$$
(4 - \lambda)(\lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 12) = 4\lambda^2 - 32\lambda + 48 - \lambda^3 + 8\lambda^2 - 12\lambda
$$
$$
= -\lambda^3 + 12\lambda^2 - 44\lambda + 48
$$

**Step 8:** Expand the second term:
$$
4(2\lambda - 4) = 8\lambda - 16
$$

**Step 9:** Combine:
$$
p(\lambda) = -\lambda^3 + 12\lambda^2 - 44\lambda + 48 + 8\lambda - 16
$$
$$
p(\lambda) = -\lambda^3 + 12\lambda^2 - 36\lambda + 32
$$

**Step 10:** Multiply by $-1$ for the monic form:
$$
\lambda^3 - 12\lambda^2 + 36\lambda - 32 = 0
$$

**Verification:** $\text{tr}(A) = 12$ ✓ matches the negative coefficient. $\det(A) = 32$ ✓ matches the constant term (with sign $(-1)^3 \cdot 32 = -32$ for $p(\lambda)$ form). 

**Step 11:** Find rational roots using the Rational Root Theorem. Test $\lambda = 2$:

$$
(2)^3 - 12(2)^2 + 36(2) - 32 = 8 - 48 + 72 - 32 = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

So $\lambda = 2$ is a root. Factor it out via synthetic division:

$$
\lambda^3 - 12\lambda^2 + 36\lambda - 32 = (\lambda - 2)(\lambda^2 - 10\lambda + 16)
$$

**Step 12:** Factor the quadratic:
$$
\lambda^2 - 10\lambda + 16 = (\lambda - 2)(\lambda - 8) \quad \text{(since } 2 + 8 = 10,\ 2 \cdot 8 = 16\text{)}
$$

**Step 13:** Complete factorization:
$$
p(\lambda) = (\lambda - 2)^2 (\lambda - 8) = 0
$$

**Eigenvalues:** $\lambda_1 = 2$ (algebraic multiplicity 2), $\lambda_2 = 8$ (algebraic multiplicity 1).

**Step 14:** Find eigenvectors for $\lambda = 8$:

Solve $(A - 8I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
A - 8I = \begin{pmatrix} -4 & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & -4 & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & -4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Row reduce: $R_1 \to -\frac{1}{2}R_1$ gives $(2, 1, -1)$. So $R_2 \to R_2 + R_1$ and $R_3 \to R_3 - R_1$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & -3 & -3 \\ 0 & -3 & -3 \end{pmatrix} \to \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Back-substitute: $y + z = 0 \Rightarrow y = -z$. Then $2x + y - z = 0 \Rightarrow 2x - z - z = 0 \Rightarrow x = z$. So:

$$
\mathbf{v} = z \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 15:** Find eigenvectors for $\lambda = 2$:

Solve $(A - 2I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
A - 2I = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

All rows are proportional! So the rank is 1, and the null space has dimension $3 - 1 = 2$. The single equation is:

$$
2x - 2y + 2z = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x - y + z = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = y - z
$$

Choosing $y = 1, z = 0$: $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$. Choosing $y = 0, z = 1$: $\mathbf{v}_3 = (-1, 0, 1)^T$.

**Step 16:** Orthogonalize the two eigenvectors for $\lambda = 2$. Check orthogonality: $\mathbf{v}_2 \cdot \mathbf{v}_3 = (1)(-1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) = -1 \neq 0$. They are not orthogonal, so apply Gram-Schmidt.

Let $\mathbf{u}_2 = \mathbf{v}_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$. Then:

$$
\mathbf{u}_3 = \mathbf{v}_3 - \frac{\mathbf{v}_3 \cdot \mathbf{u}_2}{\mathbf{u}_2 \cdot \mathbf{u}_2} \mathbf{u}_2 = (-1, 0, 1)^T - \frac{-1}{2}(1, 1, 0)^T = \left(-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, 1\right)^T
$$

We can scale to $\mathbf{u}_3 = (-1, 1, 2)^T$.

**Verification of orthogonality:** $\mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{u}_2 = 1 - 1 + 0 = 0$ ✓, $\mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{u}_3 = -1 - 1 + 2 = 0$ ✓, $\mathbf{u}_2 \cdot \mathbf{u}_3 = -1 + 1 + 0 = 0$ ✓.

**Step 17:** Normalize all three vectors to obtain the orthogonal matrix $Q$:

$$
\mathbf{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{q}_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 18:** Assemble the spectral decomposition $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{6} \\ -1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{6} \\ 1/\sqrt{3} & 0 & 2/\sqrt{6} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 8 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sqrt{3} & -1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 0 \\ -1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{6} & 2/\sqrt{6} \end{pmatrix}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation III: Power Iteration for Dominant Eigenvalue

In machine learning, computing **all** eigenvalues is expensive ($O(n^3)$). Often, we only need the **largest eigenvalue** (e.g., PageRank uses the dominant eigenvector). The **Power Iteration** method achieves this:

**Algorithm Derivation:**

Start with an arbitrary non-zero vector $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$. Define the iteration:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \frac{A \mathbf{x}^{(k)}}{\| A \mathbf{x}^{(k)} \|}$$

**Convergence Proof Sketch:** Expand $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ in the eigenbasis $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_n\}$ of $A$ (assumed diagonalizable with $|\lambda_1| > |\lambda_2| \geq \cdots \geq |\lambda_n|$):

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 \mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_n \mathbf{v}_n
$$

Apply $A$ repeatedly:

$$
A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)} = c_1 \lambda_1^k \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 \lambda_2^k \mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_n \lambda_n^k \mathbf{v}_n
$$

Factor out $\lambda_1^k$:

$$
A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \lambda_1^k \left[ c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^k \mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_n \left(\frac{\lambda_n}{\lambda_1}\right)^k \mathbf{v}_n \right]
$$

As $k \to \infty$, the ratios $\left(\frac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_1}\right)^k \to 0$ for $i \geq 2$, so $A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)} \to c_1 \lambda_1^k \mathbf{v}_1$. After normalization, $\mathbf{x}^{(k)} \to \mathbf{v}_1$ (the dominant eigenvector). The Rayleigh quotient $\frac{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T A \mathbf{x}^{(k)}}{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)}} \to \lambda_1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Convergence Rate:** Error decays as $\left\lvert \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} \right\rvert^{2k}$, so a large spectral gap $|\lambda_1|/|\lambda_2|$ ensures rapid convergence. This is why well-conditioned matrices converge in a few iterations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Eigendecomposition Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Square Matrix A in R to the n x n] --> B[Form Matrix A minus lambda I]
    B --> C[Compute Characteristic Polynomial det of A minus lambda I]
    C --> D[Set Polynomial Equal to Zero]
    D --> E{Solve Polynomial Equation}
    E -->|Real Distinct Roots| F1[Case 1: n Distinct Eigenvalues]
    E -->|Repeated Roots| F2[Case 2: Repeated Eigenvalues]
    E -->|Complex Roots| F3[Case 3: Complex Eigenvalues]
    
    F1 --> G1[Eigenvalues lambda 1 ... lambda n]
    F2 --> G2[Check Geometric vs Algebraic Multiplicity]
    G2 -->|Equal| G2a[Diagonalizable]
    G2 -->|Less| G2b[Not Diagonalizable]
    F3 --> G3[Diagonalization over Complex Field C]
    
    G1 --> H1[For each lambda solve A minus lambda I times v equals 0]
    G2a --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    
    H1 --> I1[Collect n Linearly Independent Eigenvectors]
    I1 --> J1[Form Matrix P from Columns]
    J1 --> K1[Form Diagonal Matrix D from Eigenvalues]
    K1 --> L1[Output A equals P times D times P inverse]
    
    G2b --> M1[Use Jordan Normal Form instead]
    M1 --> N1[Output A equals P times J times P inverse]
    
    L1 --> O1{Symmetric Matrix Check}
    O1 -->|Yes A equals A transpose| P1[Use Orthogonal Q: A equals Q Lambda Q transpose]
    O1 -->|No| P2[Use General P: A equals P D P inverse]
    
    P1 --> Q1[Apply to ML: PCA, Spectral Clustering, PageRank]
    P2 --> R1[Apply to Engineering: Vibration Analysis, ODEs]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map — Why Eigendecomposition Simplifies Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Complex Matrix A] -->|Eigendecomposition| B[Diagonal Matrix D]
    B -->|Easy Operations| C1[Matrix Power: D to the k]
    B -->|Easy Operations| C2[Matrix Inverse: D inverse]
    B -->|Easy Operations| C3[Matrix Exponential: e to the D]
    B -->|Easy Operations| C4[Determinant: Product of D entries]
    C1 --> D[Transform Back via P to Get A to the k]
    C2 --> E[Transform Back via P to Get A inverse]
    C3 --> F[Transform Back via P to Get e to the A]
    C4 --> G[Direct Read-off]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: When is a Matrix Diagonalizable?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given n x n Matrix A] --> Q1{Is A Symmetric?}
    Q1 -->|Yes A equals A transpose| Path1[Always Diagonalizable over R]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does A have n distinct eigenvalues?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Path2[Diagonalizable over the field]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3[Check geometric = algebraic multiplicity?]
    Q3 -->|Yes for all lambda| Path3[Diagonalizable]
    Q3 -->|No for some lambda| Path4[Not Diagonalizable - use Jordan Form]
    
    Path1 --> Out[Spectral Theorem: A equals Q Lambda Q transpose]
    Path2 --> Out2[Standard: A equals P D P inverse]
    Path3 --> Out2
    Path4 --> Out3[Jordan Normal Form: A equals P J P inverse]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a square matrix. Given that $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, find its eigenvalues.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** A non-zero vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is an eigenvector of $A$ if $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$ for some scalar $\lambda$, called the eigenvalue.
>
> **Characteristic Equation (1 mark):**
> $$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det \begin{pmatrix} 3 - \lambda & 1 \\ 1 & 3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (3 - \lambda)^2 - 1 = 0$$
> $$(3 - \lambda)^2 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 3 - \lambda = \pm 1$$
>
> **Eigenvalues (1 mark):** $\lambda_1 = 4$, $\lambda_2 = 2$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the conditions under which a square matrix $A$ is diagonalizable. Mention any two real-world applications of eigendecomposition in machine learning.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Conditions (2 marks):** A square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is diagonalizable if:
> 1. It possesses $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.
> 2. Equivalently, the geometric multiplicity equals the algebraic multiplicity for every eigenvalue.
>
> *Special case:* Every real symmetric matrix ($A = A^T$) is diagonalizable via an orthogonal matrix $Q$.
>
> **Two ML Applications (1 mark):**
> 1. **Principal Component Analysis (PCA):** Uses eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix to find principal directions of maximum variance in data.
> 2. **Google PageRank:** Computes the dominant eigenvector of the web-link stochastic matrix to rank web pages.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & 6 & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. Hence, find the matrix $P$ and diagonal matrix $D$ such that $A = PDP^{-1}$, and verify the Spectral Theorem.

**(a)** Find all eigenvalues of $A$ and verify the trace and determinant properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find a complete set of orthonormal eigenvectors, construct the orthogonal matrix $Q$, and state the spectral decomposition. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark):** The matrix is symmetric: $A = A^T$. By the Spectral Theorem, it admits orthogonal diagonalization.
>
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Form $A - \lambda I$:
> $$A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 6 - \lambda & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & 6 - \lambda & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & 6 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the determinant by expanding along row 1:
> $$\det(A - \lambda I) = (6 - \lambda)\left[(6 - \lambda)^2 - 4\right] + 2\left[-2(6 - \lambda) + 4\right] + 2\left[4 - 2(6 - \lambda)\right]$$
>
> After algebraic expansion:
> $$p(\lambda) = (6 - \lambda)^3 - 4(6 - \lambda) - 4(6 - \lambda) - 4(6 - \lambda) \cdot (-1) + \text{cross terms}$$
>
> Working carefully:
> - $M_{11} = (6 - \lambda)^2 - 4 = \lambda^2 - 12\lambda + 32$
> - $M_{12} = -2(6 - \lambda) + 4 = 2\lambda - 8$
> - $M_{13} = 4 - 2(6 - \lambda) = 2\lambda - 8$
>
> Therefore:
> $$p(\lambda) = (6 - \lambda)(\lambda^2 - 12\lambda + 32) + 2(2\lambda - 8) + 2(2\lambda - 8)$$
> $$= (6 - \lambda)(\lambda^2 - 12\lambda + 32) + 4(2\lambda - 8)$$
> $$= -\lambda^3 + 18\lambda^2 - 104\lambda + 192 + 8\lambda - 32$$
> $$= -\lambda^3 + 18\lambda^2 - 96\lambda + 160$$
>
> Multiply by $-1$: $\lambda^3 - 18\lambda^2 + 96\lambda - 160 = 0$.
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Test $\lambda = 2$: $8 - 72 + 192 - 160 = -32 \neq 0$. Test $\lambda = 4$: $64 - 288 + 384 - 160 = 0$ ✓. Test $\lambda = 8$: $512 - 1152 + 768 - 160 = -32 \neq 0$. Test $\lambda = 10$: $1000 - 1800 + 960 - 160 = 0$ ✓.
>
> So $(\lambda - 4)(\lambda - 10)$ are factors. Polynomial division:
> $$\lambda^3 - 18\lambda^2 + 96\lambda - 160 = (\lambda - 4)(\lambda - 10)(\lambda - 4) = (\lambda - 4)^2(\lambda - 10)$$
>
> **Eigenvalues (1 mark):** $\lambda_1 = 4$ (algebraic multiplicity 2), $\lambda_2 = 10$ (algebraic multiplicity 1).
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Verification:
> - Sum: $4 + 4 + 10 = 18 = \text{tr}(A)$ ✓
> - Product: $4 \cdot 4 \cdot 10 = 160 = \det(A)$ ✓ (determinant was $160$)

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Step 1 (2 marks):** For $\lambda = 10$, solve $(A - 10I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:
> $$\begin{pmatrix} -4 & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & -4 & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & -4 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$
>
> Row reduce: $R_1 \to R_1/(-2)$: $(2, 1, -1)$. $R_2 \to R_2 + R_1$: $(0, -3, -3)$, simplified: $(0, 1, 1)$. $R_3 \to R_3 - R_1$: $(0, -3, -3)$, simplified: $(0, 1, 1)$.
>
> From $y + z = 0$: $y = -z$. From $2x + y - z = 0$: $2x = 2z \Rightarrow x = z$.
>
> So $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1, -1, 1)^T$. Normalized: $\mathbf{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1, -1, 1)^T$.
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** For $\lambda = 4$, solve $(A - 4I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:
> $$\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -2 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$
>
> All rows identical → single equation: $2x - 2y + 2z = 0 \Rightarrow x - y + z = 0 \Rightarrow x = y - z$.
>
> Two basis vectors: $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$ and $\mathbf{v}_3 = (-1, 0, 1)^T$. Check orthogonality: $\mathbf{v}_2 \cdot \mathbf{v}_3 = -1 + 0 + 0 = -1 \neq 0$.
>
> Apply Gram-Schmidt: $\mathbf{u}_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$. Then:
> $$\mathbf{u}_3 = (-1, 0, 1)^T - \frac{(-1)}{2}(1, 1, 0)^T = \left(-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, 1\right)^T$$
>
> Scale by 2: $\mathbf{u}_3 = (-1, 1, 2)^T$. Verify: $\mathbf{u}_2 \cdot \mathbf{u}_3 = -1 + 1 + 0 = 0$ ✓.
>
> **Step 3 (1 mark):** Normalize: $\mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1, 0)^T$, $\mathbf{q}_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(-1, 1, 2)^T$.
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Verify cross-orthogonality with $\mathbf{q}_1$: $\mathbf{q}_1 \cdot \mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1 - 1 + 0) = 0$ ✓, $\mathbf{q}_1 \cdot \mathbf{q}_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{18}}(-1 - 1 + 2) = 0$ ✓.
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Spectral decomposition $A = Q\Lambda Q^T$:
> $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{6} \\ -1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{6} \\ 1/\sqrt{3} & 0 & 2/\sqrt{6} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sqrt{3} & -1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 0 \\ -1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{6} & 2/\sqrt{6} \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> Here $P = Q$ (orthogonal), $D = \Lambda = \text{diag}(10, 4, 4)$, and $P^{-1} = P^T$.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified — Alternative Choice]** Consider the matrix $B = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$.

**(a)** Is $B$ diagonalizable? Justify your answer by finding the eigenvalues and checking multiplicities. If yes, construct $P$ and $D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute $B^5$ using the diagonalization $B = PDP^{-1}$ and verify one entry. Discuss why this approach is computationally superior to direct multiplication. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark):** $B$ is an upper triangular matrix, so its eigenvalues are the diagonal entries: $\lambda_1 = 1, \lambda_2 = 3, \lambda_3 = 5$.
>
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** All three eigenvalues are distinct, so by the theorem on distinct eigenvalues, $B$ has three linearly independent eigenvectors. **Therefore, $B$ is diagonalizable.**
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Find eigenvectors.
>
> *For $\lambda_1 = 1$:* Solve $(B - I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:
> $$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$
> From row 3: $4z = 0 \Rightarrow z = 0$. From row 1: $2y = 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$. $x$ is free. So $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1, 0, 0)^T$.
>
> *For $\lambda_2 = 3$:* Solve $(B - 3I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:
> $$\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$
> From row 3: $2z = 0 \Rightarrow z = 0$. From row 1: $-2x + 2y = 0 \Rightarrow x = y$. So $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$.
>
> *For $\lambda_3 = 5$:* Solve $(B - 5I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:
> $$\begin{pmatrix} -4 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$
> From row 2: $-2y + z = 0 \Rightarrow z = 2y$. From row 1: $-4x + 2y = 0 \Rightarrow x = y/2$. Let $y = 2$: $x = 1, z = 4$. So $\mathbf{v}_3 = (1, 2, 4)^T$.
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Form $P$ and $D$:
> $$P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 5 (2 marks):** Compute $P^{-1}$ using back-substitution on augmented matrix $[P \mid I]$:
> $$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & \vert & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & \vert & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 & \vert & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$
> $R_3 \to R_3/4$, $R_2 \to R_2 - 2R_3$, $R_1 \to R_1 - R_2'$:
> $$P^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 1/4 \\ 0 & 1 & -1/2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/4 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Verification (1 mark):** $P^{-1}P = I$ ✓ and $PDP^{-1} = B$ (can be checked by the student).
>
> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Using $B = PDP^{-1}$, we have $B^5 = (PDP^{-1})^5 = PD^5P^{-1}$ because $P^{-1}P = I$ in between.
>
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $D^5$ trivially (just raise diagonal entries to the 5th power):
> $$D^5 = \begin{pmatrix} 1^5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3^5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5^5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 243 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3125 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $PD^5$:
> $$PD^5 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 243 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3125 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 243 & 3125 \\ 0 & 243 & 6250 \\ 0 & 0 & 12500 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute $B^5 = (PD^5)P^{-1}$:
> $$B^5 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 243 & 3125 \\ 0 & 243 & 6250 \\ 0 & 0 & 12500 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 1/4 \\ 0 & 1 & -1/2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/4 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> Entry (1,1): $1 \cdot 1 + 243 \cdot 0 + 3125 \cdot 0 = 1$.
> Entry (1,2): $1 \cdot (-1) + 243 \cdot 1 + 3125 \cdot 0 = 242$.
> Entry (1,3): $1 \cdot (1/4) + 243 \cdot (-1/2) + 3125 \cdot (1/4) = 0.25 - 121.5 + 781.25 = 660$.
> Entry (2,1): $0 \cdot 1 + 243 \cdot 0 + 6250 \cdot 0 = 0$.
> Entry (2,2): $0 \cdot (-1) + 243 \cdot 1 + 6250 \cdot 0 = 243$.
> Entry (2,3): $0 \cdot (1/4) + 243 \cdot (-1/2) + 6250 \cdot (1/4) = -121.5 + 1562.5 = 1441$.
> Entry (3,1): 0, Entry (3,2): 0, Entry (3,3): $12500 \cdot (1/4) = 3125$.
>
> So:
> $$B^5 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 242 & 660 \\ 0 & 243 & 1441 \\ 0 & 0 & 3125 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** **Verification of one entry** — for upper triangular matrices, the diagonal entries of $B^k$ are $\lambda_i^k$. So $B^5_{33}$ should be $5^5 = 3125$ ✓.
>
> **Computational Superiority (extra discussion credit):**
> - **Direct multiplication** of $B \times B$ requires $n^3$ multiplications and additions per multiplication, so $B^5$ takes $4n^3 = 324$ operations for $n=3$ (without exploiting structure).
> - **Diagonalization approach** requires: compute $P^{-1}$ (~$n^3$), then $D^5$ (just $n$ multiplications), then $PD^5$ ($n^3$ operations), then $(PD^5)P^{-1}$ ($n^3$ operations). Total: ~$4n^3$.
> - For large $k$, the savings are enormous because we only compute $D^k$ *once* (cheap), whereas direct multiplication requires $k$ full matrix-matrix products. For $k = 1000$, direct needs $999 n^3$ ops; diagonalization still needs just $3n^3$ ops.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Mark-Loss Hotspots):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign convention:** KTU examiners often present the characteristic equation as $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$, but some books use $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$. The two forms differ by a sign $(-1)^n$. Mixing them leads to wrong eigenvalues.
> 2. **Ignoring algebraic vs geometric multiplicity:** Students find a repeated eigenvalue $\lambda$ and assume there are enough eigenvectors. They must verify $\text{geom mult} = \text{alg mult}$ for diagonalizability. If $\text{geom} < \text{alg}$, the matrix is **not** diagonalizable over that field.
> 3. **Skipping the verification of $A = PDP^{-1}$:** Examiners award 1–2 marks for explicitly computing $PDP^{-1}$ and confirming equality. Don't skip this!
> 4. **Confusing left and right eigenvectors:** $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$ is the **right** eigenvector. The left eigenvector satisfies $\mathbf{w}^T A = \lambda \mathbf{w}^T$. They are different in general.
> 5. **Forgetting to normalize eigenvectors for the spectral theorem:** When asked for an **orthogonal** matrix $Q$, the columns must be **unit vectors** (norm 1). Partially normalized columns cost marks.
> 6. **Arithmetic slip in determinant expansion:** For $3 \times 3$ matrices, use the cofactor expansion or Sarrus' rule carefully. A single sign error propagates and loses 3–4 marks.
> 7. **For orthogonal matrices, $Q^{-1} = Q^T$:** Students often waste time computing $Q^{-1}$ via row reduction, which is unnecessary and error-prone.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Eigendecomposition & Diagonalization**
>
> - [x] **Eigenvalue equation:** $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ with $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$. Always the starting point.
> - [x] **Characteristic equation:** $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$, a polynomial of degree $n$.
> - [x] **Quick eigenvalue shortcut:** For triangular matrices, eigenvalues = diagonal entries.
> - [x] **Invariant properties:** $\sum \lambda_i = \text{tr}(A)$ and $\prod \lambda_i = \det(A)$ — use these for **sanity checks** in every problem.
> - [x] **Eigendecomposition form:** $A = PDP^{-1}$ where $P$'s columns are eigenvectors and $D = \text{diag}(\lambda_i)$.
> - [x] **Spectral Theorem:** Symmetric $A$ admits $A = Q\Lambda Q^T$ with $Q$ orthogonal ($Q^T Q = I$).
> - [x] **Diagonalizability test:** Geometric multiplicity $=$ algebraic multiplicity for all eigenvalues.
> - [x] **Distinct eigenvalues** $\Rightarrow$ automatically diagonalizable.
> - [x] **Matrix power via diagonalization:** $A^k = PD^k P^{-1}$, $e^A = P e^D P^{-1}$.
> - [x] **Power Iteration:** $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \frac{A\mathbf{x}^{(k)}}{\| A\mathbf{x}^{(k)} \|}$ converges to the **dominant eigenvector** if the spectral gap is large.
> - [x] **Rayleigh Quotient:** $R(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}}$ estimates the dominant eigenvalue.
> - [x] **ML Connection:** PCA = eigendecomposition of covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n} X^T X$.
> - [x] **Geometric interpretation:** Eigenvectors are invariant directions; eigenvalues are scaling factors.
> - [x] **Definiteness shortcut:** For symmetric $A$, all $\lambda_i > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **positive definite**; all $\lambda_i \geq 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **positive semi-definite**.
> - [x] **Complex eigenvalues** come in **conjugate pairs** for real matrices; they signal rotation, not pure stretching.
> - [x] **Singular matrix** $\Leftrightarrow$ **at least one eigenvalue is zero** $\Leftrightarrow$ $A^{-1}$ does not exist.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singular Value Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singular Value Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) Theorem**
> Let $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ be any real matrix (rectangular or square). Then there exist **orthogonal matrices** $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, and a **rectangular diagonal matrix** $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with non-negative real entries on the diagonal, such that:
> 
> $$A = U \, \Sigma \, V^{T}$$

The columns of $U$ are called the **left singular vectors** of $A$, the columns of $V$ are the **right singular vectors** of $A$, and the diagonal entries $\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \ldots, \sigma_{r}$ of $\Sigma$ (with $r = \text{rank}(A)$) are the **singular values** of $A$, conventionally arranged in non-increasing order: $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r} > 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Eigendecomposition**
> Unlike eigendecomposition (which requires the matrix to be square), **SVD is universally applicable to ANY rectangular matrix** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$. It is therefore one of the most powerful and general matrix factorizations in linear algebra.

## 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

**Analogy: Unrolling a 3D Object into Flat Sheets**

Imagine you have a **crumpled sheet of paper** (your data matrix $A$) lying on a table, full of creases and noise. Performing SVD is like **gently unrolling it flat** so that you can see the underlying pattern:

- $V^{T}$ is the **recipe** that tells you *how* to unroll the sheet (rotation to align it with the principal axes).
- $\Sigma$ is the **strength gauge** that tells you *how much* of each "direction" matters (singular values = importance scores).
- $U$ is the **final flattened image** showing the result in the new coordinate system.

> [!TIP]
> **Picture the Action of $A$:** Any matrix $A$ is a *linear transformer*. It takes a vector $v$, stretches it by a factor $\sigma$, and rotates it to $u$. That is, $A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} u_{i}$. The SVD precisely captures all these "stretch-and-rotate" actions in the form $U \Sigma V^{T}$.

## 1.3 Geometric Interpretation

For a unit sphere in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$, the image under $A$ is a hyperellipsoid in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. The SVD reveals:
- The **directions of the principal semi-axes** of this hyperellipsoid are the left singular vectors (columns of $U$).
- The **lengths of the semi-axes** are exactly the singular values $\sigma_{i}$.
- The **input directions** that get mapped to these axes are the right singular vectors (columns of $V$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** Singular values are **always non-negative real numbers**, $\sigma_{i} \geq 0$, regardless of the sign or reality of the entries of $A$. This is in stark contrast to eigenvalues, which can be complex or negative.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SVD Existence Theorem — Step-by-Step Logic

The existence of SVD is proved by reducing it to the **spectral theorem** (eigendecomposition of symmetric matrices):

### Step 1: Construct the Symmetric Matrix $A^{T} A$
$A^{T} A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is **symmetric** ($(A^{T}A)^{T} = A^{T}A$) and **positive semi-definite** ($x^{T}A^{T}Ax = \Vert Ax \Vert^{2} \geq 0$).

### Step 2: Diagonalize $A^{T} A$
By the spectral theorem, $A^{T}A = V \Lambda V^{T}$ where $V$ is orthogonal and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_{1}, \ldots, \lambda_{n})$ with $\lambda_{1} \geq \lambda_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_{n} \geq 0$.

### Step 3: Define Singular Values
$$\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, \min(m, n)$$

### Step 4: Build the Left Singular Vectors
For each $\sigma_{i} > 0$, set $u_{i} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{i}} A v_{i}$, then extend $\{u_{i}\}$ to an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ (if $m > r$).

### Step 5: Assemble the Matrices
Form $U = [u_{1} \; u_{2} \; \cdots \; u_{m}]$, $\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_{1}, \ldots, \sigma_{r})$ embedded in $\mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, and $V$ as above. Then $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for any SVD problem in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Property / Formula | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Fundamental SVD** | $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ | Universal factorization |
| 2 | **Singular values via $A^{T}A$** | $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}(A^{T}A)}$ | Numerical stability |
| 3 | **Singular values via $AA^{T}$** | $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}(AA^{T})}$ | Dual form (m×m) |
| 4 | **Right singular vectors** | $A^{T}A \, v_{i} = \sigma_{i}^{2} v_{i}$ | Eigendecomposition of $A^{T}A$ |
| 5 | **Left singular vectors** | $AA^{T} \, u_{i} = \sigma_{i}^{2} u_{i}$ | Eigendecomposition of $AA^{T}$ |
| 6 | **Action equation** | $A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} u_{i}$ | Geometric mapping |
| 7 | **Rank relation** | $\text{rank}(A) = $ number of non-zero $\sigma_{i}$ | Rank detection |
| 8 | **Frobenius norm** | $\Vert A \Vert_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_{i}^{2}$ | Low-rank approximation |
| 9 | **Spectral norm** | $\Vert A \Vert_{2} = \sigma_{\max} = \sigma_{1}$ | Largest singular value |
| 10 | **Moore–Penrose Pseudoinverse** | $A^{+} = V \Sigma^{+} U^{T}$ | Solving $Ax = b$ |
| 11 | **Determinant** | $\vert \det(A) \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_{i}$ (square A) | Volume scaling |
| 12 | **Best rank-$k$ approximation (Eckart–Young)** | $A_{k} = U_{k} \Sigma_{k} V_{k}^{T}$ | Dimensionality reduction |
| 13 | **Trace identity** | $\text{tr}(A^{T}A) = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_{i}^{2}$ | Energy preservation |
| 14 | **Orthogonality** | $U^{T}U = I_{m}$, $V^{T}V = I_{n}$ | Invertible factors |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, note that $\sigma_{i} \geq 0$ **always**, even for negative eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$ — this is impossible because $A^{T}A \succeq 0$. So we never take absolute value; the square root is of a non-negative quantity.

## 2.3 Types of SVD (Reduced, Compact, Truncated)

Given $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with rank $r$:

- **Full SVD**: $A = U_{m \times m} \, \Sigma_{m \times n} \, V_{n \times n}^{T}$ — uses all columns of $U$ and $V$.
- **Reduced (Thin) SVD**: $A = U_{m \times r} \, \Sigma_{r \times r} \, V_{n \times r}^{T}$ — only the $r$ non-zero singular values are kept.
- **Truncated SVD**: $A \approx \hat{U}_{m \times k} \, \hat{\Sigma}_{k \times k} \, \hat{V}_{n \times k}^{T}$ with $k < r$ — used for **dimensionality reduction** and **noise removal**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Image Compression**: Store only the top $k$ singular values/vectors; an image of rank 100 stored with $k=20$ retains 95% of the information but uses 5× less memory.
- **Recommender Systems**: Collaborative filtering via SVD (e.g., the Netflix Prize algorithm).
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**: PCA of centered data matrix $X$ is obtained by SVD: $X = U \Sigma V^{T}$, principal components are columns of $V$.
- **Natural Language Processing**: Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) uses truncated SVD on term-document matrices.
- **Solving Ill-Conditioned Systems**: Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse via SVD gives the minimum-norm least-squares solution to $Ax = b$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Eigendecomposition to SVD

We seek $U, \Sigma, V$ such that $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$. Start by post-multiplying by $V$:

$$A V = U \Sigma V^{T} V = U \Sigma$$

(since $V^{T}V = I$). Writing the $i$-th column of $V$ as $v_{i}$ and the $i$-th column of $U$ as $u_{i}$:

$$A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} u_{i} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, r$$

This is the **fundamental action equation** of SVD. Now derive the eigenvalue relations:

### 3.1.1 Right Singular Vectors from $A^{T} A$

Pre-multiply the action equation by $A^{T}$:

$$A^{T} A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} A^{T} u_{i}$$

But we also know from $A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} u_{i}$ that $A^{T} A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} A^{T} u_{i}$. Combining both:

$$A^{T} A v_{i} = \sigma_{i}^{2} v_{i} \quad \text{(by substituting } A^{T} u_{i} = \sigma_{i} v_{i}\text{)}$$

Hence $v_{i}$ are eigenvectors of $A^{T}A$ with eigenvalues $\sigma_{i}^{2} \geq 0$.

### 3.1.2 Left Singular Vectors from $AA^{T}$

Pre-multiply $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ by $A$ on the left and rearrange:

$$A A^{T} = U \Sigma V^{T} V \Sigma^{T} U^{T} = U \Sigma \Sigma^{T} U^{T}$$

The matrix $\Sigma \Sigma^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ is diagonal with entries $\sigma_{i}^{2}$. Therefore:

$$A A^{T} u_{i} = \sigma_{i}^{2} u_{i}$$

so $u_{i}$ are eigenvectors of $AA^{T}$ with the same eigenvalues $\sigma_{i}^{2}$.

### 3.1.3 Recovering $u_{i}$ from $v_{i}$ (when $\sigma_{i} > 0$)

From $A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} u_{i}$, we get the clean formula:

$$u_{i} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} A v_{i}$$

This avoids computing $AA^{T}$ directly — a **numerically efficient shortcut** that examiners love.

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example

**Problem:** Compute the full SVD of 
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$$

### Step 1: Compute $A^{T} A$

$$A^{T} A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+1+0 & 0+1+0 \\ 0+1+0 & 0+1+1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Eigenvalues of $A^{T} A$ (characteristic polynomial)

$$\det(A^{T}A - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^{2} - 1 = \lambda^{2} - 4\lambda + 3 = (\lambda - 3)(\lambda - 1)$$

So $\lambda_{1} = 3$ and $\lambda_{2} = 1$.

### Step 3: Singular Values

$$\sigma_{1} = \sqrt{3}, \quad \sigma_{2} = \sqrt{1} = 1$$

### Step 4: Right Singular Vectors (Eigenvectors of $A^{T}A$)

For $\lambda_{1} = 3$: solve $(A^{T}A - 3I) v_{1} = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} v_{1} = 0 \implies v_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

For $\lambda_{2} = 1$: solve $(A^{T}A - I) v_{2} = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} v_{2} = 0 \implies v_{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Left Singular Vectors via $u_{i} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} A v_{i}$

For $i = 1$:

$$u_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} A \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Verify: $\Vert u_{1} \Vert = \sqrt{\frac{1+4+1}{6}} = 1$ ✓

For $i = 2$:

$$u_{2} = \frac{1}{1} A \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Verify: $\Vert u_{2} \Vert = \sqrt{\frac{1+0+1}{2}} = 1$ ✓

### Step 6: Complete the Orthonormal Basis (Add $u_{3}$)

Since $U$ must be $3 \times 3$ orthogonal, we need a third column $u_{3}$ orthogonal to $u_{1}$ and $u_{2}$. Let $u_{3} = (a, b, c)^{T}$. Orthogonality to $u_{1}$ and $u_{2}$:

$$a + 2b + c = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad a - c = 0$$

From the second: $a = c$. Substituting: $2a + 2b = 0 \implies b = -a$. So $u_{3} = a(1, -1, 1)^{T}$. Normalize:

$$u_{3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 7: Assemble the Full SVD

$$U = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 2/\sqrt{6} & 0 & -1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & -1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 8: Verification (Reconstruct $A$)

Compute $U \Sigma V^{T}$:

$$U \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{2} \cdot 1 \\ 2/\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 1/\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{3} & -1/\sqrt{2} \cdot 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ \sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Then $V^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$U \Sigma V^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} (1/\sqrt{2}+1/\sqrt{2})/\sqrt{2} & (1/\sqrt{2}-1/\sqrt{2})/\sqrt{2} \\ (\sqrt{2}+0)/\sqrt{2} & (\sqrt{2}-0)/\sqrt{2} \\ (1/\sqrt{2}-1/\sqrt{2})/\sqrt{2} & (1/\sqrt{2}+1/\sqrt{2})/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = A \;\; \checkmark$$

The reconstruction matches the original matrix exactly, confirming the SVD.

## 4.1 SVD Decomposition–Reconstruction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeDecomp --> nodeStep1["Step 1 Compute eigenvalues of A transpose A"]
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2["Step 2 Singular values sigma i equals sqrt of lambda i"]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3["Step 3 Right singular vectors v i from eigenvectors of A transpose A"]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4["Step 4 Left singular vectors u i equals 1 by sigma i times A v i"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeU["Matrix U m by m orthogonal"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeS["Matrix Sigma m by n diagonal with sigma values"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeVt["Matrix V transpose n by n orthogonal"]
    nodeU --> nodeRecon["Matrix Multiplication U times Sigma times V transpose"]
    nodeS --> nodeRecon
    nodeVt --> nodeRecon
    nodeRecon --> nodeAprime["Reconstructed A prime equals A"]
```

```mermaid
    nodeCentering --> nodeSvdBlock["Truncated SVD Computation"]

    nodeSvdBlock --> nodeSapp["Top k Singular Values Sigma k"]
    nodeSvdBlock --> nodeVtapp["Top k Right Singular Vectors V k"]
    nodeUapp --> nodeRecon["Low Rank Approximation A k equals U k Sigma k V k transpose"]
    nodeSapp --> nodeRecon
    nodeVtapp --> nodeRecon
    nodeRecon --> nodeApp1["Image Compression Pipeline"]

```

## 4.3 Variants of SVD — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA2["Original A m by n with rank r"] --> nodeFull["Full SVD U m by m times Sigma m by n times V n by n transpose"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeReduced["Reduced SVD U m by r times Sigma r by r times V n by r transpose"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeTrunc["Truncated SVD U m by k times Sigma k by k times V n by k transpose where k less than r"]
    nodeFull --> nodeStorage1["Storage cost m times n plus m plus n"]
    nodeReduced --> nodeStorage2["Storage cost r times m plus n plus 1"]
    nodeTrunc --> nodeStorage3["Storage cost k times m plus n plus 1 - smallest"]
    nodeTrunc --> nodeQuality["Quality controlled by k - trade off accuracy vs compression"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of a matrix. How is it different from the eigendecomposition?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of a real matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is a factorization of the form $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$, where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are orthogonal matrices, and $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is a rectangular diagonal matrix containing the non-negative **singular values** $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r} > 0$ of $A$ on its diagonal.
> 
> **Differences from Eigendecomposition:**
> 1. Eigendecomposition is defined only for **square** matrices; SVD exists for **any** $m \times n$ matrix.
> 2. Eigenvalues of a real matrix can be negative or complex; singular values are always non-negative reals.
> 3. Eigenvectors are not necessarily orthogonal; left and right singular vectors are orthonormal.
> 4. SVD always exists; a real matrix's eigendecomposition exists only if it has a complete set of linearly independent eigenvectors.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differences.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any four properties of singular values of a matrix.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **Non-negativity:** Every singular value satisfies $\sigma_{i} \geq 0$.
> 2. **Count equals rank:** The number of non-zero singular values equals $\text{rank}(A)$.
> 3. **Spectral norm:** $\Vert A \Vert_{2} = \sigma_{1} = \max$ singular value.
> 4. **Frobenius norm:** $\Vert A \Vert_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_{i}^{2} = \text{tr}(A^{T}A)$.
> 5. **Ordering:** $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r} \geq 0$ (by convention).
> 6. **Singular values of $A^{T}$:** Identical to those of $A$.

*(Any four properties, 0.75 marks each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose** on $V$ (writing $A = U \Sigma V$ instead of $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$) is the **#1 reason students lose 2–3 marks** in SVD problems. Always check whether the question asks for $V$ or $V^{T}$.
> 2. **Not normalizing** the singular vectors: any eigenvector of $A^{T}A$ is correct, but for a valid SVD it MUST have unit length ($\Vert v_{i} \Vert = 1$). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unnormalized vectors.
> 3. **Confusing $\Sigma$ dimensions**: For $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $\Sigma$ is also $m \times n$, with shape matching $A$. A common error is to write $\Sigma$ as square $r \times r$.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step**: Always reconstruct $A$ from $U \Sigma V^{T}$ to catch arithmetic errors and earn full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Find the Singular Value Decomposition of the matrix 
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Compute $A^{T} A$:
$$A^{T} A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9+1 & 3+3 \\ 3+3 & 1+9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6 \\ 6 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Eigenvalues: $\det(A^{T}A - \lambda I) = (10-\lambda)^{2} - 36 = \lambda^{2} - 20\lambda + 64 = 0$.

$$\lambda = \frac{20 \pm \sqrt{400 - 256}}{2} = \frac{20 \pm 12}{2} \implies \lambda_{1} = 16, \; \lambda_{2} = 4$$

**Step 3:** Singular values: $\sigma_{1} = 4$, $\sigma_{2} = 2$.

**Step 4:** Right singular vectors — solve $(A^{T}A - 16I)v_{1} = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -6 & 6 \\ 6 & -6 \end{bmatrix} v_{1} = 0 \implies v_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solve $(A^{T}A - 4I)v_{2} = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 \\ 6 & 6 \end{bmatrix} v_{2} = 0 \implies v_{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Left singular vectors via $u_{i} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} A v_{i}$:

$$u_{1} = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$u_{2} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Final SVD:
$$U = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Since $A$ is symmetric, $U = V$ (a special case).

**(b)** Verify the SVD by reconstructing $A$, and explain the **Eckart–Young theorem** for best low-rank approximation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Verification:** Compute $U \Sigma V^{T}$:

$$U \Sigma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$U \Sigma V^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = A \;\; \checkmark$$

**Eckart–Young Theorem (Best Low-Rank Approximation):** For any $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with SVD $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ and singular values $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r} > 0$, the best rank-$k$ approximation (in both spectral and Frobenius norms) is given by:

$$A_{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_{i} u_{i} v_{i}^{T} = U_{k} \Sigma_{k} V_{k}^{T}$$

with the optimal error $\Vert A - A_{k} \Vert = \sigma_{k+1}$ (spectral norm) or $\sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_{i}^{2}}$ (Frobenius norm).

**Application in Machine Learning:** In PCA, the leading $k$ singular vectors $V_{k}$ form the optimal $k$-dimensional subspace onto which data should be projected to preserve maximum variance. The discarded small singular values typically correspond to noise.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For the matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, compute $A^{T} A$ and find its eigenvalues and singular values. Hence obtain the matrix $V$ of right singular vectors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Compute $A^{T} A$:

$$A^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A^{T} A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+1+0 & 0+1+0 \\ 0+1+0 & 0+1+1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Characteristic polynomial: $\det(A^{T}A - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^{2} - 1 = \lambda^{2} - 4\lambda + 3 = (\lambda-3)(\lambda-1)$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1} = 3, \; \lambda_{2} = 1$.

**Step 3:** Singular values: $\sigma_{1} = \sqrt{3}, \; \sigma_{2} = 1$.

**Step 4:** Right singular vectors (eigenvectors of $A^{T}A$):

For $\lambda_{1} = 3$: $(A^{T}A - 3I) v_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} v_{1} = 0 \implies v_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$

For $\lambda_{2} = 1$: $(A^{T}A - I) v_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} v_{2} = 0 \implies v_{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$

Therefore:

$$V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b)** Derive the left singular vectors $u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}$ and write down the complete full SVD of $A$. Compute $\text{rank}(A)$ and the Frobenius norm $\Vert A \Vert_{F}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute $u_{1}$ and $u_{2}$ using $u_{i} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} A v_{i}$:

$$u_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$u_{2} = \frac{1}{1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Find $u_{3}$ orthogonal to both $u_{1}$ and $u_{2}$. Let $u_{3} = (a, b, c)^{T}$. Conditions:

- $a + 2b + c = 0$ (from $u_{1} \cdot u_{3} = 0$)
- $a - c = 0$ (from $u_{2} \cdot u_{3} = 0$)

From the second: $a = c$. Substituting: $2a + 2b = 0 \implies b = -a$. Choose $a = 1$: $u_{3} = (1, -1, 1)^{T}$. Normalize: $u_{3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} (1, -1, 1)^{T}$.

**Step 3:** Assemble $U$:

$$U = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 2/\sqrt{6} & 0 & -1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & -1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Complete Full SVD:

$$A = U \Sigma V^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 2/\sqrt{6} & 0 & -1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & -1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Rank and Frobenius norm:

- $\text{rank}(A) = $ number of non-zero singular values $= 2$.
- $\Vert A \Vert_{F}^{2} = \sigma_{1}^{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2} = 3 + 1 = 4$, hence $\Vert A \Vert_{F} = 2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- **Core identity**: $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ — universal, no restrictions on $A$.
- **Singular values** = square roots of eigenvalues of $A^{T} A$ (or $AA^{T}$), always **non-negative reals**.
- **Right singular vectors** = eigenvectors of $A^{T} A$.
- **Left singular vectors** = eigenvectors of $AA^{T}$, or use $u_{i} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} A v_{i}$.
- **Action equation** (memorize!): $A v_{i} = \sigma_{i} u_{i}$.
- **Orthogonality**: $U^{T} U = I_{m}$ and $V^{T} V = I_{n}$.
- **Rank = number of non-zero $\sigma_{i}$**.
- **$\Sigma$ is always shaped $m \times n$** in full SVD (rectangular diagonal).
- **Frobenius norm**: $\Vert A \Vert_{F}^{2} = \sum \sigma_{i}^{2} = \text{tr}(A^{T} A)$.
- **Spectral (operator) norm**: $\Vert A \Vert_{2} = \sigma_{1}$.
- **Eckart–Young**: $A_{k} = U_{k} \Sigma_{k} V_{k}^{T}$ is the best rank-$k$ approximation.
- **PCA connection**: Principal components = right singular vectors $V$.
- **Pseudoinverse**: $A^{+} = V \Sigma^{+} U^{T}$ where $\Sigma^{+}$ flips non-zero $\sigma_{i}$.
- **Symmetric matrix shortcut**: If $A = A^{T}$, then $U = V$ and $\sigma_{i} = \vert \lambda_{i} \vert$.
- **Computation sequence**: (1) $A^{T} A$, (2) eigenvalues, (3) singular values = $\sqrt{\lambda}$, (4) $V$ from eigenvectors, (5) $U$ from $u_{i} = A v_{i} / \sigma_{i}$, (6) extend $U$ to be square, (7) **verify** by reconstruction.
- **Common slip**: Always normalize $v_{i}, u_{i}$ to unit length; always transpose $V$ in the final expression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
