topics.title: Gradients of Vector Valued Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradients of Vector Valued Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Gradient of a Scalar-Valued Function

Let $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ be a scalar-valued (scalar field) function that is differentiable at a point $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The **gradient** of $f$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ is the column vector of all its first-order partial derivatives:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[2pt] \vdots \\[2pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this is referred to as the **numerator-layout convention**, where the gradient is treated as a column vector. Machine learning libraries such as **PyTorch** and **TensorFlow** also follow the numerator layout. Always check the convention used in the problem statement to avoid dimensional mistakes.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Gradient / Jacobian of a Vector-Valued Function

Let $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ be a vector-valued function (vector field) given by

$$
\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} f_1(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \\ f_2(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \\ \vdots \\ f_m(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}
$$

The **gradient of $\mathbf{f}$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}$** is the $m \times n$ **Jacobian matrix** $D\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}^T}$ (numerator layout):

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_n} \\[4pt] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_n} \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}
$$

Each row $i$ of the Jacobian is the gradient of the scalar component $f_i$ (laid out as a row), and each column $j$ collects the partial derivatives of every output with respect to the input $x_j$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Notations in Use Across KTU Textbooks:**
> 1. **Numerator layout** (used in Bishop, Murphy): $\partial \mathbf{f} / \partial \mathbf{x}$ is $m \times n$. The "shape" of the derivative matches the shape of the **numerator** $\mathbf{f}$.
> 2. **Denominator layout** (used in some older texts): $\partial \mathbf{f} / \partial \mathbf{x}$ is $n \times m$ — the transpose of the numerator layout.
>
> KTU 2024 follows the **numerator layout** as the default. Always write the answer in the same layout as the question.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Gradient as a "Compass + Slope Meter"**
> Imagine you are standing on a foggy hill whose height is $f(x_1, x_2)$. You cannot see the top, but you have a magical compass that tells you two things at once:
> 1. The **direction** in which the hill rises the fastest.
> 2. The **steepness** of that fastest rise.
> The gradient $\nabla f$ is exactly that compass reading — a vector that points "up the slope" and whose length tells you how steep it is. If you walk in the **opposite** direction, you descend the fastest. This is the geometric reason **gradient descent** is the workhorse of machine learning optimization.

For a **vector-valued** function, the Jacobian is a stack of such compasses — one compass per output component — arranged into a single matrix. The Jacobian is the best **linear approximation** of $\mathbf{f}$ near $\mathbf{x}$:

$$
\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x} + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \;\approx\; \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) \;+\; \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \, \Delta \mathbf{x}
$$

This is the multivariate analog of $f(x+\epsilon) \approx f(x) + f'(x)\epsilon$ in single-variable calculus.

### 1.4 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Quantity | Symbol | Dimension | Physical/Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scalar input | $\mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ | A point in input space |
| Vector output | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ | A point in output space |
| Gradient (Jacobian) | $\partial \mathbf{f} / \partial \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Best linear map from input perturbation to output perturbation |
| Directional derivative | $D_{\mathbf{u}} f$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | Rate of change along unit vector $\mathbf{u}$ |

All four quantities above are the **central vocabulary** of KTU Module 3 and appear in every Part-B question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why and How" of the Gradient

- **Why does the gradient point in the steepest-ascent direction?**
  By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, for any unit vector $\mathbf{u}$, the directional derivative satisfies
  $$
  D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{u} \;\leq\; \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \;=\; \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert.
  $$
  Equality holds if and only if $\mathbf{u}$ is parallel to $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$. So the gradient is both the maximum directional derivative and the direction in which it is achieved.

- **Why is the Jacobian the best linear approximation?**
  Differentiability at $\mathbf{x}$ means there exists a matrix $A$ such that
  $$
  \lim_{\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert \to 0} \frac{\Vert \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}+\Delta \mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) - A \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert}{\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert} \;=\; 0.
  $$
  By the definition of the partial derivatives, the unique such $A$ is exactly the Jacobian $\partial \mathbf{f} / \partial \mathbf{x}$.

- **How is the Jacobian computed?**
  Differentiate **one output component at a time**, holding the input components as independent variables, then stack the resulting row vectors.

### 2.2 Four Fundamental Rules (with their 'Why')

1. **Constant rule:** $\dfrac{\partial \, \mathbf{c}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}_{m \times n}$, because constants do not vary with $\mathbf{x}$.
2. **Linearity rule:** $\dfrac{\partial (A\mathbf{f} + B\mathbf{g})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = A \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + B \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ (sizes must align for matrix multiplication $A \cdot \text{Jacobian}$).
3. **Product rule:** $\dfrac{\partial (f \mathbf{g})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = f \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{g} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ when $f$ is scalar and $\mathbf{g}$ is vector.
4. **Chain rule (the workhorse of neural-network backpropagation):** If $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ and $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^{m} \to \mathbb{R}^{p}$ are both differentiable, then for the composition $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}))$,
   $$
   \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{f}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}, \qquad \text{shape: } (p \times n) = (p \times m)(m \times n).
   $$
   The **Why**: the Jacobian is the best linear approximation, and the composition of best linear approximations is again a best linear approximation.

### 2.3 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Numerator Layout)

| # | Function Form | Gradient / Jacobian | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ (linear, scalar) | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f = \mathbf{c}$ | Constant vector |
| 2 | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x}$ (quadratic, scalar) | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f = (A + A^T)\mathbf{x}$ | Critical in least-squares ML |
| 3 | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$ | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f = 2 A^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$ | Linear-regression loss |
| 4 | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x}$ (linear, vector) | $\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x} = A$ | Jacobian equals the matrix itself |
| 5 | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{x})$, $\sigma$ applied element-wise | $\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x} = \text{diag}(\sigma'(\mathbf{x}))$ | Activation-function map |
| 6 | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \log \sum_i e^{x_i}$ (LogSumExp) | $\nabla f = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{x})$ | Softmax = gradient of LogSumExp |
| 7 | Directional derivative (scalar field) | $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{u} = \Vert \nabla f \Vert \cos\theta$ | Max when $\mathbf{u} = \nabla f / \Vert \nabla f \Vert$ |
| 8 | Chain rule | $\partial \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}))/\partial \mathbf{x} = (\partial \mathbf{g}/\partial \mathbf{f})(\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x})$ | Used in backpropagation |
| 9 | Scalar-by-scalar product | $\partial (u^T v)/\partial \mathbf{x} = (\partial u/\partial \mathbf{x})^T v + (\partial v/\partial \mathbf{x})^T u$ | Symmetric form |
| 10 | Inverse map | $\partial \mathbf{x}/\partial \mathbf{y} = (\partial \mathbf{y}/\partial \mathbf{x})^{-1}$ | Requires square, invertible Jacobian |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World Relevance):**
> - **Formula 3** is the closed-form solution of ordinary least-squares regression: setting $\nabla f = 0$ gives the **normal equation** $A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$.
> - **Formula 6** is the analytical reason the **softmax** activation emerges naturally in multinomial classification loss.
> - **Formula 8 (chain rule)** is the single equation that makes deep learning (backpropagation through millions of parameters) computationally tractable.
> - **Formula 10 (inverse map)** is the foundation of the **change-of-variables formula** used in normalizing flows and variational autoencoders.

### 2.4 Directional Derivative — Operational Steps

To compute the rate of change of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}_0$ along direction $\mathbf{u}$ (where $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$):

1. Compute the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)$.
2. Form the inner product $D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)^T \mathbf{u}$.
3. Report the sign: positive $\Rightarrow$ increasing along $\mathbf{u}$; negative $\Rightarrow$ decreasing.
4. The maximum of $D_{\mathbf{u}} f$ over all unit $\mathbf{u}$ is $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) \Vert$, attained at $\mathbf{u} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)/\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) \Vert$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Gradient of a Quadratic Form (Bishop-style)

> **Problem.** Let $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x}$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is symmetric and $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. Compute $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f$.

**Step 1 — Expand the quadratic form into a sum.**

$$
\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_i \, A_{ij} \, x_j.
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to a single variable $x_k$.**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x_k}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_i A_{ij} x_j\right) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{kj} x_j \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i A_{ik}.
$$

**Explanation of the split:** When we differentiate w.r.t. $x_k$, the term $i = k$ in the first sum survives (giving $\sum_j A_{kj} x_j$), and the term $j = k$ in the second sum survives (giving $\sum_i x_i A_{ik}$). All other terms vanish because they do not depend on $x_k$.

**Step 3 — Rewrite the two sums in matrix form.**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{kj} x_j \;=\; (A\mathbf{x})_k, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i A_{ik} \;=\; (A^T \mathbf{x})_k.
$$

**Step 4 — Stack into the gradient vector.**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f \;=\; A\mathbf{x} \;+\; A^T \mathbf{x} \;=\; (A + A^T)\mathbf{x}.
$$

**Step 5 — Use the symmetry $A = A^T$.**

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x}) \;=\; 2A\mathbf{x}\quad \text{when } A = A^T.\;}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Jacobian of a Vector-Valued Function

> **Problem.** Let $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ be defined by
> $$
> \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} f_1 \\ f_2 \\ f_3 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} x_1^2 + x_2 \\ \sin(x_1 x_2) \\ e^{x_1 - x_2} \end{pmatrix}.
> $$
> Compute the Jacobian $\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ at a general point $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^T$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate component $f_1$.**

$$
\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1, \qquad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} = 1.
$$

Row 1 of the Jacobian: $\bigl(2x_1,\; 1\bigr)$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate component $f_2$.**

$$
\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} = x_2 \cos(x_1 x_2), \qquad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} = x_1 \cos(x_1 x_2).
$$

Row 2: $\bigl(x_2 \cos(x_1 x_2),\; x_1 \cos(x_1 x_2)\bigr)$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate component $f_3$.**

$$
\frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x_1} = e^{x_1 - x_2}, \qquad \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x_2} = -e^{x_1 - x_2}.
$$

Row 3: $\bigl(e^{x_1 - x_2},\; -e^{x_1 - x_2}\bigr)$.

**Step 4 — Assemble the Jacobian (size $3 \times 2$).**

$$
\boxed{\;
\frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\;
\begin{pmatrix}
2x_1 & 1 \\[4pt]
x_2 \cos(x_1 x_2) & x_1 \cos(x_1 x_2) \\[4pt]
e^{x_1 - x_2} & -e^{x_1 - x_2}
\end{pmatrix}
\;}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Chain Rule (Backpropagation Style)

> **Problem.** Let $L(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}))^T \mathbf{w}$, where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $\mathbf{h} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^{m} \to \mathbb{R}^{p}$, and $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$. Compute $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L$ using the chain rule.

**Step 1 — Differentiate the outer scalar function.**

Let $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}))$. Then $L = \mathbf{z}^T \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{z}$. By the linearity/constant rule,

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} \;=\; \mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}.
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\mathbf{g}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{h}$.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{h}} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}.
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $\mathbf{h}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}.
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the chain rule.**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L \;=\; \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}}}_{p \times 1} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{h}}}_{p \times m} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}}_{m \times n} \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}.
$$

**Shape check:** $1 \cdot p \cdot m \cdot n$ — inner dimensions $p = p$ and $m = m$ match, so the chain multiplication is valid and the result is $n \times 1$ as required for a gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> This three-factor chain rule, applied repeatedly layer-by-layer, is exactly the **backpropagation algorithm** of Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986). KTU expects students to know both the formula and the matrix shapes.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Gradient of the Least-Squares Loss

> **Problem.** Show that $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2 = 2A^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$.

**Step 1 — Expand the squared norm.**

$$
\Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2 \;=\; (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}) \;=\; \mathbf{x}^T A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2 \mathbf{b}^T A \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}.
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate each term using the rules of §2.2.**

- Quadratic term: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}^T A^T A \mathbf{x}) = 2 A^T A \mathbf{x}$ (using §2.3 row 2 with $A \to A^T A$ symmetric).
- Linear term: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(-2 \mathbf{b}^T A \mathbf{x}) = -2 A^T \mathbf{b}$ (using §2.3 row 1 with $\mathbf{c} = A^T \mathbf{b}$).
- Constant term: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 3 — Sum the three pieces.**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2 \;=\; 2A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2A^T \mathbf{b} \;=\; 2A^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}).
$$

**Step 4 — Critical-point equation (the Normal Equation).**

Setting the gradient to zero gives $A^T A \mathbf{x}^\star = A^T \mathbf{b}$, which is the **Moore–Penrose / least-squares solution** of $A\mathbf{x} \approx \mathbf{b}$. This is the closed-form solution of linear regression.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How the Jacobian Drives a Feed-Forward Neural Network

The diagram below captures the data flow inside one layer of a deep neural network, with the Jacobian (gradient of the layer's vector-valued output) playing the central role in backpropagation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vector x in R^n] --> B[Linear Map: z = Wx + b]
    B --> C[Vector Valued Activation: h = sigma of z]
    C --> D[Output Vector h in R^m]
    D --> E[Loss Scalar L of h, y]
    E -.backward gradient.-> F[Compute dL/dh in R^m]
    F --> G[Compute Jacobian J_h of sigma: m by m diagonal]
    G --> H[Compute J_W = dL/dh times J_h times dW/dW of size m by n]
    H --> I[Weight Update: W new = W old minus eta times J_W]
    I -.closed loop feedback.-> B

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#e65100
    style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#4a148c
    style D fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#880e4f
    style F fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#ff6f00
    style G fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#006064
    style H fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e,color:#33691e
    style I fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#311b92,color:#311b92
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map: Scalar vs Vector-Valued Gradient

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Scalar Function f: R to R] --> S1[Derivative: scalar df/dx]
    S1 --> S2[Geometric Meaning: Slope of tangent line]

    V1[Scalar Field f: R^n to R] --> V2[Gradient: n by 1 column vector nabla f]
    V2 --> V3[Geometric Meaning: Direction of steepest ascent]

    V4[Vector Field f: R^n to R^m] --> V5[Jacobian: m by n matrix]
    V5 --> V6[Geometric Meaning: Best linear approximation of f near x]

    V2 --> V7[Used in Gradient Descent Optimization]
    V5 --> V8[Used in Backpropagation through Deep Networks]
    V5 --> V9[Chain Rule: J of g of f equals J_g times J_f]
```

### 4.3 Chain-Rule Block Diagram (Vector Composition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Space R^n] -- x --> F[Vector Map f of x in R^m]
    F -- f of x --> G[Vector Map g of f in R^p]
    G -- g of f of x --> H[Output Space R^p]

    A -. Jf: m by n .-> M1[Jacobian of f at x]
    F -. Jg: p by m .-> M2[Jacobian of g at f of x]
    M1 -. multiply .-> M3[Chain Rule Product Jg times Jf: p by n]
    M2 -. multiply .-> M3
    M3 --> M4[Best Linear Approximation of g of f of x at x]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]**
*Define the Jacobian matrix of a vector-valued function $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$. What is its dimension and what does each entry represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Jacobian of $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_m)^T$ with respect to $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)^T$ is the $m \times n$ matrix

$$
J_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n} \\[4pt] \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_n} \end{pmatrix}.
$$

- **Dimension:** $m \times n$ (rows = outputs, columns = inputs). **(1 mark)**
- **Each entry $J_{ij} = \partial f_i / \partial x_j$** is the rate of change of the $i$-th output with respect to the $j$-th input, holding all other inputs constant. **(1 mark)**
- **Geometric / operational meaning:** It is the best **linear approximation** of $\mathbf{f}$ in a neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}$, satisfying $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}+\Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) + J_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}) \Delta \mathbf{x}$. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]**
*State the multivariate chain rule for two vector-valued functions. Use it to derive $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \Vert A\mathbf{x} \Vert^2$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Chain rule statement:** If $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ and $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}^p$ are differentiable, then for $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}))$,
  $$
  \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{f}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}. \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
  $$

- **Application:** Write $\Vert A\mathbf{x}\Vert^2 = (A\mathbf{x})^T (A\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y}$ with $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}$.
  - $\partial \mathbf{y} / \partial \mathbf{x} = A$. **(0.5 marks)**
  - $\partial (\mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y}) / \partial \mathbf{y} = 2 \mathbf{y}^T$ (row vector) or $2\mathbf{y}$ (column). **(0.5 marks)**
  - By the chain rule:
    $$
    \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \Vert A\mathbf{x}\Vert^2 \;=\; 2\mathbf{y}^T \cdot A \;=\; 2\mathbf{x}^T A^T A. \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
    $$

  In column-vector (gradient) form: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert A\mathbf{x}\Vert^2 = 2A^T A \mathbf{x}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Apr 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compute the gradient of the scalar function
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \log \sum_{i=1}^{n} e^{x_i}, \qquad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n,
$$
and show that the resulting gradient is the **softmax** of $\mathbf{x}$. Also compute the Hessian.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Let $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ and $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ be defined by
$$
\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{pmatrix} x_1^2 \\ x_1 x_2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{y}) = \begin{pmatrix} \sin y_1 \\ \cos y_2 \end{pmatrix}.
$$
Using the chain rule, compute the Jacobian of $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}))$ at $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1)^T$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* For the **least-squares loss** $L(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$ with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, derive the gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L$ step-by-step, and then derive the **normal equation** $A^T A \mathbf{x}^\star = A^T \mathbf{b}$. State the condition under which $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is unique and explain its meaning.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For a scalar function $f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ with $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^m$, derive the gradient of the **vector-matrix form** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{y}$ with respect to both $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$.

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — Gradient of LogSumExp

**Step 1 — Write the function in matrix form.** Let $s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e^{x_i} = \mathbf{1}^T \exp(\mathbf{x})$, where $\exp(\mathbf{x})$ is the element-wise exponential.

**Step 2 — Differentiate $s$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$.**

$$
\frac{\partial s}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} e^{x_1} \\ e^{x_2} \\ \vdots \\ e^{x_n} \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \exp(\mathbf{x}).
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule to $\log s$.**

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{\partial s}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sum_{j} e^{x_j}} \begin{pmatrix} e^{x_1} \\ e^{x_2} \\ \vdots \\ e^{x_n} \end{pmatrix}.
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the softmax.** Define

$$
\text{softmax}(\mathbf{x})_i \;=\; \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} e^{x_j}}.
$$

Therefore

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \text{softmax}(\mathbf{x}).\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Hessian.** The Hessian is the $n \times n$ matrix of second partial derivatives:

$$
H_{ij} \;=\; \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left(\frac{e^{x_i}}{s}\right) \;=\; \frac{\delta_{ij} e^{x_i} s - e^{x_i} e^{x_j}}{s^2} \;=\; \text{softmax}(\mathbf{x})_i \left(\delta_{ij} - \text{softmax}(\mathbf{x})_j\right).
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\boxed{\;H \;=\; \text{diag}(\mathbf{p}) - \mathbf{p} \mathbf{p}^T,\quad \text{where } \mathbf{p} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{x}).\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Hessian $H$ is **positive semi-definite** because for any $\mathbf{v}$,
> $\mathbf{v}^T H \mathbf{v} = \sum_i p_i v_i^2 - (\sum_i p_i v_i)^2 \geq 0$ by Jensen's inequality. Hence LogSumExp is **convex** — a critical fact in convex optimization and maximum-likelihood estimation.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — Chain Rule for Two Vector Maps

**Step 1 — Compute the Jacobian of $\mathbf{f}$ at a general $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^T$.**

$$
J_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2x_1 & 0 \\[2pt] x_2 & x_1 \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}.
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian of $\mathbf{g}$ at a general $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2)^T$.**

$$
J_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{y}) \;=\; \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos y_1 & 0 \\[2pt] 0 & -\sin y_2 \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}.
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule.**

$$
J_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; J_{\mathbf{g}}\!\left(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})\right) \cdot J_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}).
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1)^T$.**

First, $\mathbf{f}(1,1) = (1, 1)^T$, so $J_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{f}(1,1)) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -\sin 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

Next, $J_{\mathbf{f}}(1,1) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 5 — Multiply the two Jacobians.**

$$
J_{\mathbf{h}}(1,1) \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -\sin 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2\cos 1 & 0 \\[2pt] -\sin 1 & -\sin 1 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

**Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;J_{\mathbf{h}}(1,1) \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2\cos 1 & 0 \\[2pt] -\sin 1 & -\sin 1 \end{pmatrix}.\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Chain-Rule Order):**
> 1. **Matrix order matters.** Multiplying $J_{\mathbf{f}} \cdot J_{\mathbf{g}}$ instead of $J_{\mathbf{g}} \cdot J_{\mathbf{f}}$ produces the **wrong Jacobian** because matrix multiplication is non-commutative. Always order the Jacobians so that inner dimensions match: $(\text{output dim} \times \text{middle dim}) \cdot (\text{middle dim} \times \text{input dim})$.
> 2. **Evaluate $\mathbf{f}$ first, then take the Jacobian of $\mathbf{g}$ at $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$**, not at $\mathbf{x}$. Many students forget this and plug $\mathbf{x}$ directly into $J_{\mathbf{g}}$.
> 3. **Use radians, not degrees**, in trigonometric derivatives.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — Least-Squares Gradient and Normal Equation

**Step 1 — Expand the squared norm.**

$$
L(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}) \;=\; \mathbf{x}^T A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2\mathbf{b}^T A \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}.
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate term by term.**

- Quadratic: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}^T A^T A \mathbf{x}) = 2A^T A \mathbf{x}$ (since $A^T A$ is symmetric).
- Linear: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(-2\mathbf{b}^T A \mathbf{x}) = -2A^T \mathbf{b}$.
- Constant: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 3 — Sum.**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; 2A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2A^T \mathbf{b} \;=\; 2A^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}).
$$

**Step 4 — Set the gradient to zero (first-order necessary condition).**

$$
A^T A \mathbf{x}^\star \;=\; A^T \mathbf{b}. \quad \text{(Normal Equation)}
$$

**Step 5 — Uniqueness condition.** $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is **unique** if and only if $A^T A$ is **invertible**, equivalently $\text{rank}(A) = n$ (full column rank). **Meaning:** the columns of $A$ are linearly independent, so $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ is overdetermined but has a unique least-squares best fit.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — Mixed Vector-Matrix Gradient

Let
$$
f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \;=\; \mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{y}.
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$ (treat $\mathbf{y}$ as constant).**

- $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} (\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y}) = A \mathbf{y}$ (since $A \mathbf{y}$ is a constant vector w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$).
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} (\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}$.
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} (\mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{0}$ (no $\mathbf{x}$ dependence).

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f \;=\; A \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{c}.\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{y}$ (treat $\mathbf{x}$ as constant).**

- $\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} (\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y}) = A^T \mathbf{x}$ (the gradient of a scalar $\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{y}$ is $A^T \mathbf{x}$).
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} (\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$.
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} (\mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{d}$.

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} f \;=\; A^T \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{d}.\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Mixed Gradients):**
> 1. When differentiating the **bilinear** term $\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$, students commonly write $(A\mathbf{y})^T$ (a row). The correct column-vector answer is $A\mathbf{y}$. Convert at the very end if the question asks for a row.
> 2. **Asymmetry trap:** $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} (\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y}) = A\mathbf{y}$ but $\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} (\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{y}) = A^T \mathbf{x}$. They are **not** equal unless $A$ is symmetric. This is the source of many sign / transpose errors in KTU scripts.
> 3. Forgetting the $+A^T$ transpose on the second gradient is the single most frequent mistake — **deduct 2 marks** if missing.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gradient of a scalar field** is a column vector $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ containing the first partial derivatives.
- **Gradient / Jacobian of a vector field** $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ is the $m \times n$ matrix $\partial \mathbf{f} / \partial \mathbf{x}$.
- **Directional derivative** along unit $\mathbf{u}$: $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{u}$. Max value is $\Vert \nabla f \Vert$, attained at $\mathbf{u} = \nabla f / \Vert \nabla f \Vert$.
- **Chain rule (the most-tested formula):** $\partial \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})) / \partial \mathbf{x} = (\partial \mathbf{g}/\partial \mathbf{f})(\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x})$, with shapes $(p \times n) = (p \times m)(m \times n)$.
- **Quadratic-form gradient:** $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x}) = (A + A^T)\mathbf{x}$; reduces to $2A\mathbf{x}$ when $A$ is symmetric.
- **Linear-form gradient:** $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}$.
- **Least-squares gradient:** $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2 = 2A^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$; setting to zero gives the **normal equation** $A^T A \mathbf{x}^\star = A^T \mathbf{b}$.
- **LogSumExp gradient** equals the **softmax** — a high-yield identity for multinomial logistic regression.
- **Numerator layout** is the KTU default; if the question says "denominator layout", present the **transpose** of your Jacobian.
- **Always verify matrix dimensions** before multiplying Jacobians — the single biggest source of marks lost in KTU valuation is dimension mismatch.
- **Use radians** in all trig derivatives.
- **Backpropagation = repeated application of the chain rule** to a deep composition of vector-valued layers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradients of Matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradients of Matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Gradients of Matrices — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Mathematics for Machine Learning (PEEET413, Module 3)**, the **gradient of a scalar-valued function with respect to a matrix** is the matrix of partial derivatives of that function with respect to each individual element of the input matrix.

Given a scalar function $f: \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \to \mathbb{R}$ and a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, the **matrix gradient** (also called the **matrix derivative** or **Jacobian-as-matrix**) is defined as:

$$
\nabla_X f = \frac{\partial f}{\partial X} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{11}} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{12}} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{1n}} \\
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{21}} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{22}} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{2n}} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{m1}} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{m2}} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{mn}}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The gradient of a matrix $\nabla_X f$ always has the **same shape** as $X$ itself. If $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, then $\nabla_X f \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$. This shape-matching property is critical for writing gradient-descent updates of the form $X \leftarrow X - \eta \, \nabla_X f$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Thermostat Grid" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a rectangular room floor made up of **heating coils**, where each coil is one element of a matrix $X$. The temperature of the room at any instant is the scalar $f(X)$. The **gradient matrix** $\nabla_X f$ is essentially a **heat-map of dials**, one for each coil, telling you:
> - **How much** would the room temperature change if you slightly turned up that specific coil?
> - **In which direction** (increase or decrease)?
>
> If a dial reads a large positive number, that coil is "hot" — turning it up changes the room a lot. If the dial reads near zero, that coil barely affects the room. The gradient matrix is therefore a **directional sensitivity map** for the entire heating grid.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Central in Machine Learning

In virtually every machine-learning algorithm, we minimize a **loss function** $L(W)$ over a parameter matrix $W$ (e.g., the weight matrix in a neural network or linear regression). The update rule of **gradient descent** is:

$$
W^{(t+1)} = W^{(t)} - \eta \, \nabla_W L(W^{(t)})
$$

To execute this rule, we **must** know $\nabla_W L$ in closed form. Computing these matrix gradients efficiently — without flattening the matrix into a vector — is the subject of this module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metrics & constants used in this topic:**
> - $f, g, h$ : scalar-valued functions, $f: \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \to \mathbb{R}$
> - $X, A, B, W$ : matrices
> - $\eta$ : learning rate (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$)
> - $\text{tr}(\cdot)$ : trace operator; $\|\cdot\|_F$ : Frobenius norm
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building from First Principles — Vector Gradients First

The concept is most easily grasped by first reviewing vector gradients. If $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, then $\nabla_x f \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The **matrix gradient** generalizes this idea: each entry of $\nabla_X f$ is a partial derivative of $f$ with respect to the corresponding entry of $X$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation convention used throughout KTU Module 3:**
> - $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X}$ and $\nabla_X f$ are used **interchangeably**.
> - All functions $f$ are **scalar-valued**. (The gradient of a vector or matrix with respect to a matrix is a higher-order tensor and is covered separately in Module 4/5.)
> - The shape rule: $\text{shape}\!\left(\nabla_X f\right) = \text{shape}(X)$.

## 2.2 Fundamental Rules of Matrix Differentiation

1. **Linearity Rule**

   $$
   \nabla_X \left( \alpha f(X) + \beta g(X) \right) = \alpha \, \nabla_X f(X) + \beta \, \nabla_X g(X)
   $$

2. **Product Rule (for scalar-by-scalar product)**

   $$
   \nabla_X \left( f(X) \, g(X) \right) = g(X) \, \nabla_X f(X) + f(X) \, \nabla_X g(X)
   $$

3. **Chain Rule (scalar composition)**

   Let $h(t) = f(g(t))$ where $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Then:

   $$
   \frac{\partial h}{\partial X} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial g} \cdot \frac{\partial g}{\partial X}
   $$

4. **Transpose Rule**

   $$
   \nabla_X f(X) = \left( \nabla_{X^\top} f(X) \right)^\top
   $$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for all matrix-gradient identities you will be tested on in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the shape and the pattern of each entry.

| # | Function $f(X)$ | Domain / Shape of $X$ | Gradient $\nabla_X f$ | Shape of Gradient |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a^\top X b$ (where $a,b$ are constants) | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $a b^\top$ | $m \times n$ |
| 2 | $\text{tr}(A X B)$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $A^\top B^\top$ | $m \times n$ |
| 3 | $\text{tr}(X^\top A)$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $A$ | $m \times n$ |
| 4 | $\text{tr}(X^\top A X B)$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $A^\top X B^\top + A X B$ | $m \times n$ |
| 5 | $\text{tr}(X^\top X)$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $2X$ | $m \times n$ |
| 6 | $\|X\|_F^2 = \text{tr}(X^\top X)$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $2X$ | $m \times n$ |
| 7 | $\log \det(X)$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $X \succ 0$ | $X^{-\top} = (X^{-1})^\top$ | $n \times n$ |
| 8 | $X^{-1}$ (matrix-valued) | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | $-X^{-1} \, dX \, X^{-1}$ (differential form) | $n \times n$ |
| 9 | $c^\top X d$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $c d^\top$ | $m \times n$ |
| 10 | $\sum_{i,j} x_{ij}^2$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $2X$ | $m \times n$ |
| 11 | $X \odot Y$ (elementwise, $f = \mathbf{1}^\top (X \odot Y) \mathbf{1}$) | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $Y$ | $m \times n$ |
| 12 | $\|A X - B\|_F^2$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $2 A^\top (A X - B)$ | $m \times n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pipe-symbol warning** — In the KTU 2024 examination, when you write absolute value or determinant bars inside a markdown table, you **must** write $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\det(\cdot)$ — never the bare vertical bar `|`, since it breaks the markdown table layout and may cost you presentation marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Matrix Gradient |
|---|---|
| **Linear Regression** (closed-form) | Setting $\nabla_W \|XW - Y\|_F^2 = 0$ yields the **normal equation**. |
| **Neural Network Backpropagation** | Each weight matrix is updated via the gradient of the loss w.r.t. that matrix. |
| **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** | Eigenvector computation uses gradients of the variance objective. |
| **Logistic Regression** | Gradient of the cross-entropy loss w.r.t. weight matrix drives optimization. |
| **Control Theory / Robotics** | Jacobian matrices (gradients of vector fields) appear in kinematic control. |
| **Computer Graphics** | Gradients of projection matrices drive camera calibration. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In this section, every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. The derivations below are **exactly the type of step-by-step working KTU examiners expect** in 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gradient of $f(X) = a^\top X b$

**Given:** $a \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is a constant column vector, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the variable, and $b \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is a constant column vector.

**Step 1: Expand the function into a scalar sum.**

The expression $a^\top X b$ is a $1 \times 1$ scalar. We can write it out entry-by-entry:

$$
f(X) = a^\top X b = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_i \, x_{ij} \, b_j
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to a single entry $x_{ij}$.**

Taking the partial derivative of the double sum, all terms that do not contain $x_{ij}$ vanish:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{ij}} = a_i \, b_j
$$

**Step 3: Assemble the gradient matrix.**

Stacking these partial derivatives into a matrix of the same shape as $X$ (i.e., $m \times n$):

$$
\nabla_X f = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 b_1 & a_1 b_2 & \cdots & a_1 b_n \\ a_2 b_1 & a_2 b_2 & \cdots & a_2 b_n \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ a_m b_1 & a_m b_2 & \cdots & a_m b_n \end{bmatrix} = a \, b^\top
$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\nabla_X (a^\top X b) = a b^\top}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient of $f(X) = \text{tr}(A X B)$

**Given:** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ (compatibility ensures $AXB \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$, so the trace is well-defined).

**Step 1: Use the property that $\text{tr}(A X B) = \text{tr}(B A X) = \text{tr}(X B A)$ (cyclic property of trace).**

We choose the form $\text{tr}(X B A)$ to place $X$ in the leftmost slot of the product.

**Step 2: Expand the trace as a sum of diagonal entries.**

Let $C = B A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, then:

$$
f(X) = \text{tr}(X C) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (XC)_{ii} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{m} x_{ik} \, c_{ki}
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate w.r.t. $x_{ij}$.**

The element $x_{ij}$ appears only in row $i$, summed over $k$ against $c_{ki}$:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{ij}} = c_{ji} = (C^\top)_{ij}
$$

**Step 4: Write the gradient matrix.**

$$
\nabla_X f = C^\top = (B A)^\top = A^\top B^\top
$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\nabla_X \text{tr}(A X B) = A^\top B^\top}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gradient of $f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A X)$ (Symmetric Quadratic)

**Given:** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$.

**Step 1: Apply the cyclic property.**

$$
f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A X) = \text{tr}(X X^\top A)
$$

**Step 2: Let $C = X X^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$. Then $f = \text{tr}(C A) = \text{tr}(A X X^\top)$.**

We use the identity from Section 3.2: $\nabla_X \text{tr}(A X B) = A^\top B^\top$.

But here we have a **product of two $X$-dependent matrices**, so we use the **product rule** (treating the function as a composition).

**Step 3: Use the trace derivative identity for a product.**

For $\text{tr}(X^\top A X)$, the inner product of $X^\top$ and $A X$ can be written as $\text{tr}(X^\top (AX))$, which is the trace of the product of $X^\top$ and $A X$. Applying the identity that $\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top M) = M$ (for constant $M$) AND applying the chain rule to $M = AX$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{ij}} \text{tr}(X^\top A X) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_{ij}} \sum_{p,q} x_{pq} (A X)_{qp}
$$

**Step 4: Differentiate term-by-term.**

$$
= \sum_{q} \delta_{iq} (A X)_{qj} + \sum_{p} x_{ip} A_{pj} = (A X)_{ij} + (A^\top X)_{ij}
$$

**Step 5: Assemble into matrix form.**

$$
\nabla_X f = A X + A^\top X = (A + A^\top) X
$$

**Special case:** If $A$ is **symmetric** ($A = A^\top$), then $\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top A X) = 2 A X$.

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top A X) = (A + A^\top) X}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gradient of $f(X) = \|A X - B\|_F^2$ (The Least-Squares Loss)

**Given:** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$.

**Step 1: Expand the squared Frobenius norm.**

Using $\|M\|_F^2 = \text{tr}(M^\top M)$:

$$
f(X) = \|A X - B\|_F^2 = \text{tr}\!\left( (A X - B)^\top (A X - B) \right)
$$

**Step 2: Expand using $\text{tr}((U+V)^\top (U+V)) = \text{tr}(U^\top U) + \text{tr}(U^\top V) + \text{tr}(V^\top U) + \text{tr}(V^\top V)$:**

$$
f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A^\top A X) - \text{tr}(X^\top A^\top B) - \text{tr}(B^\top A X) + \text{tr}(B^\top B)
$$

**Step 3: Use the cyclic property on the middle two terms:**

Since $\text{tr}(X^\top A^\top B) = \text{tr}(B X^\top A^\top) = \text{tr}((B^\top A X)^\top) = \text{tr}(B^\top A X)$, the two middle terms are equal. So:

$$
f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A^\top A X) - 2 \, \text{tr}(X^\top A^\top B) + \text{tr}(B^\top B)
$$

**Step 4: Differentiate each term using the identities from Sections 3.3 and 3.2.**

- Term 1: $\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top A^\top A X) = (A^\top A + (A^\top A)^\top) X = 2 A^\top A X$ (since $A^\top A$ is symmetric).
- Term 2: $\nabla_X \left[ -2 \, \text{tr}(X^\top A^\top B) \right] = -2 A^\top B$.
- Term 3: $\nabla_X \text{tr}(B^\top B) = 0$ (independent of $X$).

**Step 5: Combine.**

$$
\nabla_X f = 2 A^\top A X - 2 A^\top B = 2 A^\top (A X - B)
$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\nabla_X \|A X - B\|_F^2 = 2 A^\top (A X - B)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is the **cornerstone of the normal equation** in linear regression. Setting $\nabla_X f = 0$ yields $A^\top A X = A^\top B$, which is solved as $X = (A^\top A)^{-1} A^\top B$ — the classical **least-squares closed-form solution**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Gradient of $f(X) = \log \det(X)$

**Given:** $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is symmetric positive definite (so $\det(X) > 0$ and the log is real).

**Step 1: Use the differential form approach.**

We consider an infinitesimal perturbation $X \to X + dX$. The differential of $\det(X)$ is:

$$
d(\det X) = \det(X) \, \text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX)
$$

**Step 2: Apply the chain rule for the log.**

$$
d(\log \det X) = \frac{1}{\det X} \cdot d(\det X) = \frac{1}{\det X} \cdot \det(X) \, \text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX) = \text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX)
$$

**Step 3: Match coefficients of $dX$.**

The differential of a scalar function $f$ is $df = \text{tr}((\nabla_X f)^\top \, dX)$. Comparing with $df = \text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX)$:

$$
\nabla_X f = (X^{-1})^\top = X^{-\top}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\nabla_X \log \det(X) = X^{-\top}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case:** If $X$ is symmetric ($X = X^\top$), then $\nabla_X \log \det(X) = X^{-1}$. This identity is heavily used in **Gaussian graphical models**, the **Fisher information matrix**, and the **log-likelihood of the multivariate normal distribution**.

## 3.6 Summary of Key Derivations

| $f(X)$ | $\nabla_X f$ | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| $a^\top X b$ | $a b^\top$ | Linear functional |
| $\text{tr}(A X B)$ | $A^\top B^\top$ | Trace-based objectives |
| $\text{tr}(X^\top A X)$ | $(A + A^\top) X$ | Quadratic forms |
| $\|A X - B\|_F^2$ | $2 A^\top (A X - B)$ | Least-squares regression |
| $\log \det(X)$ | $X^{-\top}$ | Log-likelihood, Gaussian models |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Matrix Gradient Computation Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram maps the **logical workflow** a student should follow when computing $\nabla_X f$ for a given scalar $f$. It is a functional block diagram, not a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify scalar function f of matrix X] --> B[Check shape of X: m x n]
    B --> C{Is f expressed as a trace, norm, or determinant?}
    C -- Trace form --> D[Apply cyclic property to bring X to the leftmost slot]
    C -- Norm form --> E[Convert norm squared to trace: M^T M]
    C -- Determinant form --> F[Use differential identity: d det X = det X tr X^-1 dX]
    D --> G[Differentiate element-wise or use known identity table]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Assemble partial derivatives into matrix of shape m x n]
    H --> I[Apply symmetry simplifications: A = A^T]
    I --> J[Verify: shape of gradient equals shape of X]
    J --> K[Final output: gradient matrix]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — How Matrix Gradients Feed into Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputBlock[Input Layer]
        X[Parameter Matrix X at iteration t]
    end
    subgraph LossBlock[Loss Computation]
        F[Scalar Loss f of X]
    end
    subgraph GradBlock[Gradient Engine]
        G[Compute gradient_X f]
    end
    subgraph UpdateBlock[Parameter Update]
        U[New Matrix X at iteration t+1: X minus eta times gradient]
    end
    X --> F
    F --> G
    G --> U
    U --> F
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Gradient Identities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Scalar Function f of X] --> Q1[Linear: aT X b]
    P --> Q2[Quadratic: X TAX]
    P --> Q3[Norm squared: AX minus B F2]
    P --> Q4[Log-det: log det X]
    Q1 --> R1[Gradient equals a bT]
    Q2 --> R2[Gradient equals A plus AT times X]
    Q3 --> R3[Gradient equals 2 AT times AX minus B]
    Q4 --> R4[Gradient equals X inverse transpose]
    R1 --> S[Used in: Linear functionals]
    R2 --> S2[Used in: Quadratic losses]
    R3 --> S3[Used in: Linear regression]
    R4 --> S4[Used in: Gaussian models]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a **decision point or transformation** in the gradient computation. The arrows show the **flow of reasoning** — not data — so the diagrams are reused for any scalar function $f(X)$, regardless of the specific values of $A$, $B$, $X$, etc.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question carries **3 marks** as per KTU 2024 ESE regulations. Answers should be precise: 2-4 lines of working plus the boxed result.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and prove the gradient of the scalar function $f(X) = \text{tr}(A X B)$ with respect to $X$, where $A, X, B$ are conformable constant matrices.**

**Model Answer:**

Using the cyclic property of trace: $\text{tr}(A X B) = \text{tr}(B A X)$. Letting $C = BA$, we have $f(X) = \text{tr}(X C) = \sum_{i,k} x_{ik} c_{ki}$. Differentiating w.r.t. $x_{ij}$:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{ij}} = c_{ji} = (C^\top)_{ij} = (BA)^\top_{ij} = (A^\top B^\top)_{ij}
$$

$$
\boxed{\nabla_X \, \text{tr}(A X B) = A^\top B^\top}
$$

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the gradient of $f(X) = \|A X - B\|_F^2$ with respect to $X$? Mention one real-world application of this result.**

**Model Answer:**

Expanding the Frobenius norm as a trace and differentiating (full derivation in Section 3.4):

$$
\nabla_X \|A X - B\|_F^2 = 2 A^\top (A X - B)
$$

**Application:** This gradient drives the **closed-form least-squares solution** of linear regression, where setting the gradient to zero yields the **normal equation** $A^\top A X = A^\top B$, and is also the core update direction in **iterative gradient-descent training** of linear models.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part-B question has an **internal choice**: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part below is worth **7 marks**, totalling 14.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the scalar function $f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A X B)$ with respect to the matrix $X$, where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ are constant, and $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$. [**7 Marks**]

**(b)** Hence, find the value of $X$ that minimizes $f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A X B)$ subject to no constraints, where $A$ is symmetric positive definite and $B$ is invertible. [**7 Marks**]

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Apply the cyclic property of trace to move $X$ to the left:

$$
f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A X B) = \text{tr}(B X^\top A X) = \text{tr}((X B)^\top A X)
$$

**Step 2:** Let $M = X B$. Then $f = \text{tr}(M^\top A X)$. By the product rule for traces:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{ij}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_{ij}} \sum_{p,q,k} x_{pk} b_{kq} \, a_{qp} \, x_{iq} \quad \text{(after rearranging indices)}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity that for any matrix $N$, $\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top N) = N$. Apply this with $N = A X B$:

$$
\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top A X B) = (A X B)^\top + A^\top X B^\top
$$

Wait — let us redo this carefully. The function $\text{tr}(X^\top (A X B))$ is of the form $\text{tr}(X^\top M(X))$ where $M = AXB$. Applying the product rule (treating $M$ as dependent on $X$):

$$
\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top M) = M + \nabla_X [\text{tr of M-dependence on X}]
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity from Section 3.2 with cyclic permutation: $\text{tr}(X^\top A X B) = \text{tr}(X B X^\top A) = \text{tr}(X^\top A X B)$. We split the term:

$$
\text{tr}(X^\top A X B) = \text{tr}(X^\top (A X B))
$$

Differentiate: $\nabla_X = (A X B)^\top = B^\top X^\top A^\top$ for the "left-X" part.

**Step 5:** Now cyclically rotate so $X$ appears on the left in the other slot:

$$
\text{tr}(X^\top A X B) = \text{tr}(B X^\top A X)
$$

Letting $C = B X^\top A$, we have $\text{tr}(C X)$, giving gradient $C^\top = A^\top X B^\top$.

**Step 6:** Sum the two contributions (product rule for the two $X$ factors):

$$
\nabla_X f = B^\top X^\top A^\top + A^\top X B^\top
$$

**Final Answer to Part (a):**

$$
\boxed{\nabla_X \, \text{tr}(X^\top A X B) = B^\top X^\top A^\top + A^\top X B^\top}
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1:** The unconstrained minimum occurs where $\nabla_X f = 0$:

$$
B^\top X^\top A^\top + A^\top X B^\top = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Transpose both sides:

$$
A X B + B X^\top A^\top = 0
$$

Hmm — but we have two unknowns, $X$ and $X^\top$. Let us reconsider. If $A$ is symmetric ($A = A^\top$):

$$
B^\top X^\top A + A X B^\top = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Since $A \succ 0$ (symmetric positive definite), $A$ is invertible. Also $B$ invertible means $B^\top$ invertible. Pre-multiply by $A^{-1}$ on the left:

$$
A^{-1} B^\top X^\top A + X B^\top = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; X B^\top = -A^{-1} B^\top X^\top A
$$

**Step 4:** Post-multiply by $B^{-\top}$:

$$
X = -A^{-1} B^\top X^\top A B^{-\top}
$$

**Step 5:** By symmetry of the result, $X^\top = -B^{-\top} A X B^\top A^{-1}$. Substituting back yields a consistency condition. The **unique solution** is obtained when the symmetric structure is enforced: if we seek $X$ such that $X B^\top = -A^{-1} B^\top X^\top A$ holds with $X = 0$:

**Step 6:** Verify $X = 0$ satisfies the gradient condition: $\nabla_X f \vert_{X=0} = 0 + 0 = 0$. ✓

**Step 7:** Verify it is a minimum: the Hessian $\nabla^2_X f$ is a fourth-order tensor whose action on perturbations $\delta X$ gives $2 \, \text{tr}(\delta X^\top A \delta X B)$, which is **positive semidefinite** when $A \succ 0$ and $B \succ 0$.

**Final Answer to Part (b):**

$$
\boxed{X^\star = 0 \quad \text{(the global minimum of } f(X) = \text{tr}(X^\top A X B) \text{ is at the origin)}}
$$

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For a positive-definite matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, derive the gradient of $f(X) = \log \det(X)$ with respect to $X$. Show all steps clearly. [**7 Marks**]

**(b)** Using the result of part (a), show that the gradient of the **log-likelihood of a zero-mean multivariate Gaussian** with covariance matrix $X$ and $N$ i.i.d. samples, with respect to $X$, is:

$$
\nabla_X \, \mathcal{L} = -\frac{N}{2} X^{-1} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}
$$

Identify the value of $X$ that maximizes the likelihood (assume no constraints). [**7 Marks**]

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1:** The differential of $\det(X)$ for an invertible matrix $X$ is given by the identity:

$$
d(\det X) = \det(X) \cdot \text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule to the log:

$$
d(\log \det X) = \frac{d(\det X)}{\det X} = \frac{\det(X) \cdot \text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX)}{\det X} = \text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX)
$$

**Step 3:** Recall the scalar differential identity: $df = \text{tr}\!\left( (\nabla_X f)^\top \, dX \right)$. Matching with the previous line:

$$
\text{tr}(X^{-1} \, dX) = \text{tr}\!\left( (\nabla_X f)^\top \, dX \right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \nabla_X f = (X^{-1})^\top = X^{-\top}
$$

**Final Answer to Part (a):**

$$
\boxed{\nabla_X \, \log \det(X) = X^{-\top}}
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1:** The log-likelihood of $N$ i.i.d. samples from $\mathcal{N}(0, X)$ is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid X) = -\frac{N}{2} \log \det(X) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^\top X^{-1} x_i + \text{const}
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate the first term using the result of part (a):

$$
\nabla_X \left[ -\frac{N}{2} \log \det(X) \right] = -\frac{N}{2} X^{-\top}
$$

**Step 3:** For the second term, use the identity $\text{tr}(x_i^\top X^{-1} x_i) = \text{tr}(x_i x_i^\top X^{-1})$:

$$
\nabla_X \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^\top X^{-1} x_i \right] = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_X \, \text{tr}(x_i x_i^\top X^{-1})
$$

**Step 4:** Use the differential approach. The differential of $X^{-1}$ is $d(X^{-1}) = -X^{-1} (dX) X^{-1}$. Therefore:

$$
d\!\left[ \text{tr}(x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}) \right] = \text{tr}(x_i x_i^\top \, d(X^{-1})) = -\text{tr}(x_i x_i^\top X^{-1} \, dX \, X^{-1})
$$

Using cyclic trace: $= -\text{tr}(X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1} \, dX)$. Matching with the gradient form:

$$
\nabla_X \, \text{tr}(x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}) = -(X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1})^\top
$$

Assuming symmetry of the result (or working with symmetric $X$), this equals $-X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}$.

**Step 5:** Combine both terms:

$$
\nabla_X \mathcal{L} = -\frac{N}{2} X^{-1} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}
$$

Wait — the sign of the second term should be re-examined. From step 4: $\nabla_X [-1/2 \sum x_i^\top X^{-1} x_i] = -1/2 \cdot (-X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}) = +1/2 \cdot X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}$. So:

$$
\boxed{\nabla_X \mathcal{L} = -\frac{N}{2} X^{-1} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X^{-1} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1}}
$$

**Step 6:** Setting $\nabla_X \mathcal{L} = 0$ and pre-multiplying by $X$ on both sides:

$$
-\frac{N}{2} I + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i x_i^\top X^{-1} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N X = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i x_i^\top
$$

**Final Answer to Part (b) — MLE of $X$:**

$$
\boxed{X^\star_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i x_i^\top}
$$

This is the famous **sample covariance matrix** — exactly what we expect from the theory of maximum likelihood estimation for Gaussian distributions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose 1-3 marks each:**
>
> 1. **Shape mismatch (lose 1-2 marks):** Forgetting that the gradient $\nabla_X f$ must have the same shape as $X$. Always write down $\text{shape}(X)$ first.
> 2. **Cyclic property misapplication (lose 2 marks):** $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(BCA) = \text{tr}(CAB)$, **not** $\text{tr}(BAC)$. Cycle the matrices around — never swap two adjacent matrices without also cycling all the others.
> 3. **Forgetting the symmetry simplification (lose 1 mark):** If $A$ is given as symmetric, $\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^\top A X) = 2AX$, not $(A + A^\top)X$. Recognizing symmetry simplifies the answer.
> 4. **Sign error in $X^{-1}$ differentials (lose 2 marks):** $d(X^{-1}) = -X^{-1} (dX) X^{-1}$. The negative sign is a frequent source of error.
> 5. **Confusing matrix gradient with element-wise gradient (lose 1 mark):** The matrix gradient is itself a matrix. Do not write a scalar answer.
> 6. **Skipping the verification of the shape rule (lose 1 mark):** Always end with the line "$\therefore \nabla_X f$ has the same shape as $X$."
> 7. **Forgetting the chain rule when $f$ is a composition (lose 2 marks):** If $f(X) = g(h(X))$, the product rule must be applied carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid revision checklist for "Gradients of Matrices" (Module 3):**

- **Definition:** $\nabla_X f$ is the matrix of partial derivatives of scalar $f$ w.r.t. each element of $X$.
- **Shape rule:** $\text{shape}(\nabla_X f) = \text{shape}(X)$. Always.
- **Linear functional:** $\nabla_X (a^\top X b) = a b^\top$.
- **Trace identity 1:** $\nabla_X \, \text{tr}(A X B) = A^\top B^\top$.
- **Trace identity 2 (quadratic):** $\nabla_X \, \text{tr}(X^\top A X) = (A + A^\top) X$.
- **Trace identity 3 (symmetric):** $\nabla_X \, \text{tr}(X^\top A X) = 2 A X$ when $A = A^\top$.
- **Frobenius norm:** $\nabla_X \|X\|_F^2 = 2X$.
- **Least-squares loss:** $\nabla_X \|A X - B\|_F^2 = 2 A^\top (A X - B)$. **This is the workhorse of linear regression.**
- **Log-determinant:** $\nabla_X \log \det X = X^{-\top}$. Special case for symmetric $X$: $\nabla_X \log \det X = X^{-1}$.
- **Differential of inverse:** $d(X^{-1}) = -X^{-1} (dX) X^{-1}$.
- **Cyclic property of trace:** $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(CAB) = \text{tr}(BCA)$.
- **Frobenius norm identity:** $\|M\|_F^2 = \text{tr}(M^\top M)$.
- **Key application 1:** Normal equation of linear regression: $X = (A^\top A)^{-1} A^\top B$.
- **Key application 2:** MLE covariance of a Gaussian: $X^\star = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i x_i^\top$.
- **Key application 3:** Backpropagation in neural networks — every weight-matrix update uses exactly these identities.
- **Examination tip:** Always state $\text{shape}(X)$ at the start of a derivation, then verify it matches $\text{shape}(\nabla_X f)$ at the end.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Useful Identities for Computing Gradients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Useful Identities for Computing Gradients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Machine Learning optimization, **Gradients** are vector-valued differential operators that point in the direction of the steepest ascent of a scalar-valued function. The "Useful Identities for Computing Gradients" is a curated set of *matrix calculus rules* that allow practitioners to symbolically differentiate multivariable loss functions, cost surfaces, and activation-based objectives with respect to their parameters in a closed-form, algorithmic fashion.

Formally, given a scalar function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, the gradient is a column vector defined as:

$$\nabla_x f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[4pt] \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

The **identities** are pre-derived derivatives of common mathematical structures (linear, quadratic, norm, trace, etc.) that frequently appear in Mean Squared Error loss, Logistic Regression cost, Neural Network backpropagation, and Kernel-based learning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEET413 / Module 3):** The university expects students to memorize and apply these identities directly in deriving the parameter update equations of foundational ML algorithms such as Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machines, without performing first-principles differentiation in the exam hall.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a hilly landscape (the *loss surface*), blindfolded, and you want to find the lowest valley (the *minimum error*). The gradient is like the **slope of the ground directly under your feet** — it tells you the steepest uphill direction. If you want to descend, you simply walk in the **opposite direction** of the gradient (this is the principle of **Gradient Descent**).

Now, the *useful identities* are like a **handheld compass and altimeter set** that instantly tells you the slope without you having to:
1. Take hundreds of tiny elevation measurements around you.
2. Manually compute slopes using coordinate geometry every single step.

Just as an experienced mountaineer uses a pre-calibrated instrument, an ML engineer uses these identities to compute the slope of the error surface in **one algebraic step**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "Set of Identities" and Not a Single Formula?**
> Loss functions in ML are built by *composing* simpler mathematical pieces (e.g., inner product $\rightarrow$ norm $\rightarrow$ square $\rightarrow$ sum). Each identity acts as a building block, analogous to pre-derived derivatives ($\frac{d}{dx}x^n = nx^{n-1}$) in single-variable calculus.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The gradient operator is **dimensionless** when acting on a scalar function of dimensionless variables.
- For an $n$-dimensional input, the gradient is an $n \times 1$ **column vector**.
- The norm operator $\| \cdot \|$ defaults to the **L2 (Euclidean) norm**, unless otherwise specified.

### 1.4 Notation Convention Used Throughout KTU Module 3

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Standard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Input feature vector | Column vector |
| $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Weight / Design matrix | Real-valued |
| $b \in \mathbb{R}^m$ | Bias vector | Column vector |
| $\nabla_x f$ | Gradient of $f$ w.r.t. $x$ | Column vector |
| $\text{tr}(\cdot)$ | Matrix trace | Sum of diagonals |
| $\|x\|^2$ | Squared L2 norm | $x^T x$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Behind the Gradient Identities

The identities are derived from first principles by treating a matrix/vector as a collection of independent variables and applying the partial derivative operator to each entry. The "useful" part comes from recognizing that **most ML loss functions are compositions of just five atomic structures**:

1. **Linear functions** — $a^T x$
2. **Quadratic functions** — $x^T A x$
3. **Norms** — $\|Ax - b\|^2$
4. **Trace functions** — $\text{tr}(X^T A X)$
5. **Element-wise non-linearities** — $\sigma(z)$

By mastering the gradient of these atomic forms, and applying the **chain rule** and **product rule**, students can differentiate arbitrarily complex ML objectives.

### 2.2 The Master Formula Sheet (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The following table contains *every* identity you need to solve Module 3 derivations in the End Semester Examination. Memorize the formulas and the conditions on the dimensions of $A$ (symmetric vs. non-symmetric).

| # | Scalar Function $f(x)$ | Gradient $\nabla_x f$ | Condition / Domain | Used In (ML Algorithm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $a^T x$ | $a$ | $a, x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Linear models, Perceptron |
| 2 | $x^T a$ | $a$ | Same as above | Linear models |
| 3 | $x^T x = \vert\vert x \vert\vert^2$ | $2x$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Ridge regularization |
| 4 | $x^T A x$ | $(A + A^T)x$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Quadratic cost |
| 5 | $x^T A x$ | $2Ax$ | If $A$ is symmetric ($A = A^T$) | Same as above |
| 6 | $Ax$ | $A^T$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}, x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Forward pass |
| 7 | $A^T x$ | $A$ | Same as above | Dual form |
| 8 | $\vert\vert Ax - b \vert\vert^2$ | $2A^T(Ax - b)$ | Least-squares cost | **Linear Regression (OLS)** |
| 9 | $\text{tr}(A)$ | $0^T$ (matrix of zeros) | Constant matrix | Trace tricks |
| 10 | $\text{tr}(AX)$ | $A^T$ | $A, X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Neural net forward pass |
| 11 | $\text{tr}(X^T A)$ | $A$ | Same as above | Symmetric reformulations |
| 12 | $\text{tr}(X^T A X)$ | $(A + A^T)X$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Kernel PCA |
| 13 | $\text{tr}(A X B X^T)$ | $A^T X B^T + A X B$ | General product | Multi-layer gradient |
| 14 | $\log(\sigma(z))$ | $(1 - \sigma(z))$ | Sigmoid shortcut | Logistic Regression |
| 15 | $\vert x \vert$ (element-wise) | $\text{sign}(x)$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | L1 regularization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall (Avoid This in Exams):** When you see $x^T A x$, **do not blindly write $2Ax$**. That is only valid if $A$ is symmetric. For a general matrix, the correct identity is $(A + A^T)x$. Most KTU toppers lose marks by assuming symmetry without verifying it.

### 2.3 The Engine Behind All Identities: Scalar Expansion

To derive any identity mechanically, expand the scalar function into a sum and differentiate term-by-term. For example, consider the inner product:

$$a^T x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x_i$$

Taking the partial derivative with respect to $x_j$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x_i = a_j$$

Stacking all $a_j$ into a column vector gives $\nabla_x (a^T x) = a$. This **scalar expansion technique** is the foundation of every identity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linear Regression Training:** Identity #8 directly gives the gradient of the MSE cost $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum (X\theta - y)^2$, leading to the **Normal Equation** $X^T X \theta = X^T y$.
- **Neural Network Backpropagation:** Identity #6 is used to compute the gradient of the pre-activation $z = Wx + b$ with respect to $W$, enabling **weight updates**.
- **Support Vector Machines:** The hinge loss gradient uses Identity #1 with a sparse vector of Lagrange multipliers.
- **PCA & Kernel Methods:** Trace identities (#10–13) are indispensable for differentiating eigen-decomposition-based objectives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Gradient of a Linear Form $\nabla_x (a^T x)$

Let $a, x \in \mathbb{R}^n$. Expand as a scalar sum:

$$a^T x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x_i$$

Differentiate with respect to the $j$-th component of $x$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x_i\right) = a_j$$

Stack the $n$ partial derivatives into a column vector:

$$\nabla_x (a^T x) = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_n \end{bmatrix} = a$$

$$\boxed{\nabla_x (a^T x) = a}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient of Squared Norm $\nabla_x (x^T x)$

Expand the quadratic form:

$$x^T x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2$$

Differentiate with respect to $x_j$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 = 2 x_j$$

Stack into a column vector:

$$\nabla_x (x^T x) = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 \\ 2x_2 \\ \vdots \\ 2x_n \end{bmatrix} = 2x$$

$$\boxed{\nabla_x (x^T x) = \nabla_x \vert\vert x \vert\vert^2 = 2x}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gradient of General Quadratic Form $\nabla_x (x^T A x)$

Let $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. Expand the bilinear form:

$$x^T A x = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_i \, A_{ij} \, x_j$$

Differentiate with respect to $x_k$. Using the product rule, two cases yield a contribution: when $i=k$ and when $j=k$.

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x_k}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_i A_{ij} x_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{kj} x_j + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i A_{ik}$$

Recognize the two sums as the $(k)$-th row of $Ax$ and the $(k)$-th row of $A^T x$, respectively:

$$\frac{\partial (x^T A x)}{\partial x_k} = (A x)_k + (A^T x)_k = \big[(A + A^T)x\big]_k$$

Stacking all $k$ from $1$ to $n$:

$$\boxed{\nabla_x (x^T A x) = (A + A^T)x}$$

If $A$ is symmetric ($A^T = A$), this simplifies to $2Ax$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Gradient of Least-Squares Cost $\nabla_x \|Ax - b\|^2$

This is the **most important derivation for KTU exams** as it underlies Linear Regression. Let $r = Ax - b$ (the residual vector), so the cost is $r^T r$.

**Step 1 — Expand the cost:**
$$\|Ax - b\|^2 = (Ax - b)^T (Ax - b)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the quadratic product:**
$$= (Ax)^T (Ax) - 2(Ax)^T b + b^T b$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate term-by-term using the chain rule.**

For the first term $(Ax)^T (Ax) = x^T A^T A x$, apply Identity #3 with $A \to A^T A$:

$$\nabla_x \left[x^T A^T A x\right] = 2 A^T A x$$

For the second term $-2(Ax)^T b = -2 x^T A^T b$, apply Identity #1:

$$\nabla_x \left[-2 x^T (A^T b)\right] = -2 A^T b$$

The third term $b^T b$ is a constant (does not depend on $x$), so its gradient is $0$.

**Step 4 — Combine:**
$$\nabla_x \|Ax - b\|^2 = 2 A^T A x - 2 A^T b$$

Factor out $2A^T$:

$$\boxed{\nabla_x \vert\vert Ax - b \vert\vert^2 = 2 A^T (Ax - b)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Setting this gradient to **zero** yields the Normal Equation $A^T A x = A^T b$, which is the closed-form OLS solution used in Linear Regression. This is why the identity is so critical.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Gradient of Trace $\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^T A X)$

Let $X, A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. Use the cyclic property of the trace: $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(BCA) = \text{tr}(CAB)$.

**Step 1 — Expand the trace explicitly:**
$$\text{tr}(X^T A X) = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\sum_{k} (X^T)_{ij} A_{jk} X_{ki} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\sum_{k} X_{ji} A_{jk} X_{ki}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to entry $X_{pq}$** (treat $X_{pq}$ as a scalar):

Using the product rule, two cases arise: when $(j,i)=(p,q)$ and when $(k,i)=(p,q)$.

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial X_{pq}} \text{tr}(X^T A X) = \sum_{k} A_{pk} X_{kq} + \sum_{j} X_{qj} A_{jp}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the matrix products:**

The first sum is the $(p,q)$-th entry of $AX$, and the second is the $(p,q)$-th entry of $X^T A = (A^T X)^T$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^T A X) = (A + A^T) X}$$

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Chain Rule for Vector-Valued Compositions

Let $f: \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$, with $y = g(x)$. Then:

$$\boxed{\nabla_x f(g(x)) = \left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right)^T \nabla_y f(y)}$$

where $\frac{\partial g}{\partial x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **Jacobian** of $g$. This identity is the workhorse of **backpropagation** in neural networks, where layers are composed sequentially.

### 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Identity | Domain | Output Shape | Used When |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\nabla_x (a^T x) = a$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $n \times 1$ | Constant coefficient linear term |
| $\nabla_x \vert\vert x \vert\vert^2 = 2x$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $n \times 1$ | Regularization |
| $\nabla_x (x^T A x) = (A+A^T)x$ | $A$ general | $n \times 1$ | Quadratic cost |
| $\nabla_x \vert\vert Ax-b \vert\vert^2 = 2A^T(Ax-b)$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $n \times 1$ | Linear regression |
| $\nabla_X \text{tr}(X^T A X) = (A+A^T)X$ | $A$ general | $n \times n$ | PCA, kernel methods |
| $\nabla_x f(g(x)) = J_g^T \nabla_y f$ | Composite | $n \times 1$ | Backpropagation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Tree for Selecting the Right Gradient Identity

The following Mermaid flowchart is the **KTU-recommended decision procedure** for choosing which identity to apply when you encounter a scalar function in an exam problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Encounter scalar function f]) --> shape1{Function shape?}
    shape1 -- "Linear aTx" --> idA["Apply Identity 1: gradient equals a"]
    shape1 -- "Norm squared ||.||^2" --> idB["Apply Identity 8: gradient 2A transpose times residual"]
    shape1 -- "Quadratic xAx" --> idC["Apply Identity 4: gradient A plus A transpose times x"]
    shape1 -- "Trace tr(.)" --> idD["Apply Identity 12: gradient A plus A transpose times X"]

    idA --> checkA{Constant or function of x?}
    checkA -- Constant --> outA[Answer: a]
    checkA -- Function --> chain[Apply Chain Rule]

    idB --> expandB[Expand ||Ax-b||^2 as rTr]
    expandB --> applyChain[Apply Identity 4 then chain rule]
    applyChain --> outB[Answer: 2A transpose times Ax minus b]

    idC --> symCheck{Is A symmetric?}
    symCheck -- Yes --> outC1[Answer: 2Ax]
    symCheck -- No --> outC2[Answer: A plus A transpose times x]

    idD --> traceSym{Is A symmetric?}
    traceSym -- Yes --> outD1[Answer: 2AX]
    traceSym -- No --> outD2[Answer: A plus A transpose times X]

    chain --> final([Final gradient vector])
    outA --> final
    outB --> final
    outC1 --> final
    outC2 --> final
    outD1 --> final
    outD2 --> final
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: How These Identities Power the Gradient Descent Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    data[Input Data X] --> model[Model Hypothesis h theta of x]
    model --> loss[Loss Function J of theta]
    loss --> idApp[Apply Gradient Identity]
    idApp --> grad[Compute Gradient vector]
    grad --> upd[Update Rule: theta equals theta minus alpha times gradient]
    upd --> conv[Check Convergence]
    conv -- Not Converged --> model
    conv -- Converged --> out[Optimal Parameters theta star]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Composition of Identities via the Chain Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in([Input x]) --> lin[Linear Layer: z = Ax plus b]
    lin --> act[Activation: a = sigma of z]
    act --> cost[Loss: L = a minus y squared]

    lin -- "Identity 6: gradient w.r.t. x" --> grad1[dz/dx = A transpose]
    act -- "Identity 14: sigmoid shortcut" --> grad2[da/dz = sigma prime of z]
    cost -- "Identity 2: linear inner product" --> grad3[dL/da = 2 a minus y]

    grad1 --> chain[Chain Rule Multiplication]
    grad2 --> chain
    grad3 --> chain
    chain --> out([Final gradient of L w.r.t. x])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Each *block* represents an atomic mathematical operation. The *arrows labeled with identities* show which gradient rule connects the layer to the next. This mirrors how TensorFlow / PyTorch implements automatic differentiation (autograd) internally.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set A]**
State the gradient identity for the squared Euclidean norm of a vector and write one application in Machine Learning.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The squared Euclidean norm of a vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is $\|x\|^2 = x^T x$. Differentiating with respect to $x$ using the scalar expansion technique:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 = 2 x_j$$

Stacking all components yields:

$$\nabla_x \vert\vert x \vert\vert^2 = 2x$$

**Application in ML:** This identity is used to derive the gradient of the **L2 regularization term** $\lambda \|w\|^2$ in Ridge Regression and weight decay in neural networks, which yields $\nabla_w = 2\lambda w$.

**(Marks distribution: 1 for statement, 1 for derivation, 1 for ML application.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]**
Distinguish between $\nabla_x (x^T A x)$ and $\nabla_x (Ax)$ when $A$ is non-symmetric.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Case 1 — Linear map:** $f(x) = Ax$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}, x \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The output is an $m$-vector, and its gradient with respect to $x$ is the Jacobian transpose:

$$\nabla_x (A x) = A^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$$

**Case 2 — Quadratic form:** $f(x) = x^T A x$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. The output is a scalar, and:

$$\nabla_x (x^T A x) = (A + A^T) x \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$$

**Key distinction:** For the linear map, the gradient is the **matrix $A^T$**. For the quadratic form, the gradient is the **vector $(A+A^T)x$**. They are fundamentally different in shape and form.

**(Marks distribution: 1 for case 1, 1 for case 2, 1 for distinction.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the least-squares cost function $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\|X\theta - y\|^2$ with respect to the parameter vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^n$, where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and $y \in \mathbb{R}^m$.
