### 2.1.4 Higher-Order Moments

**A. Skewness** — third standardized moment; measures **asymmetry**.

**B. Kurtosis** — fourth standardized moment; measures **tail-heaviness**.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework of Independence

### 2.2.1 Mutual Independence
A collection of events $\{A_i\}_{i=1}^n$ is **mutually independent** if for every subcollection $A_{i_1}, \dots, A_{i_k}$:

$$P\!\left(\bigcap_{j=1}^{k} A_{i_j}\right) = \prod_{j=1}^{k} P(A_{i_j})$$

### 2.2.2 Pairwise vs. Mutual Independence
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtle Pitfall:** Pairwise independence (independence of every pair) does **not** imply mutual independence. KTU examiners frequently test this with classical counterexamples (e.g., the coin-and-dice problem).

### 2.2.3 Conditional Independence
$X$ and $Y$ are **conditionally independent given $Z$** (written $X \perp\!\!\!\perp Y \mid Z$) if:

$$f_{X,Y \mid Z}(x, y \mid z) = f_{X \mid Z}(x \mid z) \cdot f_{Y \mid Z}(y \mid z)$$

This is the **structural backbone** of Bayesian Networks, Hidden Markov Models, and Naive Bayes classifiers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Statistic | Formula (Sample) | Range / Units | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Mean** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Same as $x$ | Minimizes SSE |
| **Sample Variance** | $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Squared units | **Bessel's correction** |
| **Population Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$ | Squared units | MLE estimator |
| **Std. Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Same as $x$ | Used in Z-score |
| **Median** | Middle value of sorted $x$ | Same as $x$ | Robust to outliers |
| **MAD** | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ | Same as $x$ | $\ell_1$ dispersion |
| **IQR** | $Q_3 - Q_1$ | Same as $x$ | Used in boxplots |
| **Skewness** | $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{E[(X-\mu)^3]}{\sigma^3}$ | $[-\infty, \infty]$ | $0$ = symmetric |
| **Kurtosis** | $\gamma_2 = \dfrac{E[(X-\mu)^4]}{\sigma^4}$ | $[1, \infty)$ | $3$ = Gaussian |
| **Covariance** | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | Sign = direction |
| **Pearson $\rho$** | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Linear association |
| **Independence** | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$ | Boolean relation | Full factorization |
| **Conditional Indep.** | $f(x,y \mid z) = f(x\mid z) f(y\mid z)$ | Boolean relation | Bayesian Networks |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this topic?**
> 1. **Standardization** in pipelines (`StandardScaler`) uses $\mu$ and $\sigma$ — without these, gradient descent on features with different scales diverges.
> 2. **Covariance matrix $\Sigma$** is the central object in **Multivariate Gaussian**, **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**, and **Mahalanobis distance**.
> 3. **Independence assumption** in **Naive Bayes** dramatically simplifies $P(C \mid x_1, \dots, x_n)$ from intractable to a simple product.
> 4. **Pearson correlation** is the workhorse of feature selection in high-dimensional genomics, finance, and computer vision.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Variance via the Computational Shortcut

**Problem:** Prove that
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2$$

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_i^2 - 2 x_i \bar{x} + \bar{x}^2\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 \;-\; 2\bar{x}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \;+\; n\bar{x}^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line-by-line logic:**
- Line 1: Substitute $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ into the squared term and expand.
- Line 2: Distribute the sum over the three additive terms.
- Line 3: Factor out constants $-2\bar{x}$ and $\bar{x}^2$ from the summation (since $\bar{x}$ is independent of $i$).

Now apply the definition of $\bar{x}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 \;-\; 2\bar{x} \cdot (n\bar{x}) \;+\; n\bar{x}^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 \;-\; 2n\bar{x}^2 \;+\; n\bar{x}^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 \;-\; n\bar{x}^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Finally, substitute back $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum x_i$, hence $n\bar{x}^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\left(\sum x_i\right)^2$:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2 \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Covariance from Joint Moments

**Starting definition:**
$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E\!\left[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)\right]$$

**Step 1 — Expand the product inside the expectation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cov}(X,Y) &= E\!\left[XY - X\mu_Y - Y\mu_X + \mu_X \mu_Y\right] \\
&= E[XY] \;-\; \mu_Y E[X] \;-\; \mu_X E[Y] \;+\; \mu_X \mu_Y \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the linearity of expectation and the fact that $E[X] = \mu_X$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= E[XY] \;-\; \mu_Y \mu_X \;-\; \mu_X \mu_Y \;+\; \mu_X \mu_Y \\
&= E[XY] \;-\; \mu_X \mu_Y \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the bilinear identity:**

$$\boxed{\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]}$$

**Logical Comment:** This is the **fundamental identity** tested in KTU exams. It immediately proves that **independence implies zero covariance** because if $X \perp\!\!\!\perp Y$, then $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$, forcing $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pearson Correlation $\rho$ as a Normalized Covariance

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{Var}(X+Y)$ using the bilinear property:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X+Y) &= E\!\left[(X+Y)^2\right] - \left(E[X+Y]\right)^2 \\
&= E[X^2] + 2E[XY] + E[Y^2] - (\mu_X + \mu_Y)^2 \\
&= \text{Var}(X) + 2\text{Cov}(X,Y) + \text{Var}(Y) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:**

For real random variables with finite non-zero variances, $|\text{Cov}(X,Y)| \le \sigma_X \sigma_Y$.

**Step 3 — Normalize the covariance to get the bounded correlation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(X,Y) &= \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \rho &\in [-1, +1] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\rho = +1$ implies perfect positive linear relation; $\rho = -1$ implies perfect negative linear relation; $\rho = 0$ implies **no linear** relation (but possibly non-linear dependence).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Skewness and Kurtosis as Standardized Moments

**Step 1 — Define the $k$-th central moment:**
$$\mu_k = E\!\left[(X - \mu)^k\right]$$

**Step 2 — Standardize by dividing by $\sigma^k$ (to make dimensionless):**
$$\tilde{\mu}_k = \frac{\mu_k}{\sigma^k}$$

**Step 3 — Skewness (3rd standardized moment):**
$$\gamma_1 = \frac{E[(X-\mu)^3]}{\sigma^3} = \frac{\mu_3}{\mu_2^{3/2}}$$

- $\gamma_1 > 0$: right-skewed (tail extends right).
- $\gamma_1 < 0$: left-skewed (tail extends left).
- $\gamma_1 = 0$: symmetric (e.g., Gaussian, Uniform).

**Step 4 — Excess Kurtosis (4th standardized moment minus 3):**
$$\gamma_2 = \frac{E[(X-\mu)^4]}{\sigma^4} - 3$$

- $\gamma_2 = 0$: Gaussian (mesokurtic).
- $\gamma_2 > 0$: heavy tails (leptokurtic) — e.g., Laplace, Student's $t$.
- $\gamma_2 < 0$: light tails (platykurtic) — e.g., Uniform.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Joint Distribution Independence Check

**Problem:** Given the joint PMF:

| $X \backslash Y$ | $0$ | $1$ |
|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $0.1$ | $0.4$ |
| $1$ | $0.3$ | $0.2$ |

Test whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

**Step 1 — Compute marginals of $X$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= 0.1 + 0.4 = 0.5 \\
P(X=1) &= 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute marginals of $Y$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Y=0) &= 0.1 + 0.3 = 0.4 \\
P(Y=1) &= 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.6 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute product of marginals at $(0,0)$:**

$$P(X=0) \cdot P(Y=0) = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.20$$

**Step 4 — Compare with joint:**

$$P(X=0, Y=0) = 0.10 \neq 0.20$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:** Since the equality fails at the cell $(0,0)$, the variables are **NOT independent**.

**Step 6 — Compute Covariance as a sanity check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= 0 \cdot 0.5 + 1 \cdot 0.5 = 0.5 \\
E[Y] &= 0 \cdot 0.4 + 1 \cdot 0.6 = 0.6 \\
E[XY] &= (0)(0)(0.1) + (0)(1)(0.4) + (1)(0)(0.3) + (1)(1)(0.2) = 0.2 \\
\text{Cov}(X,Y) &= 0.2 - (0.5)(0.6) = 0.2 - 0.3 = -0.1 \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

A **non-zero covariance** confirms dependence, consistent with Step 5.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Independence vs. Uncorrelated

| Property | Independent $X \perp\!\!\!\perp Y$ | Uncorrelated $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$ |
|---|---|---|
| Joint PDF factorization | $f_{X,Y} = f_X f_Y$ | Not required |
| $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ | ✓ Always | ✓ Always |
| $E[g(X) h(Y)] = E[g(X)] E[h(Y)]$ for all $g, h$ | ✓ Always | ✗ Not required |
| Reverse implication | Independent $\Rightarrow$ Uncorrelated | Uncorrelated $\not\Rightarrow$ Independent |
| Counterexample | — | $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$, $Y = X^2$ |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following with one example each:**
(a) Population mean and Sample mean
(b) Variance and Standard deviation

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Population Mean ($\mu$) and Sample Mean ($\bar{x}$):**
> The **population mean** is the arithmetic average of *all* members of a finite population $N$:
> $$\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$
> The **sample mean** is the arithmetic average of $n$ randomly selected members ($n \le N$):
> $$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
> **Example:** If 5 students scored 70, 80, 90, 60, 100, then $\bar{x} = 80$.

> **(b) Variance and Standard Deviation:**
> **Variance** measures the *average squared* deviation from the mean:
> $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$
> **Standard deviation** is the positive square root of variance, restoring original units:
> $$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between independent events and mutually exclusive events. Are they equivalent?**

**Model Answer:**

> Two events $A$ and $B$ are:
> - **Mutually Exclusive** if they **cannot occur simultaneously**: $A \cap B = \emptyset \Rightarrow P(A \cap B) = 0$.
> - **Independent** if the occurrence of one does **not affect the probability** of the other: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$.

> They are **NOT equivalent**. In fact, if $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive with $P(A), P(B) > 0$, then $P(A \cap B) = 0 \neq P(A) P(B) > 0$, so they are **dependent**.
>
> **Example:** Drawing a King OR a Queen from a deck — these are mutually exclusive but dependent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the following joint probability distribution of discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$, compute:
- (i) The marginals $P(X)$ and $P(Y)$.
- (ii) $E[X]$, $E[Y]$, $E[XY]$.
- (iii) $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$ and Pearson $\rho(X, Y)$.
- (iv) Comment on independence.

| $X \backslash Y$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0.12 | 0.08 | 0.10 |
| **1** | 0.18 | 0.22 | 0.12 |
| **2** | 0.06 | 0.08 | 0.04 |

**Solution:**

**(i) Marginals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= 0.12 + 0.08 + 0.10 = 0.30 \\
P(X=1) &= 0.18 + 0.22 + 0.12 = 0.52 \\
P(X=2) &= 0.06 + 0.08 + 0.04 = 0.18 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Y=0) &= 0.12 + 0.18 + 0.06 = 0.36 \\
P(Y=1) &= 0.08 + 0.22 + 0.08 = 0.38 \\
P(Y=2) &= 0.10 + 0.12 + 0.04 = 0.26 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Expectations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= 0(0.30) + 1(0.52) + 2(0.18) = 0 + 0.52 + 0.36 = 0.88 \\
E[Y] &= 0(0.36) + 1(0.38) + 2(0.26) = 0 + 0.38 + 0.52 = 0.90 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[XY] &= \sum_{x,y} xy \cdot P(x,y) \\
&= (0)(0)(0.12) + (0)(1)(0.08) + (0)(2)(0.10) \\
&\quad + (1)(0)(0.18) + (1)(1)(0.22) + (1)(2)(0.12) \\
&\quad + (2)(0)(0.06) + (2)(1)(0.08) + (2)(2)(0.04) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0.22 + 0.24 + 0 + 0.16 + 0.16 \\
&= 0.78
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Covariance and Correlation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cov}(X,Y) &= E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = 0.78 - (0.88)(0.90) = 0.78 - 0.792 = -0.012
\end{aligned}
$$

To compute $\rho$, we need the variances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X^2] &= 0(0.30) + 1(0.52) + 4(0.18) = 0.52 + 0.72 = 1.24 \\
\text{Var}(X) &= E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = 1.24 - 0.7744 = 0.4656 \\
\sigma_X &= \sqrt{0.4656} \approx 0.6824
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[Y^2] &= 0(0.36) + 1(0.38) + 4(0.26) = 0.38 + 1.04 = 1.42 \\
\text{Var}(Y) &= 1.42 - 0.81 = 0.61 \\
\sigma_Y &= \sqrt{0.61} \approx 0.7810
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(X,Y) &= \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} = \frac{-0.012}{(0.6824)(0.7810)} = \frac{-0.012}{0.5330} \approx -0.0225
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Comment on Independence:**
Since $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = -0.012 \neq 0$, the variables are **not independent** (and not even uncorrelated). However, the magnitude of the correlation is very small ($\rho \approx -0.02$), indicating a **very weak negative linear association**. They are strongly **dependent in distribution** but **weakly dependent in linear association**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the identity
$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$$
from the first principles. Hence, prove that if $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$.

**Solution:**

**Derivation from first principles:**

Starting from the definition of covariance:
$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E\!\left[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)\right]$$

Expanding the product inside the expectation operator:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cov}(X,Y) &= E\!\left[XY - X\mu_Y - Y\mu_X + \mu_X \mu_Y\right] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the linearity of expectation, $E[aZ_1 + bZ_2] = aE[Z_1] + bE[Z_2]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&= E[XY] - \mu_Y E[X] - \mu_X E[Y] + \mu_X \mu_Y \\
&= E[XY] - \mu_Y \mu_X - \mu_X \mu_Y + \mu_X \mu_Y \\
&= E[XY] - \mu_X \mu_Y \\
&= E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof that independence $\Rightarrow$ zero covariance:**

Assume $X$ and $Y$ are independent. By the definition of independence, for any functions $g, h$:

$$E[g(X) h(Y)] = E[g(X)] \cdot E[h(Y)]$$

Choosing $g(X) = X$ and $h(Y) = Y$:

$$E[XY] = E[X] \cdot E[Y]$$

Substituting into the covariance identity:
$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[X]E[Y] - E[X]E[Y] = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Converse does not hold:** The reverse implication ($\text{Cov} = 0 \Rightarrow$ Independence) is **FALSE** in general. A standard counterexample: $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and $Y = X^2$. Then $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$ but $X$ and $Y$ are highly dependent ($Y$ is a deterministic function of $X$). Examiners award **zero marks** for claiming the converse.
> 2. **Don't drop units:** In numerical problems, always state the **units** of $\sigma$, $\text{Cov}$, and $\rho$ explicitly.
> 3. **Bessel's correction:** Use $n-1$ (not $n$) in the **sample** variance. Population variance uses $N$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following higher-order statistics and explain their significance in machine learning:
(i) Skewness
(ii) Kurtosis

**Solution:**

> **(i) Skewness ($\gamma_1$)** is the **third standardized central moment** of a random variable $X$:
> $$\gamma_1 = \frac{E\!\left[(X - \mu)^3\right]}{\sigma^3}$$
> It measures the **asymmetry** of a distribution about its mean.
> - $\gamma_1 = 0$: symmetric (Gaussian, Uniform).
> - $\gamma_1 > 0$: right-skewed (e.g., log-normal, exponential).
> - $\gamma_1 < 0$: left-skewed (e.g., days-until-deadline distribution).
>
> **ML Significance:** Many ML algorithms (Linear Regression, LDA, GMM) assume symmetric (Gaussian-like) features. High skewness violates this — applying a **log transform** or **Box-Cox transform** is a common remediation. Tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost) are robust to skewness.

> **(ii) Kurtosis ($\gamma_2$)** is the **fourth standardized central moment** of a random variable $X$, often expressed as **excess kurtosis**:
> $$\gamma_2 = \frac{E\!\left[(X - \mu)^4\right]}{\sigma^4} - 3$$
> It measures the **tailedness** (or "peakedness") of a distribution relative to a Gaussian.
> - $\gamma_2 = 0$: mesokurtic (Gaussian).
> - $\gamma_2 > 0$: leptokurtic (heavy tails — Student's $t$, Laplace).
> - $\gamma_2 < 0$: platykurtic (light tails — Uniform).
>
> **ML Significance:** High kurtosis indicates **frequent extreme outliers**, which can destabilize gradient descent. Robust losses (Huber loss, MAE) are preferred over MSE for heavy-tailed data. Financial returns exhibit $\gamma_2 \gg 0$, motivating **Value-at-Risk (VaR)** modeling.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** For two fair dice (one red, one green) rolled simultaneously, consider the events:
- $A$: Sum of dice is 7.
- $B$: Red die shows an even number.
- $C$: Green die shows a 3.

Compute $P(A)$, $P(B)$, $P(C)$, $P(A \cap B)$, $P(A \cap C)$, $P(A \cap B \cap C)$, and test:
- (i) Are $A$ and $B$ independent?
- (ii) Are $A$ and $C$ independent?
- (iii) Are $A$, $B$, $C$ mutually independent?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Enumerate the sample space:**
$|\Omega| = 6 \times 6 = 36$ equally likely outcomes.

**Step 2 — Event $A$ (sum = 7):**
Pairs $(r, g)$ summing to 7: $\{(1,6),(2,5),(3,4),(4,3),(5,2),(6,1)\}$.
$$P(A) = \frac{6}{36} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Event $B$ (red die even):**
$r \in \{2, 4, 6\}$, so $|B| = 3 \times 6 = 18$.
$$P(B) = \frac{18}{36} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Event $C$ (green die = 3):**
$g = 3$, so $|C| = 6$.
$$P(C) = \frac{6}{36} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Event $A \cap B$ (sum 7 AND red even):**
$(r, g) \in \{(2,5), (4,3), (6,1)\}$, so $|A \cap B| = 3$.
$$P(A \cap B) = \frac{3}{36} = \frac{1}{12}$$

**Step 6 — Event $A \cap C$ (sum 7 AND green = 3):**
$(r, g) \in \{(4, 3)\}$, so $|A \cap C| = 1$.
$$P(A \cap C) = \frac{1}{36}$$

**Step 7 — Event $A \cap B \cap C$ (sum 7, red even, green = 3):**
$(r, g) \in \{(4, 3)\}$, so $|A \cap B \cap C| = 1$.
$$P(A \cap B \cap C) = \frac{1}{36}$$

**Step 8 — Independence Tests:**

> **(i) Test $A \perp\!\!\!\perp B$:**
> $P(A) \cdot P(B) = \dfrac{1}{6} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{12} = P(A \cap B)$.
> ✅ **$A$ and $B$ are independent.**

> **(ii) Test $A \perp\!\!\!\perp C$:**
> $P(A) \cdot P(C) = \dfrac{1}{6} \times \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{36} = P(A \cap C)$.
> ✅ **$A$ and $C$ are independent.**

> **(iii) Test mutual independence of $A$, $B$, $C$:**
> $P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C) = \dfrac{1}{6} \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{72}$
> $P(A \cap B \cap C) = \dfrac{1}{36}$
> Since $\dfrac{1}{36} \neq \dfrac{1}{72}$, **$A$, $B$, $C$ are NOT mutually independent**.

> **Conclusion:** All three events are **pairwise independent** but **not mutually independent**. This is a **classic KTU-style counterexample** demonstrating that pairwise independence does not imply mutual independence.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers in PEEET413:**
> 1. **Confusing "uncorrelated" with "independent"** — examiners deduct **2-3 marks** if you treat them as synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's correction** in sample variance — use $n-1$, not $n$.
> 3. **Using $|x|$ in markdown tables** — corrupts the table; use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX.
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — always state units of $\sigma$ and $\text{Cov}$.
> 5. **Claiming the converse of "independent $\Rightarrow$ zero covariance"** — automatic zero.
> 6. **Treating pairwise independence as mutual independence** — read the question carefully.
> 7. **Not showing the expansion step** in covariance derivations — examiners award partial credit only if intermediate algebra is visible.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Summary Statistics and Independence":**

### A. Summary Statistics
- **Mean** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ — first raw moment, sensitive to outliers.
- **Median** — 50th percentile, robust to outliers, minimizes MAD.
- **Variance** $\sigma^2$ — second central moment, *mean squared deviation*.
- **Standard Deviation** $\sigma$ — square root of variance, same unit as data.
- **MAD** — mean absolute deviation, uses $\ell_1$ norm.
- **IQR** — $Q_3 - Q_1$, robust spread, used in boxplots.
- **Covariance** $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ — unbounded, sign indicates direction.
- **Pearson $\rho$** — normalized covariance, $\rho \in [-1, +1]$.
- **Skewness** $\gamma_1$ — third standardized moment, asymmetry measure.
- **Kurtosis** $\gamma_2$ — fourth standardized moment minus 3, tail-heaviness.

### B. Independence
- **Definition:** $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$.
- **Equivalent (events):** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$.
- **Implication:** Independence $\Rightarrow$ zero covariance (converse **FALSE**).
- **Pairwise** $\not\Rightarrow$ **Mutual** independence.
- **Conditional independence** $X \perp\!\!\!\perp Y \mid Z$ is the basis of Bayesian Networks.
- **Independent** $\not\Rightarrow$ **Mutually Exclusive** (and vice versa).

### C. Computational Shortcuts
- $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \sum x_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}$.
- $\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$.
- $\text{Cov}(aX + b, cY + d) = ac \cdot \text{Cov}(X, Y)$.

### D. ML Connection Hooks
- **Z-score normalization** uses $\mu$, $\sigma$.
- **MSE loss** is a second-moment estimator.
- **PCA** diagonalizes the **covariance matrix** $\Sigma$.
- **Naive Bayes** assumes **feature independence** given the class.
- **GMM** models data as a **multivariate Gaussian** characterized by $\mu$ and $\Sigma$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** Before answering, classify the problem as either (i) *compute-and-substitute* (use formula table directly) or (ii) *proof-based* (use derivations from Section 3). In KTU board valuations, *proof-based* 7-mark questions require **all algebraic steps** — skipping intermediate algebra attracts a 2-mark penalty.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gaussian Distribution**, also called the **Normal Distribution**, is a continuous probability distribution for a real-valued random variable $X$ whose probability density function (PDF) is completely characterized by two parameters: the **mean** $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ (location) and the **variance** $\sigma^2 > 0$ (scale). It is formally expressed as:

$$X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

The probability density function is given by the **de Moivre–Laplace–Gauss equation**:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

where the constant $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$ is the **normalizing factor** that ensures the total area under the curve equals **1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Gaussian Distribution is the single most important probability distribution in Machine Learning. It underpins Linear Regression (assumed Gaussian noise), Gaussian Naive Bayes, Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM), Expectation–Maximization, Principal Component Analysis (PCA), and the foundational **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you measure the **height of every 18-year-old male in Kerala**. If you plot a histogram with height on the x-axis and the number of students on the y-axis, you will see:
- A **huge bump in the middle** (most students are around 170 cm).
- The frequency **tapers off symmetrically** in both directions.
- Almost **no one is below 140 cm or above 200 cm**.

That perfectly symmetric "bell-shaped" histogram is a Gaussian curve! The **peak (mean)** tells you the most likely value, and the **width (standard deviation)** tells you how spread out the measurements are. The taller and narrower the bell, the more confident you are about the typical height.

Another intuitive analogy: **dart throws at a dartboard**. A skilled (or lucky) player clusters darts near the center, with strays scattering outward in a roughly circular (Gaussian) pattern. The center is the mean, and the scatter radius is the standard deviation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Whenever many small, independent random effects are added together — measurement noise, genetic variation, accumulated rounding errors — the result tends toward a Gaussian, regardless of the original distribution. This is the Central Limit Theorem in action.

## 1.3 Notation, Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Mean (Expected Value):** $E[X] = \mu$  
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$  
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma$ (always non-negative)  
- **Standard Normal Variate:** $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$  
- **Normalization Constant:** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx \mathbf{0.3989}$  
- **Inflection Points of the bell curve:** $x = \mu \pm \sigma$ (points where curvature changes sign)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Bell Shape? — Operational Logic

The Gaussian PDF arises naturally from three intersecting mathematical facts:

1. **Symmetry around the mean:** Replacing $x$ with $2\mu - x$ leaves $f(x)$ unchanged, enforcing a mirror-image bell.
2. **Exponential decay of probability:** The term $\exp(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2})$ ensures probability drops *very fast* as we move away from $\mu$.
3. **Normalizing constant:** The factor $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$ is precisely chosen so $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$. This constant comes from **Euler's Gaussian integral** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx = \sqrt{\pi}$.

## 2.2 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

The CDF $F_X(x)$ is the area under the curve to the left of $x$:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(t-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dt$$

There is **no closed-form elementary expression** for $F_X(x)$. Instead, the standard normal CDF is denoted $\Phi(z)$ and is evaluated via tables or numerical approximation:

$$\Phi(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

where $\text{erf}(\cdot)$ is the **error function**.

## 2.3 Properties of the Gaussian Distribution

- **Symmetry:** $f(\mu + k) = f(\mu - k)$ — perfectly mirror-symmetric about $\mu$.
- **Mean = Median = Mode:** All three central-tendency measures coincide at $x = \mu$.
- **Total Probability:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ (always).
- **Tail Behavior:** Decays *super-exponentially* ($\sim e^{-x^2}$), much faster than polynomials.
- **Inflection Points:** Located at $\mu - \sigma$ and $\mu + \sigma$.
- **Reproducibility under linear combination:** If $X_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$ are independent, then $\sum a_i X_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\sum a_i \mu_i,\; \sum a_i^2 \sigma_i^2\right)$ — **sums of independent Gaussians remain Gaussian**.
- **Memoryless Property:** **NOT applicable** — the Gaussian is *not* memoryless (unlike the exponential).

## 2.4 The 68–95–99.7 Empirical Rule (KTU Favorite!)

For **any** Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

| Interval (in terms of $\mu, \sigma$) | Probability Mass Enclosed |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mu \pm 1\sigma$ | $\approx \mathbf{68.27\%}$ |
| $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ | $\approx \mathbf{95.45\%}$ |
| $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | $\approx \mathbf{99.73\%}$ |
| $\mu \pm 4\sigma$ | $\approx \mathbf{99.9937\%}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The "$\pm 3\sigma$" interval is the famous **"three-sigma rule"** used extensively in quality control (Six Sigma) and anomaly/outlier detection in ML pipelines.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Valid for $x \in (-\infty, \infty)$ |
| 2 | Mean | $E[X] = \mu$ | Units of $X$ |
| 3 | Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$ | Squared units of $X$ |
| 4 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ | Units of $X$ |
| 5 | Standardization | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ |
| 6 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = 0$ | Symmetric |
| 7 | Excess Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = 0$ | Mesokurtic (neither heavy nor light tailed) |
| 8 | MGF | $M_X(t) = \exp\!\left(\mu t + \dfrac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\right)$ | Exists for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 9 | Characteristic Function | $\phi_X(t) = \exp\!\left(i\mu t - \dfrac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\right)$ | $i = \sqrt{-1}$ |
| 10 | CDF (Standard) | $\Phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{z}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | Look up tables for $z \in [0, 3]$ |
| 11 | Reproducibility (Sum) | $\sum_{i=1}^n a_i X_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\sum a_i\mu_i,\; \sum a_i^2 \sigma_i^2\right)$ | Requires **independence** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Gaussian noise (AWGN — Additive White Gaussian Noise) models thermal noise in communication channels.
- **Signal Processing:** Matched filters are optimal for detecting Gaussian signals.
- **Quality Control:** Six-Sigma manufacturing uses the 68-95-99.7 rule to set control limits.
- **Machine Learning:** Gaussian assumptions appear in Linear Regression (residual error), Gaussian Naive Bayes (likelihood), and GMMs (cluster assignment).
- **Finance:** Black–Scholes option pricing uses log-normal (closely related to Gaussian) returns.
- **Biostatistics:** Heights, blood pressures, IQ scores — all approximately Gaussian.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean of the Gaussian Distribution

We want to show $E[X] = \mu$. By definition:

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x)\, dx = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

**Step 1 — Substitution.** Let $u = \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$, so that $x = \sigma u + \mu$ and $dx = \sigma\, du$.

The limits $-\infty < x < \infty$ map to $-\infty < u < \infty$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the integral.**

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma u + \mu) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) \cdot \sigma\, du$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the algebra.**

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma u + \mu) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du$$

**Step 4 — Split the integral.**

$$E[X] = \sigma \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du}_{I_1} \;+\; \mu \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du}_{I_2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $I_1$.** The integrand $u \cdot e^{-u^2/2}$ is an **odd function**, and the limits are symmetric. Therefore:

$$I_1 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate $I_2$.** This integral is the total area of the standard normal PDF, which by normalization equals 1:

$$I_2 = 1$$

**Step 7 — Combine.**

$$E[X] = \sigma \cdot 0 + \mu \cdot 1 = \mu \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Variance of the Gaussian Distribution

By definition, $\text{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2]$.

**Step 1 — Write the variance integral.**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x-\mu)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

**Step 2 — Substitute again** $u = \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$, giving $x-\mu = \sigma u$ and $dx = \sigma\, du$:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma u)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) \cdot \sigma\, du$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the second moment of the standard normal.** By definition, this is $E[Z^2]$ where $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Use the identity $E[Z^2] = \text{Var}(Z) + (E[Z])^2 = 1 + 0 = 1$. (Independent derivation can also be done using integration by parts, but this shortcut leverages Step 3 of the previous derivation where we showed $E[Z]=0$.)

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 \cdot 1 = \sigma^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Moment Generating Function (MGF)

The MGF is $M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]$.

**Step 1 — Write the MGF integral.**

$$M_X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{tx} \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

**Step 2 — Combine exponentials and complete the square.**

$$M_X(t) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(tx - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

Expand the negative quadratic:

$$-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{x^2 - 2\mu x + \mu^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu x}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

Add $tx$:

$$tx - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2} + \left(\frac{\mu}{\sigma^2} + t\right) x - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

Let $a = \frac{\mu}{\sigma^2} + t$. Then:

$$= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[x^2 - 2\sigma^2 a x\right] - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

**Step 3 — Complete the square** in $x$:

$$x^2 - 2\sigma^2 a x = (x - \sigma^2 a)^2 - \sigma^4 a^2$$

Therefore:

$$tx - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{(x - \sigma^2 a)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{\sigma^2 a^2}{2} - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the constant term** $\frac{\sigma^2 a^2}{2} - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2}$:

Substitute $a = \frac{\mu}{\sigma^2} + t$:

$$\sigma^2 a^2 = \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\sigma^2} + t\right)^2 = \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^4} + \frac{2\mu t}{\sigma^2} + t^2\right) = \frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2} + 2\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2$$

So:

$$\frac{\sigma^2 a^2}{2} - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2} + 2\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2\right) - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2} = \mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Pull the constant out and recognize the Gaussian integral.**

$$M_X(t) = \exp\!\left(\mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \sigma^2 a)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

The remaining integral is the total area under a Gaussian PDF (with shifted mean $\sigma^2 a$, same variance $\sigma^2$), which equals **1**.

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{M_X(t) = \exp\!\left(\mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Connection:** Setting $\mu = 0, \sigma^2 = 1$ recovers the MGF of the standard normal: $M_Z(t) = \exp(t^2/2)$. This compact result is heavily tested.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Standardization (Z-Transformation)

Given $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, define $Z = aX + b$. Using linearity:

$$E[Z] = a\mu + b, \quad \text{Var}(Z) = a^2 \sigma^2$$

For $Z$ to be standard normal $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$, we need:

$$a\mu + b = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad a^2 \sigma^2 = 1$$

Solving: $a = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$ (taking positive root), and $b = -\dfrac{\mu}{\sigma}$. Hence:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}}$$

This is the foundation for using **Z-tables** to compute probabilities for *any* Gaussian without re-integrating.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State any six important properties of the Gaussian (Normal) Distribution.**

**Model Answer (6 points, 3 marks):**

1. **Symmetry:** The PDF is symmetric about the mean $\mu$, i.e., $f(\mu+k) = f(\mu-k)$.
2. **Mean = Median = Mode:** All three measures of central tendency coincide at $\mu$.
3. **Total Probability:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ (PDF is properly normalized).
4. **Inflection Points:** Located at $\mu - \sigma$ and $\mu + \sigma$.
5. **Reproducibility:** Any linear combination of independent Gaussian random variables is again Gaussian.
6. **Decay:** Tails decay super-exponentially ($\sim e^{-x^2}$), faster than any polynomial.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "the distribution is bell-shaped" as a property. While descriptive, this is **not a mathematically precise property** — better to mention *symmetry*, *unimodality*, or the specific inflection-point locations.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the Standard Normal Distribution and explain the process of standardization with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Standard Normal Distribution** is a special case of the Gaussian distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and variance $\sigma^2 = 1$. It is denoted $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$, with PDF:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$$

**Standardization** is the process of converting any Gaussian variable $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ into the standard normal via the linear transformation:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Example:** If $X \sim \mathcal{N}(100, 25)$ (mean 100, SD 5), then $Z = \dfrac{X - 100}{5}$. To find $P(X \le 110)$, we compute $P(Z \le 2)$ and look up $\Phi(2) \approx 0.9772$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the mean and variance of a Gaussian random variable $X$ with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$.**

**Solution:**

The PDF is $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.

**Deriving the Mean:**

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

Substitute $u = \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$, so $x = \sigma u + \mu$, $dx = \sigma\, du$:

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma u + \mu) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$$

$$= \sigma \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du \;+\; \mu \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$$

The first integral vanishes (odd integrand), the second equals 1 (normalized PDF):

$$\boxed{E[X] = \mu}$$

**Deriving the Variance:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x-\mu)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

Substituting $u = \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$, $(x-\mu)^2 = \sigma^2 u^2$:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du = \sigma^2 \cdot E[Z^2]$$

Since $E[Z]=0$, $E[Z^2] = \text{Var}(Z) = 1$:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The marks of 1000 students in a KTU examination are normally distributed with mean 65 and standard deviation 10. Find:**
- **(i) The number of students scoring more than 75.**
- **(ii) The number of students scoring between 55 and 75.**
- **(iii) The maximum marks of the bottom 10% of students.**

**Solution:**

Given: $\mu = 65$, $\sigma = 10$, $N = 1000$.

**(i) Probability of scoring more than 75:**

$$P(X > 75) = P\!\left(Z > \frac{75 - 65}{10}\right) = P(Z > 1) = 1 - \Phi(1)$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi(1) = 0.8413$.

$$P(X > 75) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$$

Number of students $\approx 1000 \times 0.1587 = \mathbf{158.7 \approx 159}$.

**(ii) Probability of scoring between 55 and 75:**

$$P(55 < X < 75) = P\!\left(\frac{55-65}{10} < Z < \frac{75-65}{10}\right) = P(-1 < Z < 1)$$

$$= \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1) = \Phi(1) - (1 - \Phi(1)) = 2\Phi(1) - 1$$

$$= 2(0.8413) - 1 = \mathbf{0.6826}$$

Number of students $\approx 1000 \times 0.6826 = \mathbf{683}$.

**(iii) Bottom 10% cutoff (10th percentile):**

We need $x$ such that $P(X \le x) = 0.10$.

$$P\!\left(Z \le \frac{x - 65}{10}\right) = 0.10$$

From standard tables, $\Phi(z) = 0.10 \Rightarrow z \approx -1.2816$.

So:

$$\frac{x - 65}{10} = -1.2816 \implies x = 65 - 12.816 = \mathbf{52.184}$$

Maximum marks of the bottom 10% is approximately **52 marks**.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Moment Generating Function of a Gaussian random variable $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and hence obtain the MGF of the standard normal distribution.**

**Solution:**

By definition:

$$M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{tx} \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

Combine the exponentials and complete the square in $x$:

$$M_X(t) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(tx - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

Rewriting the exponent:

$$tx - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[x^2 - 2(\mu + \sigma^2 t)x\right] - \frac{\mu^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

Completing the square:

$$= -\frac{(x - (\mu + \sigma^2 t))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$M_X(t) = \exp\!\left(\mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\right) \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - (\mu+\sigma^2 t))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx}_{=\,1 \text{ (Gaussian area)}}$$

$$\boxed{M_X(t) = \exp\!\left(\mu t + \frac{\sigma^2 t^2}{2}\right)}$$

**Standard Normal MGF:** Setting $\mu = 0, \sigma^2 = 1$:

$$\boxed{M_Z(t) = \exp\!\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\right)}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If $X \sim \mathcal{N}(50, 16)$, find:**
- **(i) $P(X < 60)$**
- **(ii) $P(45 \le X \le 58)$**
- **(iii) The value of $x$ such that $P(X \ge x) = 0.20$**

**Solution:**

Given $\mu = 50$, $\sigma = \sqrt{16} = 4$.

**(i) $P(X < 60)$:**

$$Z = \frac{60 - 50}{4} = 2.5$$

From standard tables: $\Phi(2.5) = 0.9938$.

$$P(X < 60) = \mathbf{0.9938}$$

**(ii) $P(45 \le X \le 58)$:**

$$Z_1 = \frac{45 - 50}{4} = -1.25, \quad Z_2 = \frac{58 - 50}{4} = 2.0$$

$$\Phi(-1.25) = 1 - \Phi(1.25) = 1 - 0.8944 = 0.1056$$
$$\Phi(2.0) = 0.9772$$

$$P(45 \le X \le 58) = \Phi(2.0) - \Phi(-1.25) = 0.9772 - 0.1056 = \mathbf{0.8716}$$

**(iii) $P(X \ge x) = 0.20$:**

We need $P(X < x) = 0.80$.

$$P\!\left(Z < \frac{x-50}{4}\right) = 0.80 \implies \frac{x - 50}{4} = \Phi^{-1}(0.80) \approx 0.8416$$

$$x - 50 = 4 \times 0.8416 = 3.366 \implies \boxed{x \approx 53.37}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never forget to subtract 1:** When asked $P(X > a)$, students often give $\Phi(z)$ instead of $1 - \Phi(z)$. This single mistake costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Watch the direction:** $P(X \le a) = \Phi(z)$ but $P(X \ge a) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. Read the inequality carefully!
> 3. **Standardize correctly:** $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$, NOT $(X - \sigma)/\mu$ and NOT $(\mu - X)/\sigma$.
> 4. **For negative Z values:** Always use $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ instead of looking up negative tables.
> 5. **Always cite the parameter:** State $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ at the start of any problem to earn "method marks" even if calculations go wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Gaussian PDF $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$; depends only on $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
- **Two-parameter family:** $\mu$ = location (center), $\sigma$ = scale (spread).
- **Standard Normal:** $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ with PDF $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$.
- **Standardization:** Always convert $X$ to $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$ before using tables.
- **Mean / Variance:** $E[X] = \mu$, $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$.
- **Skewness = 0, Excess Kurtosis = 0:** Always symmetric and mesokurtic.
- **MGF:** $M_X(t) = \exp(\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2 / 2)$ — exists for **all** real $t$.
- **CDF:** No closed form; computed via $\Phi(z) = \frac{1}{2}[1 + \text{erf}(z/\sqrt{2})]$ or tables.
- **Empirical Rule:** 68% within $1\sigma$, 95% within $2\sigma$, 99.7% within $3\sigma$.
- **Reproducibility:** Linear combinations of independent Gaussians remain Gaussian.
- **Not memoryless:** Unlike the exponential distribution.
- **Tails decay as $e^{-x^2}$:** Super-exponentially fast — out-of-distribution points have *vanishingly* small probability.
- **ML Importance:** Foundation for Gaussian Naive Bayes, GMMs, PCA, AWGN channel models, and the **Central Limit Theorem**.
- **Common Z-table values to memorize:** $\Phi(0) = 0.5$, $\Phi(1) \approx 0.8413$, $\Phi(2) \approx 0.9772$, $\Phi(3) \approx 0.9987$, $\Phi(-1.96) \approx 0.025$.
- **Symmetry property:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ — saves time during exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conjugacy and the Exponential Family - Change of Variables/Inverse Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conjugacy and the Exponential Family - Change of Variables/Inverse Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Exponential Family of Distributions

### Formal Definition
A probability distribution belongs to the **Exponential Family** if its probability density (or mass) function can be written in the canonical form:

$$f(x \mid \theta) = h(x) \, \exp\!\left(\eta(\theta)\, T(x) - A(\theta)\right)$$

where each component plays a distinct statistical role:

| Symbol | Name | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | Natural/Canonical Parameter | Encodes information about the distribution |
| $T(x)$ | Sufficient Statistic | Compresses the data without losing information about $\theta$ |
| $\eta(\theta)$ | Link / Natural Parameter Function | Connects the parameter to the sufficient statistic |
| $A(\theta)$ | Log-Partition / Cumulant Function | Normalization constant (log of normalizing constant) |
| $h(x)$ | Base Measure | Carries the part of the density independent of $\theta$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The canonical form above is the unified template for Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Gaussian, Beta, Gamma, and many other distributions. Recognizing a distribution as a member of the exponential family is the first step toward identifying its conjugate prior.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the exponential family as a **"factory mold"** for probability distributions. Every distribution in the family is cast from the same mold — only the **ingredients** (the functions $h$, $T$, $\eta$, $A$) change. Once you understand the mold, you can instantly identify the family member and predict its behavior. This is why statisticians love this form: it gives a universal algebraic skeleton.

---

## 1.2 Conjugacy — A Bayesian Marriage

### Formal Definition
A prior distribution $\pi(\theta)$ is called **conjugate** to a likelihood $f(x \mid \theta)$ if the resulting posterior $\pi(\theta \mid x)$ belongs to the **same parametric family** as the prior:

$$\pi(\theta \mid x) \;\propto\; \pi(\theta)\, f(x \mid \theta) \;\Longrightarrow\; \pi(\theta \mid x) \in \mathcal{F}(\theta)$$

where $\mathcal{F}(\theta)$ denotes the family of the prior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conjugacy = Closed-Form Posterior.** The defining feature of conjugate priors is that Bayesian updating reduces to **simple arithmetic on hyperparameters** — no numerical integration or MCMC is required.

### Canonical Conjugate Pairs (High-Yield Table)

| Likelihood $f(x \mid \theta)$ | Conjugate Prior $\pi(\theta)$ | Posterior $\pi(\theta \mid x)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli / Binomial | Beta($\alpha, \beta$) | Beta($\alpha + \sum x_i,\; \beta + n - \sum x_i$) |
| Poisson($\lambda$) | Gamma($\alpha, \beta$) | Gamma($\alpha + \sum x_i,\; \beta + n$) |
| Gaussian($\mu, \sigma^2$) with known $\sigma^2$ | Normal($\mu_0, \tau^2$) | Normal weighted mean/precision |
| Exponential($\lambda$) | Gamma($\alpha, \beta$) | Gamma($\alpha + n,\; \beta + \sum x_i$) |
| Multinomial | Dirichlet($\boldsymbol{\alpha}$) | Dirichlet($\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \mathbf{n}$) |

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you have a **belief** (prior) about how a coin is weighted. After flipping it 100 times, your belief **updates** (posterior). Conjugacy is like a **fountain of youth** — no matter how many times you update, your belief never leaves its original family. The shape stays the same; only the parameters grow.

---

## 1.3 Change of Variables & Inverse Transform

### Change of Variables — Formal Definition
Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with density $f_X(x)$ and let $Y = g(X)$ be a **monotonic, differentiable** transformation. Then the density of $Y$ is:

$$f_Y(y) = f_X\!\left(g^{-1}(y)\right) \left\vert \frac{d}{dy}\, g^{-1}(y) \right\vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Jacobian Factor is Mandatory.** Skipping $\left\vert \frac{dx}{dy} \right\vert$ is the single most common KTU exam mistake in this topic. It compensates for the stretching/compression of probability mass when $g$ is not area-preserving.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a **rubber sheet** with probability "ink" smeared on it. If you stretch the sheet horizontally by a factor of 2, the same amount of ink now covers twice the area — so its **density must halve**. The Jacobian $\left\vert dx/dy \right\vert$ captures precisely this stretching.

### Inverse Transform Sampling — Formal Definition
**Theorem (Inverse CDF Method):** If $X$ has a continuous, strictly increasing CDF $F_X$ and $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, then:

$$X \;=\; F_X^{-1}(U) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad X \;\sim\; F_X$$

### Intuitive Analogy
The uniform distribution $U(0,1)$ is the **"raw material"** of random number generation. The inverse CDF $F_X^{-1}$ is a **"mold"** that reshapes that uniform randomness into the target distribution $F_X$. This is the bridge from "easy" random numbers to "hard" distributions.

---

## 1.4 Why This Matters in Machine Learning
- **Variational Inference & Laplace Approximations** rely on the exponential family because its log-partition $A(\theta)$ has closed-form derivatives, enabling efficient gradient-based optimization.
- **Conjugate priors** power the entire framework of Bayesian logistic regression, Bayesian neural networks, and graphical models.
- **Change of variables** underpins normalizing flows, reparameterization tricks (e.g., the reparameterization trick in VAEs), and copula transformations.
- **Inverse transform sampling** is the foundational method in Monte Carlo simulation, MCMC initialization, and synthetic data generation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of the Exponential Family

A distribution $f(x \mid \theta)$ is in the exponential family **if and only if** its log-likelihood is a linear function of features $\phi_i(x)$ with parameters $\theta_i$:

$$\log f(x \mid \theta) \;=\; \langle \theta, \phi(x) \rangle - A(\theta) + \log h(x)$$

This is the **most general** form. Several structural properties follow:

- **Sufficiency of $T(x)$:** The statistic $T(x)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ if the likelihood factors as $f(x \mid \theta) = g(T(x), \theta) \, H(x)$. In exponential form, $T(x)$ is automatically sufficient.
- **Moment Generation:** $A'(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\theta[T(X)]$ and $A''(\theta) = \text{Var}_\theta[T(X)]$. Thus the cumulant function encodes all moments.
- **Maximum Likelihood:** $\nabla_\theta \log f = T(x) - A'(\theta) = 0 \Rightarrow \hat\theta = (A')^{-1}(T(x))$, the MLE.

### Why is the Normalization $A(\theta)$ Required?
The density must integrate to 1:

$$\int f(x\mid\theta)\, dx = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \exp\!\big(A(\theta)\big) = \int h(x)\exp\!\big(\eta(\theta)T(x)\big) dx$$

so $A(\theta)$ acts as a **compensating constant** that rebalances the exponential tilt introduced by $\eta(\theta)T(x)$.

---

## 2.2 Constructing a Conjugate Prior

For a likelihood from the exponential family, the **natural conjugate prior** has the form:

$$\pi(\theta) \;\propto\; \exp\!\big(\eta(\theta) \nu - \lambda A(\theta)\big)$$

where $\nu$ and $\lambda$ are hyperparameters (often called the prior "pseudo-count" and "prior strength"). The resulting posterior has the **same functional form** with updated parameters:

$$\nu \mapsto \nu + \sum_i T(x_i), \qquad \lambda \mapsto \lambda + n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step Logic of Why Conjugacy Works:**
> 1. Write likelihood in exponential form: $f(x\mid\theta) = h(x)\exp(\eta T(x) - A(\theta))$.
> 2. Multiply by a prior that has the **same $\eta$-dependence**: $\pi(\theta) \propto \exp(\eta \nu - \lambda A(\theta))$.
> 3. The product is $\propto \exp(\eta(\nu + T(x)) - (\lambda+1)A(\theta))$.
> 4. Recognize the product as a member of the same family with updated $\nu' = \nu + T(x)$, $\lambda' = \lambda+1$.
> 5. Hence the posterior is in the same family — conjugacy proven.

---

## 2.3 Change of Variables — Two Equivalent Formulations

Let $Y = g(X)$ with $X = g^{-1}(Y)$ (assume $g$ is monotonic and differentiable):

**Form 1 (forward Jacobian):**

$$f_Y(y) = f_X(x)\, \left\vert \frac{dx}{dy} \right\vert \quad \text{where } x = g^{-1}(y)$$

**Form 2 (inverse Jacobian):**

$$f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y))\, \left\vert \frac{d}{dy} g^{-1}(y) \right\vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both forms are equivalent. Form 1 is usually easier because $x = g^{-1}(y)$ and the derivative $dx/dy$ are computed by inverting $y = g(x)$.

### Multivariate Extension
For $\mathbf{Y} = g(\mathbf{X}) \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the density transforms via the **Jacobian matrix** $J = \partial \mathbf{x}/\partial \mathbf{y}$:

$$f_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{y}) = f_{\mathbf{X}}\!\big(g^{-1}(\mathbf{y})\big)\, \big\vert \det(J) \big\vert$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exponential family canonical form | $f(x\vert\theta) = h(x)\exp(\eta(\theta)T(x) - A(\theta))$ | Foundation of all families |
| 2 | Natural conjugate prior | $\pi(\theta) \propto \exp(\eta(\theta)\nu - \lambda A(\theta))$ | Same family as posterior |
| 3 | Bayes update (conjugate) | $\nu' = \nu + \sum T(x_i)$, $\lambda' = \lambda + n$ | Hyperparameter arithmetic |
| 4 | Mean from cumulant | $\mathbb{E}[T(X)] = A'(\theta)$ | First moment identity |
| 5 | Variance from cumulant | $\text{Var}[T(X)] = A''(\theta)$ | Second moment identity |
| 6 | MLE in exponential family | $\hat\theta = (A')^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum T(x_i)\right)$ | Method-of-moments form |
| 7 | Change of variables (univariate) | $f_Y(y) = f_X(x)\vert dx/dy \vert$, $x=g^{-1}(y)$ | Jacobian is mandatory |
| 8 | Change of variables (multivariate) | $f_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{y}) = f_{\mathbf{X}}(g^{-1}(\mathbf{y}))\vert \det J \vert$ | $J = \partial \mathbf{x}/\partial \mathbf{y}$ |
| 9 | Logit transform | $p = \sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ | Maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$ |
| 10 | Inverse logit density | $f_Z(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))\, f_P(\sigma(z))$ | Used in logistic regression |
| 11 | Inverse transform theorem | $X = F_X^{-1}(U)$, $U \sim U(0,1) \Rightarrow X \sim F_X$ | Foundation of MC sampling |
| 12 | Beta–Binomial posterior mean | $\mathbb{E}[\theta\vert x] = \frac{\alpha + \sum x_i}{\alpha + \beta + n}$ | Smoothed MLE |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Conjugate Gamma–Poisson models detect anomalous event counts in patient heartbeats or neural spike trains.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Inverse transform sampling generates synthetic sensor noise distributions for testing robustness of perception systems.
- **A/B Testing & Marketing Analytics:** Beta–Binomial conjugacy gives closed-form credible intervals for click-through-rate estimation.
- **Speech Recognition:** Gaussian conjugacy underwrites the Bayesian filter for hidden Markov models (HMMs).
- **Climate Modeling:** Reparameterization (a multivariate change of variables) maps skewed rainfall distributions to symmetric Gaussian for easier inference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Showing Bernoulli is in the Exponential Family

**Goal:** Express $f(x \mid p) = p^x (1-p)^{1-x}$, $x \in \{0,1\}$, in canonical form.

**Step 1:** Take the natural log to expose linearity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x \mid p) &= p^x (1-p)^{1-x} \\
\log f(x \mid p) &= x \log p + (1-x)\log(1-p) \\
&= x \log p + \log(1-p) - x \log(1-p) \\
&= x \log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) + \log(1-p)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Exponentiate to recover the exponential-family form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x \mid p) &= \exp\!\left[x \log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) + \log(1-p)\right] \\
&= (1-p)\cdot \exp\!\left[x\, \eta(p)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the canonical components:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
h(x) &= 1 \\
T(x) &= x \\
\eta(p) &= \log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) \;\; \text{(logit link)} \\
A(p) &= -\log(1-p) \;\; \text{(log-partition)}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Conclusion:** Bernoulli is in the exponential family. Since $A'(p) = \dfrac{1}{1-p} = p$ and $A''(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)^2} = p(1-p)$, these are the mean and variance of Bernoulli — confirming the cumulant identities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beta–Binomial Conjugacy (Complete)

**Setup:**
- Likelihood: $X_i \mid p \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$, so $\sum_{i=1}^n X_i \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$.
- Prior: $p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ with density $\pi(p) = \dfrac{p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}}{B(\alpha,\beta)}$.

**Step 1:** Posterior proportionality (drop constants independent of $p$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi(p \mid \mathbf{x}) &\propto \pi(p) \cdot f(\mathbf{x} \mid p) \\
&\propto p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i} \\
&\propto p^{\alpha-1 + \sum x_i}\,(1-p)^{\beta-1 + (n - \sum x_i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize the kernel of a Beta distribution:

$$
\pi(p \mid \mathbf{x}) \;\propto\; p^{(\alpha + \sum x_i) - 1}\,(1-p)^{(\beta + n - \sum x_i) - 1}
$$

This is the kernel of $\text{Beta}\!\left(\alpha + \sum x_i,\; \beta + n - \sum x_i\right)$.

**Step 3:** Conclude the conjugate update rule:

$$
\boxed{\;p \mid \mathbf{x} \;\sim\; \text{Beta}\!\left(\alpha + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i,\;\; \beta + n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)\;}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the posterior mean:

$$
\mathbb{E}[p \mid \mathbf{x}] \;=\; \frac{\alpha + \sum x_i}{\alpha + \beta + n} \;=\; \frac{\alpha + \beta}{\alpha + \beta + n}\cdot\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta} \;+\; \frac{n}{\alpha+\beta+n}\cdot\frac{\sum x_i}{n}
$$

This is a **convex combination** of the prior mean $\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}$ and the MLE $\bar{x}$, with weights controlled by $\alpha + \beta$ (prior strength).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Change of Variables: Log Transform

**Problem:** If $X \sim \text{LogNormal}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, find the density of $Y = \log X$. Equivalently, derive the lognormal density from a normal.

**Step 1:** Start with $Y = \log X$, so $X = e^Y$ and $\dfrac{dx}{dy} = e^y$.

**Step 2:** Apply the change-of-variables formula:

$$
f_Y(y) \;=\; f_X(x)\, \left\vert \frac{dx}{dy} \right\vert \;=\; f_X(e^y)\cdot e^y
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $f_X$ for the lognormal (defined as $X = e^Z$ with $Z \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$):

$$
f_X(x) = \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\log x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x > 0
$$

**Step 4:** Plug $x = e^y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_Y(y) &= \frac{1}{e^y \sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\cdot e^y \\
&= \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Identify the result. We get back the normal density on $y$:

$$
\boxed{\;Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\;}
$$

This is consistent with the definition: a lognormal $X$ is one whose logarithm $Y$ is normal. The change-of-variables Jacobian $e^y$ exactly cancels the $1/x$ factor in the lognormal density.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Inverse Transform Sampling on Exponential Distribution

**Problem:** Use the inverse CDF method to sample $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ given $U \sim U(0,1)$.

**Step 1:** Write the CDF of the exponential:

$$
F_X(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \ge 0
$$

**Step 2:** Solve $u = F_X(x)$ for $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= 1 - e^{-\lambda x} \\
1 - u &= e^{-\lambda x} \\
\log(1-u) &= -\lambda x \\
x &= -\frac{1}{\lambda}\log(1-u)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $U \sim U(0,1)$, the random variable $1 - U$ is also $U(0,1)$. Thus the inverse CDF is:

$$
\boxed{\;X = F_X^{-1}(U) = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\log(U)\;}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification. Compute the CDF of $X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \le x) &= P\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{\lambda}\log U \le x\right) \\
&= P\!\left(\log U \ge -\lambda x\right) \\
&= P\!\left(U \ge e^{-\lambda x}\right) \\
&= 1 - e^{-\lambda x} = F_X(x) \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Practical sampling algorithm:
1. Generate $U \sim U(0,1)$ using a pseudo-random generator.
2. Return $X = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\log U$.
3. $X$ is distributed as $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Bivariate Change of Variables (Box–Muller Preview)

**Problem:** Transform two independent uniforms $(U_1, U_2)$ to two independent standard normals $(Z_1, Z_2)$.

**Step 1:** Set up the polar transformation:

$$
Z_1 = R\cos\Theta, \quad Z_2 = R\sin\Theta, \quad R \ge 0,\; \Theta \in [0, 2\pi)
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Jacobian of $(r, \theta) \mapsto (z_1, z_2)$:

$$
J = \begin{vmatrix} \partial z_1/\partial r & \partial z_1/\partial \theta \\ \partial z_2/\partial r & \partial z_2/\partial \theta \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} \cos\theta & -r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & r\cos\theta \end{vmatrix} = r
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the bivariate change-of-variables formula:

$$
f_{Z_1, Z_2}(z_1, z_2) = f_{R,\Theta}(r, \theta)\cdot \frac{1}{|\det J|} = f_{R,\Theta}(r, \theta) \cdot \frac{1}{r}
$$

**Step 4:** The standard bivariate normal density is:

$$
f_{Z_1,Z_2}(z_1, z_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\exp\!\left(-\frac{z_1^2 + z_2^2}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Equate to deduce the joint density of $(R, \Theta)$:

$$
f_{R,\Theta}(r, \theta) = r \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi}\exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2\pi}}_{f_\Theta(\theta)} \cdot \underbrace{r\, e^{-r^2/2}}_{f_R(r)}
$$

Hence $R$ and $\Theta$ are independent, with $\Theta \sim U(0,2\pi)$ and $R^2 \sim \text{Exp}(1/2)$.

**Step 6:** Invert the CDFs to derive Box–Muller:

$$
\Theta = 2\pi U_1, \quad R = \sqrt{-2\log U_2}
$$

and the final sampling formulas:

$$
\boxed{Z_1 = \sqrt{-2\log U_2}\cos(2\pi U_1), \quad Z_2 = \sqrt{-2\log U_2}\sin(2\pi U_1)}
$$

This elegant derivation is a flagship application of the change-of-variables theorem in machine learning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conjugacy Update Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prior Distribution pi theta] --> B[Observe Data x1 ... xn]
    B --> C[Likelihood f x given theta]
    C --> D[Bayes Theorem]
    A --> D
    D --> E{Conjugate Family?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Posterior in Same Family]
    E -- No --> G[Numerical or MCMC Methods]
    F --> H[Closed Form Hyperparameters Updated]
    H --> I[Posterior Predictive]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style F fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

## 4.2 Exponential Family Anatomy Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Exponential Family Density] --> B[h x - Base Measure]
    A --> C[T x - Sufficient Statistic]
    A --> D[eta theta - Link Function]
    A --> E[A theta - Log Partition]
    B --> F[Independent of theta]
    C --> G[Carries Data Information]
    D --> H[Connects Parameter to Data]
    E --> I[Normalizing Constant]
    F --> J[Member of Exponential Family]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    style A fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style J fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

## 4.3 Change of Variables and Inverse Transform Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Random Variable X with fX] --> B[Transformation Y = g X]
    B --> C[Invert to get X = ginv Y]
    C --> D[Compute Jacobian dx over dy]
    D --> E[Apply fY y = fX ginv y times Jacobian]
    E --> F[New Density fY]
    G[U uniform 0 1] --> H[Compute FX inverse]
    H --> I[Sample X = FX inverse U]
    I --> J[Distribution of X is F]
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style J fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

## 4.4 Bayesian Update Loop with Conjugate Priors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Hyperparameters alpha0 beta0] --> B[Observe Data Point x1]
    B --> C[Update alpha to alpha0 + x1]
    B --> D[Update beta to beta0 + 1 minus x1]
    C --> E[Posterior Beta with new params]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Next Data Point?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Observe x2]
    G --> C
    F -- No --> H[Posterior Predictive Distribution]
    style A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.5 Sampling Method Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Random Sample Generation] --> B[Target Distribution F]
    B --> C[Inverse Transform]
    B --> D[Rejection Sampling]
    B --> E[Box Muller]
    B --> F[MCMC Methods]
    C --> G[Requires Closed Form CDF]
    D --> H[Uses Envelope Distribution]
    E --> I[For Standard Normal]
    F --> J[For Complex Posteriors]
    G --> K[Simple and Exact]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    style A fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style K fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **exponential family** of distributions. Identify whether the Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda$ belongs to the exponential family, and write its canonical form.

**Model Answer:**

A probability density (or mass) function $f(x \mid \theta)$ belongs to the **exponential family** if it can be expressed as:

$$f(x \mid \theta) = h(x)\,\exp\!\big(\eta(\theta)\, T(x) - A(\theta)\big)$$

**Poisson membership.** The Poisson PMF is $f(x \mid \lambda) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x}{x!}$ for $x \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$. Taking the log and exponentiating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x \mid \lambda) &= \frac{1}{x!}\,\exp\!\big(x \log\lambda - \lambda\big) \\
\Rightarrow h(x) &= \frac{1}{x!}, \;\; T(x) = x, \;\; \eta(\lambda) = \log\lambda, \;\; A(\lambda) = \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the Poisson distribution **belongs to the exponential family**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State the **change of variables theorem** for a continuous random variable. Why is the Jacobian factor $\left\vert dx/dy \right\vert$ essential?

**Model Answer:**

**Theorem.** Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with density $f_X(x)$ and $Y = g(X)$ a strictly monotonic, differentiable transformation. Then:

$$f_Y(y) = f_X\!\big(g^{-1}(y)\big)\cdot \left\vert \frac{d}{dy}\, g^{-1}(y) \right\vert$$

**Why the Jacobian is essential.** The function $g$ stretches or compresses the real line, redistributing probability mass. The Jacobian $\left\vert dx/dy \right\vert$ compensates for this distortion, ensuring that $f_Y$ still integrates to **1**. Without it, the resulting "density" would either overestimate (compression) or underestimate (stretching) the true probability. $\blacksquare$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern]** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and provides an internal choice between two alternatives. Each alternative is split into **(a) [7 marks]** and **(b) [7 marks]**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Show that the **Gamma distribution** is a member of the exponential family, and derive the natural conjugate prior for the **rate parameter $\beta$** of an Exponential likelihood.

**(b) [7 marks]** Suppose $X \sim \text{Exponential}(\beta)$ with density $f(x \mid \beta) = \beta e^{-\beta x}$ for $x > 0$. Given the prior $\beta \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \theta)$ with density $\pi(\beta) \propto \beta^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta/\theta}$, derive the posterior distribution of $\beta$ given observations $x_1, \ldots, x_n$.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Gamma in the Exponential Family & Conjugate Prior

**Step 1.** Show Gamma is in the exponential family. The Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ density is:

$$f(x \mid \alpha, \beta) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\, x^{\alpha - 1} e^{-\beta x}, \quad x > 0$$

Take the log and exponentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log f(x \mid \alpha, \beta) &= (\alpha - 1)\log x - \beta x + \alpha\log\beta - \log\Gamma(\alpha) \\
f(x \mid \alpha, \beta) &= \frac{x^{\alpha-1}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\,\exp\!\big((\alpha - 1)\log x - \beta x + \alpha\log\beta\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

In the canonical form $f = h(x)\exp(\eta(\theta)T(x) - A(\theta))$, with $\theta = (\alpha, \beta)$ and $T(x) = (\log x, x)$:

$$
h(x) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)},\quad \eta(\alpha,\beta) = (\alpha, -\beta),\quad A(\alpha,\beta) = -\alpha\log\beta
$$

**Step 2.** Conjugate prior for $\beta$ in $X \mid \beta \sim \text{Exp}(\beta)$. The exponential density is:

$$f(x \mid \beta) = \exp\!\big(\log\beta - \beta x\big) = \exp\!\big(\eta(\beta) T(x) - A(\beta)\big)$$

with $\eta(\beta) = -\beta$, $T(x) = x$, $A(\beta) = -\log\beta$. A prior that matches the $\eta$-structure is:

$$\pi(\beta) \propto \exp\!\big(-\beta\nu - \lambda(-\log\beta)\big) = \beta^{\lambda - 1}\exp(-\beta\nu)$$

This is precisely the Gamma$(\lambda, 1/\nu)$ density. Hence the Gamma family is conjugate to the Exponential likelihood. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Posterior Derivation

**Step 1.** Write the joint posterior kernel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi(\beta \mid \mathbf{x}) &\propto \pi(\beta)\cdot f(\mathbf{x} \mid \beta) \\
&\propto \beta^{\alpha - 1} e^{-\beta/\theta} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} \beta e^{-\beta x_i} \\
&\propto \beta^{\alpha - 1} \cdot \beta^{n}\cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta}{\theta} - \beta\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right) \\
&\propto \beta^{(\alpha + n) - 1}\,\exp\!\left(-\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{\theta} + \sum x_i\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize the kernel of a Gamma distribution:

$$
\boxed{\;\beta \mid \mathbf{x} \;\sim\; \text{Gamma}\!\left(\alpha + n,\; \frac{1}{\frac{1}{\theta} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}\right)\;}
$$

Equivalently, with the rate parameterization $\beta \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha', \text{rate} = \beta')$:

$$
\alpha' = \alpha + n, \quad \beta' = \frac{1}{\theta} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

**Step 3 — Posterior Mean:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[\beta \mid \mathbf{x}] = \frac{\alpha + n}{\frac{1}{\theta} + \sum x_i}
$$

This gives a Bayesian shrinkage estimate of the rate, blending the prior $1/\theta$ with the data MLE $1/\bar{x}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with density $f_X(x) = 2x$ for $0 < x < 1$ and zero otherwise. Find the density of $Y = -\log X$ using the change-of-variables theorem.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **inverse transform sampling method**. Use it to derive a sampling algorithm for the Cauchy distribution with CDF $F(x) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{\pi}\arctan(x)$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Density of $Y = -\log X$

**Step 1:** Identify the transformation. $Y = -\log X$ implies $X = e^{-Y}$ and the range $0 < X < 1$ becomes $0 < Y < \infty$.

**Step 2:** Compute the Jacobian:

$$\frac{dx}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(e^{-y}\right) = -e^{-y} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \left\vert \frac{dx}{dy} \right\vert = e^{-y}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the change-of-variables formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_Y(y) &= f_X(e^{-y})\cdot e^{-y} \\
&= 2 e^{-y} \cdot e^{-y} \quad \text{(since } 0 < e^{-y} < 1\text{)} \\
&= 2 e^{-2y}, \quad y > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\;Y \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda = 2)\;}$$

This shows that applying a log transform to a uniform-like density can yield an exponential. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Inverse Transform Sampling for the Cauchy Distribution

**Step 1 — Statement of the Method.** If $F_X$ is a continuous, strictly increasing CDF and $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, then $X = F_X^{-1}(U)$ has CDF $F_X$.

**Proof sketch:** $P(X \le x) = P(F_X^{-1}(U) \le x) = P(U \le F_X(x)) = F_X(x)$ since $F_X$ is invertible.

**Step 2 — Inverse CDF of the Cauchy.** Solve $u = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{\pi}\arctan(x)$ for $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u - \frac{1}{2} &= \frac{1}{\pi}\arctan(x) \\
\pi\!\left(u - \frac{1}{2}\right) &= \arctan(x) \\
x &= \tan\!\left(\pi\!\left(u - \frac{1}{2}\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sampling Algorithm:**

1. Generate $U \sim U(0, 1)$.
2. Compute $X = \tan\!\left(\pi\!\left(U - \frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$.
3. $X$ follows the standard Cauchy distribution.

**Step 4 — Verification (informal):** Differentiating $F_X^{-1}(u) = \tan(\pi(u-1/2))$:

$$
\frac{d}{du} F_X^{-1}(u) = \pi \sec^2\!\left(\pi\!\left(u - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\right)
$$

and using the identity $\sec^2\theta = 1 + \tan^2\theta$ with $x = \tan(\pi(u-1/2))$, we recover $f_X(x) = \dfrac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}$ as expected. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the Jacobian** $\left\vert dx/dy \right\vert$ in change-of-variables problems — costs up to **3 marks**.
> - **Confusing natural parameter $\eta$ with the original parameter $\theta$** when writing the canonical form. Be explicit.
> - **Forgetting to verify the support** of $Y$ after transformation. The range of $y$ must be specified for the density to be valid.
> - **Confusing $\text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ parameterizations.** Always state whether $\beta$ is the rate or the scale — the KTU 2024 scheme uses the **rate** form unless stated otherwise.
> - **In inverse-transform problems,** forgetting that $1-U$ and $U$ have the same $U(0,1)$ distribution — this lets you drop the $1-u$ inside $\log$.
> - **In Box–Muller derivations,** sign errors in the Jacobian determinant $r$ (always positive) are a frequent trap.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Exponential Family Canonical Form:** $f(x \mid \theta) = h(x)\exp(\eta(\theta)T(x) - A(\theta))$. Recognise Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Gaussian, Beta, Gamma, Exponential as members.
- **Conjugate Prior Definition:** Prior and posterior must be in the **same family** — gives closed-form Bayesian updates.
- **Beta–Binomial Conjugacy:** Posterior hyperparameters are $(\alpha + \sum x_i, \beta + n - \sum x_i)$. This is the workhorse of A/B testing and click-through-rate estimation.
- **Gamma–Poisson / Gamma–Exponential Conjugacy:** The Gamma family is conjugate to count/rate-based likelihoods.
- **Normal–Normal Conjugacy:** With known $\sigma^2$, the prior is $\mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau_0^2)$ and the posterior is a precision-weighted average of prior mean and sample mean.
- **Cumulant Function Identities:** $\mathbb{E}[T(X)] = A'(\theta)$, $\text{Var}[T(X)] = A''(\theta)$. These are essential for quick moment computations.
- **Change of Variables Formula:** $f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \left\vert \frac{d}{dy} g^{-1}(y) \right\vert$. The Jacobian is non-negotiable.
- **Multivariate Extension:** Replace the scalar Jacobian with $|\det J|$ where $J = \partial \mathbf{x}/\partial \mathbf{y}$.
- **Inverse Transform Theorem:** $X = F_X^{-1}(U)$ with $U \sim U(0,1)$ gives $X \sim F_X$. The most elementary general-purpose sampling method.
- **Worked Examples to Remember:** LogNormal density derivation (Jacobian $e^y$ cancels $1/x$), Box–Muller transform (uses polar coordinates and bivariate change of variables), Beta posterior mean as a convex combination of prior mean and MLE.
- **Practical Sampling Algorithms:** Exponential — $X = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\log U$; Cauchy — $X = \tan(\pi(U - 1/2))$; Standard Normal via Box–Muller — $Z_1, Z_2 = \sqrt{-2\log U_2}\cdot(\cos 2\pi U_1, \sin 2\pi U_1)$.
- **Engineering Relevance:** Conjugate priors power Bayesian ML pipelines; change of variables underwrites normalizing flows, VAE reparameterization, and copula models; inverse transform sampling initializes MCMC chains.
- **Common KTU Exam Traps:** Jacobian omission, support mis-specification, parameterization ambiguity, confusing sufficient statistic $T(x)$ with raw data $x$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Using Gradient Descent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET413 - MATHEMATICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization Using Gradient Descent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable, real-valued cost (or loss) function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$, where $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ represents the parameter vector. The method proceeds by updating the parameters in the *direction of steepest descent*, i.e., opposite to the gradient of the function at the current point.

Mathematically, the iterative update rule is given by:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k} - \alpha_{k} \, \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$$

where:
- $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}$ is the parameter vector at the $k$-th iteration.
- $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k})$ is the gradient vector of the cost function evaluated at $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k}$.
- $\alpha_{k} > 0$ is the **learning rate** (also called the **step size**), which controls the magnitude of the update.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Hiker"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (Real-world analogy):** Imagine you are a hiker trapped on a foggy mountain, blindfolded, and you want to reach the valley (lowest point). The only sense you have is the *slope of the ground under your feet*. You feel around — if the ground slopes downward to your right, you take a step to the right. You repeat this process, taking proportional steps based on how steep the slope is. Eventually, you reach a point where the ground feels flat — that is the **local minimum**, the solution of gradient descent.

| Mountain Hiking Term | Mathematical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mountain terrain | Cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| Hiker's position | Parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}_k$ |
| Slope under the feet | Gradient $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k)$ |
| Step length | Learning rate $\alpha_k$ |
| Valley floor | Global/local minimum of $J$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** The hiker takes *larger steps* on steep slopes and *smaller steps* near flat regions, mimicking how the gradient's magnitude controls the step size naturally.

## 1.3 Why Gradient Descent is Central to Machine Learning

In supervised learning, the goal is to find parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (weights of a model) that minimize a **loss function** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ which quantifies the error between predicted and actual values. Since the loss surface in deep learning can have **millions of dimensions**, closed-form solutions are infeasible. Gradient descent provides a scalable, iterative approach to navigate this high-dimensional space.

The standard performance metric used in KTU evaluations is the **mean squared error (MSE)** given by:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

and $\nabla J$ is computed via **backpropagation** in neural networks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Taylor's Theorem Motivation

Gradient descent is rigorously justified by the **first-order Taylor expansion**. For a small step $\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$ from the current point $\boldsymbol{\theta}_k$:
